title: Differential expression of tsRNAs and miRNAs in embryo culture medium: potential impact on embryo implantation
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: This study reveals that embryo-derived small RNAs in culture medium, particularly tRNA fragments (tsRNAs) and microRNAs (miRNAs), serve as critical mediators of embryo-endometrial communication and implantation success. High-quality embryos release nine upregulated tsRNAs and four miRNAs that promote endometrial receptivity and embryo development, while low-quality embryos produce 37 tsRNAs and 12 miRNAs with opposing effects. Notably, tiRNA-1:35-Leu-TAG-2, highly expressed in culture medium from low-quality embryos, directly inhibits endometrial receptivity by reducing the attachment capacity of trophoblastic cells when introduced to endometrial cell lines. These findings demonstrate a previously unrecognized mechanism whereby embryo quality is communicated to the endometrium through distinct small RNA signatures that either facilitate or impede successful implantation. The differential expression patterns of tsRNAs and miRNAs between high- and low-quality embryos suggest these molecules could serve as biomarkers for embryo viability and predictors of implantation potential. This research provides mechanistic insights into the molecular foundations of embryo-endometrial crosstalk relevant to assisted reproductive outcomes.

title: miR-338-5p regulated the NF-κB/MAPK pathway to alleviate inflammation and oxidative stress by targeting IL-6 in rats with atrial fibrillation
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that miR-338-5p exerts cardioprotective effects in atrial fibrillation by directly targeting IL-6 and suppressing the NF-κB/MAPK inflammatory pathway. MiR-338-5p overexpression reduced phosphorylation of key pathway components (p-p38, p-p65, and p-ERK1/2), thereby attenuating both inflammation and oxidative stress. Elevated miR-338-5p levels ameliorated myocardial injury by reducing cardiac biomarkers including creatine kinase (CK), CK-MB, and cardiac troponin T (cTnT). These findings establish miR-338-5p as a negative regulator of the IL-6/NF-κB/MAPK axis, suggesting potential therapeutic value for miRNA-based interventions in atrial fibrillation and associated cardiac damage.

title: Dysregulated miR-124-3p in endometrial epithelial cells reduces endometrial receptivity by altering polarity and adhesion
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
summary:
# Summary

Dysregulated microRNA-124-3p (miR-124-3p) impairs endometrial receptivity and embryo implantation by disrupting epithelial cell adhesion and polarity. The study demonstrates that miR-124-3p is abnormally elevated in the uterine epithelium of infertile women during the receptive phase and directly targets IQGAP1, a protein essential for maintaining endometrial epithelial cell adhesion and preventing loss of cell polarity. Overexpression of miR-124-3p in mice and human endometrial epithelial cells significantly reduced implantation sites, prevented proper uterine lumen closure, and impaired embryo trophectoderm attachment, while miR-124-3p inhibition restored adhesive capacity in infertile cells. The dysregulation also downregulates multiple receptivity-associated genes including adhesion molecules (E-cadherin, Muc1), stromal decidualization factors (Bmp2, Hand2, Cox2), and critical reproductive genes (Hoxa11, Egf, Vegfa, cytokines IL-15 and IL-11). These findings establish miR-124-3p dysregulation as a novel mechanism of unexplained infertility, suggesting that therapeutic targeting of miR-124-3p or restoration of IQGAP1 expression represents a potential therapeutic strategy for improving endometrial receptivity and reproductive outcomes.

title: Biomarkers and diagnostic significance of non-coding RNAs in extracellular vesicles of pathologic pregnancy
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: Extracellular vesicles function as critical mediators of intercellular communication by transferring diverse non-coding RNA molecules, including miRNAs, lncRNAs, and circRNAs, between cells. These non-coding RNAs contained within extracellular vesicles are involved in regulating both physiological and pathological processes relevant to female reproductive health. Alterations in the expression patterns of these non-coding RNAs in maternal extracellular vesicles have been implicated in several pregnancy-related pathologies, including preeclampsia and recurrent spontaneous abortion. The genomic and proteomic information carried by extracellular vesicles can serve as biomarkers for diagnosing and monitoring female reproductive system diseases and placental dysfunction. This review consolidates evidence demonstrating that extracellular vesicle-associated non-coding RNAs represent important diagnostic and therapeutic targets for understanding the molecular mechanisms underlying pathologic pregnancy complications.

title: Jumonji and AT-Rich Interacting Domain 2 (JARID2) exhibits a tumor-suppressive role in Oral Squamous Cell Carcinoma by modulating tumor progression and metastasis
summary: JARID2 functions as a tumor suppressor in oral squamous cell carcinoma, with its downregulation during disease progression enabling enhanced metastatic capacity. Loss of JARID2 expression promotes epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition through increased expression of mesenchymal markers (N-cadherin and vimentin) coupled with decreased E-cadherin expression, hallmarks of enhanced cellular plasticity. JARID2 knockdown significantly accelerates malignant phenotypes including increased migration, invasion, and colony-formation ability while simultaneously suppressing apoptosis in cancer cells. The protein localizes to the cytoplasm in OSCC tissues and cell lines, suggesting a functional role in regulating metastatic signaling pathways. These findings collectively establish JARID2 as a critical epigenetic regulator whose loss directly drives OSCC progression and metastatic dissemination through modulation of epithelial-mesenchymal plasticity and cell survival mechanisms.

title: Research progress of circular RNA FOXO3 in diseases (review)
summary:
# CircFOXO3 as a Pleiotropic Regulator in Disease Pathogenesis

Circular RNA FOXO3 (circFOXO3), derived from exon 2 of the FOXO3 gene and localized to the cytoplasm, functions as a critical regulator of gene expression and protein translation through multiple molecular mechanisms. CircFOXO3 exerts complex, context-dependent roles across diverse diseases by acting as a miRNA sponge, protein scaffold, and regulator of key signaling pathways including PI3K/AKT, mTORC1, and NF-κB. In cancer contexts, circFOXO3 demonstrates opposing functions—suppressing progression in prostate cancer through cell cycle arrest and epithelial-mesenchymal transition inhibition while promoting inflammation-related pathologies in conditions such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Additionally, circFOXO3 is elevated in cardiovascular conditions including aged hearts and ischemia-reperfusion injury, where it promotes senescence and cellular damage through interactions with antiaging proteins like ID1 and E2F1. These findings establish circFOXO3 as a disease-associated regulatory RNA with potential therapeutic significance, though its divergent roles across tissue types and pathological contexts suggest disease-specific targeting strategies may be necessary for clinical application.

title: Mechanism of microRNA-152-3p-Mediated Regulation of Autophagy and Sensitivity in Paclitaxel-Resistant Ovarian Cancer Cells
summary:
# Summary

This study elucidates a critical mechanism of paclitaxel resistance in ovarian cancer through microRNA-152-3p (miR-152-3p) regulation of autophagy. miR-152-3p is significantly elevated in paclitaxel-resistant A2780T cells and ovarian cancer tissues, where it promotes autophagy and drug resistance by suppressing PTEN expression and downregulating drug-resistance markers including P-glycoprotein and GSTP1. Inhibition of miR-152-3p reverses paclitaxel-induced autophagy, restores drug sensitivity by approximately 50%, and enhances apoptosis through modulation of Bax and Bcl-2 expression. The findings demonstrate that miR-152-3p functions as a key regulator of the autophagy-mediated resistance pathway, with PTEN emerging as a direct functional target whose downregulation correlates with increased paclitaxel sensitivity. These results identify miR-152-3p as a potential therapeutic target to overcome chemoresistance in ovarian cancer patients and highlight the central role of autophagy-regulating microRNAs in therapeutic resistance.

title: Research progress of circRNAs in bone-related diseases
summary:
# Circular RNAs in Bone-Related Diseases: Mechanisms and Clinical Significance

Circular RNAs (circRNAs) play irreplaceable roles in the pathogenesis and progression of multiple bone-related diseases, including osteosarcoma, osteoporosis, osteoarthritis, osteonecrosis, and intervertebral disc degeneration. CircRNAs regulate gene expression through several mechanisms: direct modulation of transcription and RNA splicing, competitive sequestration of miRNAs (ceRNA mechanism), and interaction with RNA-binding proteins. In osteosarcoma specifically, dysregulated circRNAs drive tumorigenesis through distinct pathways—while oncogenic circRNAs such as circFIRRE, circMYO10, and circECE1 promote cell proliferation, invasion, and metabolic reprogramming via miRNA sponging and signaling pathway activation, tumor suppressor circRNAs like circITCH and hsa_circ_0000190 inhibit these processes. Key circRNAs regulate important pathways including PI3K/AKT and Wnt/β-catenin signaling, positioning them as potential therapeutic targets and diagnostic biomarkers for bone malignancies and degenerative diseases.

title: Therapeutic potential of exosomal lncRNAs derived from stem cells in wound healing: focusing on mesenchymal stem cells
summary: Mesenchymal stem cell-derived exosomes (MSC-Exos) represent a promising therapeutic approach for wound healing, leveraging their ability to deliver bioactive long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) and growth factors to promote tissue repair. These exosomal lncRNAs regulate multiple critical wound healing phases by suppressing inflammatory cytokines and reactive oxygen species while simultaneously enhancing angiogenesis, fibroblast proliferation, and epithelial cell migration. Specific lncRNAs such as KLF3-AS1, MEG3, Neat1, and HAND2-AS1 function through distinct mechanisms—including sponging microRNAs and upregulating pro-angiogenic factors like VEGF—to accelerate wound closure and extracellular matrix remodeling. MSC-Exos also facilitate macrophage polarization toward the M2 phenotype, which reduces inflammation and promotes collagen deposition, while containing multiple bioactive cytokines including IL-10, TGF-β1, and hepatocyte growth factor. These findings suggest that exosomal lncRNAs derived from stem cells could address wound healing complications across diverse clinical contexts, including diabetic wounds and keloid formation, by orchestrating coordinated regenerative responses.

title: miR-17-5p-Mediated RNA Activation Upregulates KPNA2 Expression and Inhibits High-Glucose-Induced Apoptosis of Sheep Granulosa Cells
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that miR-17-5p acts as a critical protective factor for sheep granulosa cells under high-glucose stress by targeting KPNA2 through a novel RNA activation mechanism. High glucose concentrations downregulate miR-17-5p expression and suppress KPNA2, leading to granulosa cell apoptosis and impaired steroidogenesis. Overexpression of miR-17-5p promotes granulosa cell proliferation and inhibits apoptosis by downregulating pro-apoptotic factors (Bax, Caspase-3, Caspase-9) while upregulating the anti-apoptotic protein Bcl-2 and steroidogenic enzymes (CYP11A1, CYP19A1). The miR-17-5p/KPNA2 pathway synergistically enhances these protective effects, with KPNA2 knockdown reversing miR-17-5p-mediated apoptosis inhibition and proliferation promotion. These findings establish the miR-17-5p/KPNA2 axis as a key regulatory mechanism in granulosa cell survival under metabolic stress, with potential implications for understanding reproductive disorders such as endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome that are associated with glucose dysregulation.

title: Vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) polymorphism and increased risk of epithelial ovarian cancer
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary:
# Summary

Angiogenesis, the formation of new blood vessels, is essential for epithelial ovarian cancer development and is primarily regulated by opposing actions of VEGF, which promotes angiogenesis, and endostatin, which inhibits it. This study identified genetic polymorphisms in the genes encoding these angiogenic regulators as risk factors for epithelial ovarian cancer, with the VEGF C936T polymorphism and COL18A1 D104N polymorphism both showing significant associations with cancer susceptibility. Specifically, the VEGF 936CC genotype was more frequent in ovarian cancer patients than controls and conferred a nearly twofold increased risk of disease, while individuals carrying both the VEGF 936CC and COL18A1 DN genotypes showed substantially elevated cancer risk compared to controls. These findings provide preliminary evidence that genetic variation in angiogenic pathway genes, particularly when combined, may predispose individuals to epithelial ovarian cancer development through altered regulation of tumor-promoting blood vessel formation.

title: Decreased expression of miR-200a and miR-223-3p in endometriosis during the secretory phase of menstrual cycle: Insights from a case-control study on molecular biomarkers and disease-related infertility
summary: This case-control study identified significantly reduced expression of miR-200a and miR-223-3p in endometrial tissue from women with endometriosis during the secretory phase of the menstrual cycle compared to controls. Both microRNAs are involved in critical processes for successful embryo implantation and endometrial receptivity, suggesting their decreased expression contributes to endometriosis-related infertility. MiR-200a regulates epithelial-mesenchymal transition through its interaction with Zeb1 and Zeb2 genes, while miR-223-3p suppresses cell migration and proliferation by inhibiting leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF) gene expression, a key mediator of uterine receptivity. The hormonal and inflammatory dysregulation characteristic of endometriosis—including progesterone resistance, estrogen dominance, and chronic inflammation—appears to drive downregulation of these protective microRNAs. These findings establish miR-200a and miR-223-3p as molecular biomarkers linking endometriosis pathogenesis to impaired implantation competence and suggest that restoring their expression may represent a therapeutic strategy for managing endometriosis-associated infertility.

title: Exosomes: a double‐edged sword in cancer immunotherapy
summary: Exosomes function as double-edged swords in cancer immunotherapy, capable of both suppressing and activating immune responses depending on their molecular cargo. Tumor-derived exosomes carry immunosuppressive molecules such as PD-L1 and transforming growth factor beta that mediate immune escape, while simultaneously inhibiting effector T cell and natural killer cell function to promote tumor progression and metastasis. These exosomes remodel the tumor microenvironment by altering stromal cell phenotypes, disrupting endothelial barrier integrity, inducing angiogenesis, and conditioning distant premetastatic sites through their bioactive nucleic acids, proteins, lipids, and metabolites. However, exosomes exposed to physical stimuli like radiotherapy, UV light, or pH changes can exhibit immunostimulatory properties, suggesting potential therapeutic applications. The directional delivery of exosomal cargo to recipient cells through surface protein-membrane interactions enables precise intercellular communication that fundamentally influences tumor growth, invasion, and metastatic spread. Understanding this dual functionality of exosomes is critical for developing effective cancer immunotherapies that can harness their immune-activating potential while mitigating their immunosuppressive effects.

title: Exosomal ncRNAs in reproductive cancers†
summary: Exosomes serve as critical mediators of intercellular communication by transporting non-coding RNAs (ncRNAs), including microRNAs, which regulate gene expression and cell proliferation in reproductive cancers. Tumor-derived exosomes promote cancer progression through multiple mechanisms: they facilitate immunosuppression, drive uncontrolled cell growth, and promote angiogenesis by delivering pro-angiogenic factors such as VEGF and TNF-α. These exosomes also orchestrate epithelial-mesenchymal transition and extracellular matrix remodeling, processes essential for metastasis, while exosomal ncRNAs help cancer cells evade immune surveillance. Gender-specific microRNAs associated with exosomes have been identified as biomarkers—including miR-125a-5p, miR-21, and miR-375 in male reproductive cancers, and miR-26a-5p, miR-148b, miR-205, and miRNA-423-3p in female reproductive cancers. Beyond cancer, extracellular vesicles are implicated in endometriosis and uterine inflammation, suggesting that exosomal ncRNA pathways represent a shared mechanism across multiple reproductive diseases and a potential avenue for therapeutic intervention.

title: Engineered Extracellular
summary:
# Summary

Extracellular vesicles, particularly exosomes secreted by biological cells, function as mediators of intercellular communication by transporting functional nucleic acids between cells through pathways involving Rab GTPases, ESCRT machinery, and SNAREs. Exosomes bearing surface markers such as CD63, CD9, and CD81 can regulate immune responses by suppressing pro-inflammatory macrophage and Th17 cell phenotypes while promoting T-regulatory cell polarization, effects achieved through mesenchymal stem cell-derived extracellular vesicles that reduce pro-inflammatory cytokine expression and enhance anti-inflammatory signaling. These therapeutic exosomes demonstrate broad clinical potential by reducing cell apoptosis, tissue inflammation, and neuro-inflammation while promoting angiogenesis, tissue regeneration, and functional recovery in acute lung injury, ischemic events, and COVID-19-associated respiratory distress syndrome. Engineering approaches, including hypoxia pretreatment to enrich exosomes with miR-103, miR-210, and miR-17, lentivector transduction to generate miR-140-5p or GATA-4-enriched exosomes, heat shock treatment to upregulate HSP70, and surface optimization with dual receptors (TNFR1 and IL-6ST), significantly enhance targeting ability and therapeutic efficacy for conditions ranging from cartilage regeneration and myocardial infarction to osteoarthritis prevention.

title: Immunomodulatory Significance of Mast Cell Exosomes (MC-EXOs) in Immune Response Coordination
summary:
# Summary

Mast cells produce exosomes (MC-exosomes) that contain characteristic markers including tetraspanins, heat shock proteins (Hsp70, Hsp90), and mast cell-specific proteins (FcεRI, KIT), along with functional mRNAs and microRNAs. Mast cell activation through multiple pathways including IgE:FcεRI, C5a:C5aR, and MRGPRX2 engagement triggers degranulation and MC-exosome release, with IL-4 required for exosome production. These immunologically active MC-exosomes modulate adaptive immunity by stimulating T and B cell proliferation, blast formation, and cytokine production including IL-2 and IFN-γ. Functionally, specific miRNAs within MC-exosomes regulate immune and epithelial responses: miR-409-3p promotes NF-κB pathway activation and inflammatory cytokine production by suppressing Nr4a2, while miR-223 disrupts the epithelial barrier by inhibiting claudin 8 expression in intestinal epithelial cells. These findings establish MC-exosomes as key intercellular communicators coordinating immune responses and potentially contributing to pathological conditions including allergic disease, infertility, and barrier dysfunction.

title: Propofol reduces breast cancer cell stemness via FOXO3/SOX2 axis
summary:
# Summary

Propofol, an intravenous anesthetic with anti-tumor properties, suppresses breast cancer cell stemness through a FOXO3/SOX2 regulatory axis. The anesthetic upregulates FOXO3 expression, which then acts as a transcriptional repressor by binding directly to the SOX2 promoter region and inhibiting its expression. This pathway results in reduced expression of pluripotency factors (SOX2, OCT4, Nanog) and decreased populations of cancer stem cell markers (ESA+/CD44+/CD24−/low cells), ultimately diminishing mammosphere formation capacity. Propofol's anti-stemness effects appear dependent on FOXO3, as blocking FOXO3 overexpression eliminates the drug's inhibitory effects on stemness markers. These findings establish the FOXO3/SOX2 axis as a key mechanism underlying propofol's tumor-suppressive effects and suggest potential applications for enhancing chemotherapeutic responses in breast cancer treatment.

title: Hsa-miR-532-3p protects human decidual mesenchymal stem cells from oxidative stress in recurrent spontaneous abortion via targeting KEAP1
summary:
# Summary

Human decidual mesenchymal stem cells (hDMSCs) are essential for pregnancy maintenance and placental development, but their impaired resistance to oxidative stress contributes to recurrent spontaneous abortion (RSA). This study demonstrates that hsa-miR-532-3p protects hDMSCs from oxidative damage by targeting KEAP1 to activate the KEAP1/NRF2 antioxidant pathway. Expression of hsa-miR-532-3p is significantly reduced in hDMSCs from RSA patients, and overexpression of this miRNA increases antioxidant capacity while reducing embryo absorption rates in RSA mouse models. The study further reveals that serum hsa-miR-532-3p levels correlate inversely with gestational age and serve as a potential biomarker for RSA prediction. These findings establish hsa-miR-532-3p as a critical regulator of decidual oxidative stress resistance and suggest targeting this miRNA-mediated pathway may represent a therapeutic approach to prevent recurrent miscarriage.

title: Overexpression of FOS enhances the malignant potential of eutopic endometrial stromal cells in patients with endometriosis‑associated ovarian cancer
summary: This study identifies FOS (Fos proto-oncogene) as a key driver of malignant transformation in endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer (EAOC), with overexpression in eutopic endometrial stromal cells promoting cell viability, proliferation, and migration. FOS operates through multiple signaling cascades including MAPK, AP-1, and ERK pathways to enhance the conversion of endometrial cysts to cancer, while its inhibition upregulates the cell cycle inhibitor P21 and downregulates proliferation-associated proteins CDK4, Cyclin D1, and matrix metalloproteinases MMP2 and MMP9. The malignant transformation process involves oxidative stress, inflammation, estrogen stimulation, DNA methylation aberrance, and oncogene activation in the eutopic endometrium, with FOS showing functional association with genes including JUN, MKI67, and TOP2A in protein interaction networks. Treatment with the FOS inhibitor T-5224 effectively suppresses endometrial stromal cell malignant characteristics in a dose-dependent manner, reducing viability, proliferation, and migration while suppressing p-Stat3 signaling, suggesting FOS inhibition as a potential therapeutic strategy for EAOC prevention and treatment. These findings establish FOS as a novel biomarker and therapeutic target in the progression from benign endometriosis to ovarian malignancy.

title: Unlocking Gamete Quality Through Extracellular Vesicles: Emerging Perspectives
summary: Extracellular vesicles (EVs), particularly exosomes and epididymosomes, emerge as critical mediators of reproductive success by orchestrating gamete quality across both male and female reproductive environments. In males, seminal plasma-derived EVs and epididymosomes enhance sperm functionality through multiple mechanisms: they stabilize sperm membranes, promote motility and capacitation, and transport bioactive molecules including microRNAs (miR-191, miR-375, miR-467a/d/e), proteins, and lipids essential for sperm maturation. Prostasomes, prostate-derived EVs, additionally protect spermatozoa from immune attack by modulating the complement pathway and inhibiting phagocytosis. In females, EVs present in follicular, oviductal, and uterine fluids regulate oocyte maturation, follicular development, and early embryonic development through their cargo of microRNAs and bioactive molecules. The therapeutic potential of EVs is being explored for drug delivery applications, positioning these nanosized particles as promising interventions for enhancing reproductive outcomes in both clinical and agricultural contexts.

title: Roles of lncRNA in the crosstalk between osteogenesis and angiogenesis in the bone microenvironment
journal: Journal of Zhejiang University. Science. B
summary: Long noncoding RNAs (lncRNAs) serve as critical mediators of crosstalk between osteogenesis and angiogenesis in the bone microenvironment, two processes that are inherently linked for optimal bone development and maintenance. Within bone tissue, multiple cell types including osteoblasts, osteoclasts, and bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells actively regulate angiogenesis, while the bone vasculature reciprocally supports osteogenesis by providing essential nutrients and growth factors necessary for bone growth and repair. LncRNAs participate directly in both osteogenic and angiogenic pathways, thereby enabling coordinated regulation of these interdependent processes in bone homeostasis. Age-related declines in both osteogenesis and bone angiogenesis contribute significantly to osteoporosis development, highlighting the functional importance of maintaining robust vascular-osteogenic signaling. By elucidating lncRNA-mediated mechanisms that coordinate bone cell differentiation with endothelial sprouting and vascular expansion, this work provides molecular insights into how gene regulation maintains skeletal health and suggests potential therapeutic targets for age-related bone diseases.

title: miR-100: A key tumor suppressor regulatory factor in human malignant tumors (Review)
summary:
# Summary

miR-100 functions as a potent tumor suppressor that broadly inhibits cancer cell proliferation, migration, and invasion while promoting apoptosis across multiple malignancies including gastric, lung, ovarian, prostate, thyroid, and bladder cancers. At the molecular level, miR-100 exerts its effects by downregulating key oncogenic targets such as IGF1R, FGFR3, PLK1, NOX4, and LGR5, while simultaneously inhibiting critical signaling pathways including PI3K/AKT and mTOR that drive metabolic processes like glycolysis and autophagy. The tumor-suppressive effects of miR-100 are antagonized by long non-coding RNAs including HAGLROS, which function as competitive inhibitors (molecular sponges) to sequester miR-100, thereby reactivating downstream pathways that promote tumor progression and chemotherapy resistance. Additionally, miR-100 regulates normal physiological processes such as embryo implantation and germ cell proliferation, and its dysregulation is associated with non-malignant diseases including diabetes, osteoporosis, and osteoarthritis. This comprehensive regulatory network positions miR-100 as a pivotal molecular hub with potential therapeutic and diagnostic applications across diverse cancer types and disease states.

title: The microRNA-mediated apoptotic signaling axis in male reproduction: a possible and targetable culprit in male infertility
summary:
# Summary

MicroRNAs regulate apoptotic signaling in male reproductive tissues through multiple pathways including PI3K/AKT, NOTCH, Wnt/β-catenin, and mTOR, with dysregulation of miRNAs driving excessive apoptosis in spermatogonial stem cells, Sertoli cells, and Leydig cells that ultimately causes male infertility. Environmental stressors including heat stress, hypoxia, diabetes, silica nanoparticles, Cyclosporine A, and smoking induce miRNA dysregulation, triggering both extrinsic (death receptor-mediated caspase-8 activation) and intrinsic (mitochondrial permeabilization-mediated cytochrome C release) apoptotic pathways. Specific miRNAs play distinct roles in reproductive dysfunction: miR-210-3p promotes spermatogenic cell apoptosis via caspase-3 activation, miR-322 downregulation increases apoptotic gene expression (Bax, caspases) while reducing anti-apoptotic Bcl-2, and miR-130a induces apoptosis by suppressing androgen receptor expression in Sertoli cells. Conversely, elevated miR-100-3p protects Sertoli cells from apoptosis by targeting SGK3, suggesting that modulation of specific miRNAs could prevent reproductive cell death and serve as a therapeutic strategy for male infertility.

title: miR-21 regulates autophagy and apoptosis of ectopic endometrial stromal cells of adenomyosis via PI3K/ AKT/ mTOR pathway
summary: This study demonstrates that microRNA-21 (miR-21) promotes pathological cellular behaviors in adenomyosis by suppressing apoptosis and autophagy through activation of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway in ectopic endometrial stromal cells. The research shows that estrogen upregulates miR-21 expression, which subsequently inhibits autophagy and apoptosis while promoting cell proliferation and migration. Conversely, pathway inhibition using LY294002 suppresses the PI3K/AKT/mTOR cascade, leading to increased apoptosis and autophagy while reducing proliferation. These findings indicate that miR-21 acts as a key regulator maintaining the pathological survival and motility of adenomyotic stromal cells through a well-defined molecular axis, suggesting that targeting miR-21 or its downstream PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway could represent a potential therapeutic strategy for adenomyosis treatment.

title: Mechanism of human chorionic gonadotropin in endometrial receptivity via the miR‐126‐3p/PI3K/Akt/eNOS axis
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG), produced by trophoblast cells, improves endometrial receptivity in endometriosis-induced dysfunction (EID) mice through activation of the miR-126-3p/PI3K/Akt/eNOS signaling axis. hCG upregulates miR-126-3p, which directly targets and suppresses PIK3R2, a negative regulator of the PI3K pathway, thereby activating downstream PI3K/Akt/eNOS signaling and promoting endothelial cell proliferation and angiogenesis. The enhanced angiogenic response, evidenced by elevated endothelial markers (CD144, CD146, CD105), facilitates endometrial vascular development essential for successful embryo implantation and pregnancy establishment. These findings reveal a mechanistic basis for clinical applications of hCG infusion to enhance endometrial receptivity in patients with endometriosis-related infertility and repeated implantation failure, while also highlighting the critical role of miRNA dysregulation in endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: SP1‐induced lncRNA DANCR contributes to proliferation and invasion of ovarian cancer
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the transcription factor SP1 promotes ovarian cancer progression by transcriptionally activating the long non-coding RNA DANCR through direct binding to its promoter region. SP1 and DANCR are both significantly upregulated in ovarian cancer tissues and cell lines (CAOV3, SKOV3, A2780), and their expression levels positively correlate with each other. Functionally, DANCR promotes ovarian cancer cell viability, migration, and invasion, with elevated DANCR expression associated with larger tumor size, advanced FIGO stage, lymph node metastasis, and shorter patient survival. Mechanistically, DANCR acts as a competing endogenous RNA to sponge miR-145, thereby regulating angiogenesis, and also modulates protein stabilization of UPF1 and p21. Knockdown of DANCR suppresses these malignant phenotypes, while SP1 overexpression can reverse DANCR knockdown effects, confirming that the SP1-DANCR axis is critical for ovarian cancer progression and represents a potential therapeutic target.

title: Long non‐coding RNA‐H19 promotes ovarian cancer cell proliferation and migration via the microRNA‐140/Wnt1 axis
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that long non-coding RNA H19 is significantly upregulated in ovarian cancer tissues and cell lines, where it promotes cell proliferation and migration while correlating with poor prognosis and disease recurrence. H19 functions as a competitive inhibitor of microRNA-140 (miR-140), which is correspondingly downregulated in ovarian cancer, thereby allowing increased expression of the oncogene Wnt1. Knockdown of H19 effectively suppresses ovarian cancer cell proliferation and migration by upregulating miR-140 and downregulating Wnt1 expression, while inhibition of miR-140 reverses these anti-tumor effects. The H19/miR-140/Wnt1 regulatory axis represents a novel mechanism driving ovarian cancer initiation and progression, suggesting that targeting this pathway could provide a therapeutic strategy for ovarian cancer treatment. This ceRNA (competing endogenous RNA) mechanism reflects a broader pattern where multiple long non-coding RNAs, including PTAR, TUG1, and DANCR, similarly regulate ovarian cancer development through microRNA-mediated pathways.

title: MicroRNA‐494‐3p facilitates the progression of bladder cancer by mediating the KLF9/RGS2 axis
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary: This study elucidates a regulatory axis driving bladder cancer progression through miR-494-3p-mediated suppression of tumor suppressors. MicroRNA-494-3p is aberrantly elevated in bladder cancer cells, where it directly binds the 3'-UTR of KLF9 mRNA to suppress KLF9 expression. KLF9, acting as a transcription factor, normally binds the RGS2 promoter to activate RGS2 expression, which functions to inhibit cancer cell growth. The miR-494-3p/KLF9/RGS2 axis operates as a functional cascade wherein elevated miR-494-3p promotes bladder cancer cell proliferation, migration, and invasion by silencing this protective pathway. Notably, miR-494-3p inhibition or antagonism suppresses these malignant phenotypes both in vitro and in vivo through restoration of KLF9 and RGS2 levels, with RGS2 knockdown abrogating the therapeutic benefits, confirming RGS2 as an essential downstream effector. These findings identify miR-494-3p as a potential therapeutic target for bladder cancer management.

title: Lower growth arrest‐specific 5 level in endometrium is related to endometriosis via promoting cell proliferation and angiogenesis
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that reduced growth arrest-specific 5 (GAS5), a long noncoding RNA, in endometrial tissues from endometriosis patients contributes significantly to disease pathogenesis through multiple mechanisms. Lower GAS5 levels in both eutopic and ectopic endometrium promote cell proliferation and reduce apoptosis by downregulating p27, a cell cycle inhibitor, leading to increased endometrial stromal cell viability and dysregulated cell cycle progression. Additionally, GAS5 suppresses angiogenesis—the formation of new blood vessels critical for endometriotic lesion growth—by acting as a molecular sponge that represses miR-181c, which otherwise inhibits the antiangiogenic protein TIMP3. The study demonstrates that GAS5 overexpression restores p27 levels, increases cell cycle arrest in the G0/G1 phase, reduces miR-181c expression, elevates TIMP3, and impairs capillary formation, while GAS5 knockdown produces opposite effects. These findings identify the GAS5/miR-181c/TIMP3 regulatory axis as a critical node in endometriosis pathogenesis, suggesting that restoring GAS5 expression could represent a therapeutic strategy to suppress both abnormal cell proliferation and pathological angiogenesis in endometriotic lesions.

title: Defining optimal electrospun membranes to enhance biological activities of human endometrial MSCs
summary:
# Summary

Human endometrial mesenchymal stem cells (H-EMSCs) possess intrinsic capacity to inhibit endometrial fibrosis, repair damaged endometrium, and promote tissue regeneration, making them promising candidates for treating fibrotic endometrial conditions. The study demonstrates that PCL-HA electrospun composite membranes significantly enhance H-EMSCs survival, adhesion, and proliferation compared to PCL or PCL-SF alternatives, with superior biocompatibility achieved through hyaluronic acid's hydrophilicity counteracting PCL's inherent hydrophobicity. When cultured on PCL-HA membranes, H-EMSCs exhibit an anti-inflammatory and pro-regenerative phenotype, characterized by decreased IL-6 expression and increased IL-10, VEGFA, and TGF-β expression, while maintaining high levels of mesenchymal markers CD90 and Meflin. The PCL-HA composite maintains the FDA-approved biocompatibility and mechanical properties of PCL while achieving faster degradation kinetics and effectively reducing endometrial fibrosis and thickness abnormalities. These findings support PCL-HA as an optimized biomaterial scaffold for delivering H-EMSCs therapeutically to restore endometrial structure and function in fibrotic conditions.

title: Cell Migration in Endometriosis Responds to Omentum-Derived Molecular Cues Similar to Ovarian Cancer
summary: Endometriosis shares striking molecular and cellular similarities with ovarian cancer, particularly in response to omental-derived secreted factors that promote cell migration. Endometriotic tissue harbors the same oncogenic mutations found in ovarian cancer (ARID1A, PIK3CA, KRAS, and PPP2R1A), and endometriosis increases ovarian cancer risk 2- to 3-fold. Omental adipocytes and stromal cells secrete multiple migration-promoting factors including HGF, SDF-1α, MCP-1, VEGF-A, IL-6, and IL-8, which stimulate migration in both endometriotic and ovarian cancer cells via HGF/c-MET signaling that can be blocked by c-MET inhibitors. Notably, PTTG1 is consistently upregulated in migrated cells from both endometriosis and all ovarian cancer histological subtypes, suggesting a conserved molecular driver of intra-abdominal cell dissemination. Omental secretions from ovarian cancer patients induce more robust migration in endometriotic cells than secretions from endometriosis patients, while omental adipocyte-derived HGF is elevated in peritoneal fluid from endometriosis patients, indicating bidirectional molecular crosstalk. These findings demonstrate that the omentum provides a permissive microenvironment for cell migration in both conditions through similar cytokine-driven mechanisms.

title: LncRNA HOTAIR promotes aerobic glycolysis by recruiting Lin28 to induce inflammation and apoptosis in acute lung injury
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the long non-coding RNA HOTAIR promotes acute lung injury through a metabolic-inflammatory axis centered on aerobic glycolysis. HOTAIR is upregulated in lipopolysaccharide-induced acute lung injury and functions by recruiting the Lin28 protein in the cytoplasm to enhance glucose transporter 1 (GLUT1) expression, thereby increasing glucose uptake and aerobic glycolysis in alveolar epithelial cells. This metabolic reprogramming elevates glycolysis-derived reactive oxygen species, which activates the NLRP3 inflammasome and drives secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) through NF-κB pathway activation. Concurrently, HOTAIR promotes cell apoptosis by upregulating pro-apoptotic proteins (cleaved caspase 3 and Bax) while suppressing the anti-apoptotic protein Bcl-2. Experimental knockdown of HOTAIR significantly reduced glucose metabolism, inflammatory factor production, and apoptosis, establishing HOTAIR inhibition as a potential therapeutic strategy for acute lung injury.

title: Integrated analysis of differential expression profile of miRNA in the uterus of seasonal estrus sheep
summary:
# Summary

This integrated analysis identified 31 differentially expressed microRNAs (DEM) in sheep uteri under short photoperiod conditions compared to long photoperiod, with photoperiod-regulated seasonal estrus being a key reproductive process. Three novel miRNAs—nove_320, nove_338, and nove_339—emerged as major regulators, each targeting multiple mRNAs including TRIM71, PRDM1, and PTPN13 respectively, which are involved in cell proliferation, apoptosis, and uterine receptivity. Functional analysis revealed that DEM target genes are significantly enriched in apoptosis and platelet activation pathways, suggesting these processes are critical for maintaining uterine homeostasis during seasonal breeding cycles. The uterine miRNA expression profile is dynamically regulated by both photoperiod signals and hormonal factors, including estradiol and progesterone, highlighting the role of uterine miRNAs as key mediators of seasonal reproductive transitions in sheep. These findings indicate that photoperiod-regulated miRNA expression in the uterus represents a fundamental molecular mechanism controlling seasonal estrous behavior through regulation of cell proliferation, apoptosis, and receptivity pathways.

title: Extracellular vesicles in endometrial-related diseases: role, potential and challenges
summary:
# Summary

Extracellular vesicles, particularly exosomes derived from endometrial cells, mediate multiple pathogenic processes in endometriosis through intercellular communication via microRNAs and long non-coding RNAs. These vesicles promote disease progression by enhancing angiogenesis, fibrosis, and cell proliferation while simultaneously suppressing immune function through mechanisms including HOTAIR-mediated endothelial cell survival, aHIF-induced VEGF activation, and miR-301a-3p-driven M2 macrophage polarization. Specific exosomal miRNAs such as miR-210, miR-22-3p, and miR-199a-5p activate pro-angiogenic pathways including STAT3 and NF-κB signaling to further promote ectopic endometrial growth. Conversely, miR-214-3p exhibits therapeutic potential by inhibiting endometrial fibrosis through CCN2 regulation. These findings establish exosomal miRNAs as critical regulators in endometriosis pathogenesis and identify multiple molecular targets for developing diagnostic biomarkers and therapeutic interventions.

title: Research on human burden/toxicants
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary: This study identifies a critical regulatory pathway in endometriosis pathogenesis centered on the miR-199a-5p–SOD2 axis in endometrial stromal cells. The microRNA miR-199a-5p is significantly elevated in cells from endometriosis patients and directly suppresses SOD2, a mitochondrial antioxidant enzyme that normally scavenges reactive oxygen species (ROS). SOD2 downregulation leads to ROS accumulation, which stabilizes and activates HIF-1α, a key transcription factor that upregulates pro-angiogenic factors VEGF and PDGF, ultimately promoting aberrant angiogenesis and cell proliferation while suppressing apoptosis. Inhibition of miR-199a-5p restores SOD2 expression, reduces ROS and HIF-1α signaling, and reverses pathological features including enhanced cell proliferation, decreased apoptosis, and increased angiogenesis. These findings establish the miR-199a-5p–SOD2–ROS–HIF-1α pathway as a mechanistic driver of endometriosis, which affects 10% of reproductive-age women and is strongly associated with infertility and chronic pelvic pain. The study suggests that therapeutic targeting of miR-199a-5p may represent a novel strategy to suppress endometriosis progression by restoring oxidative stress homeostasis.

title: Exosomal miR-92a-3p serves as a promising marker and potential therapeutic target for adenomyosis
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies exosomal miR-92a-3p as a key pathogenic factor in adenomyosis derived from ectopic endometrial lesions. The research demonstrates that miR-92a-3p is significantly elevated in exosomes from adenomyotic tissue and circulating in plasma and urine of affected patients, where it correlates with clinical symptoms including severe dysmenorrhea, heavy menstrual bleeding, and enlarged uterine volume. Mechanistically, exosomal miR-92a-3p promotes the migration and invasion of eutopic endometrial cells, dorsal root ganglion neurons, and endothelial cells while simultaneously enhancing angiogenesis through increased tube formation. Therapeutic knockdown of miR-92a-3p effectively inhibited these pathological processes, and post-hysterectomy analysis confirmed that urinary miR-92a-3p levels decreased following lesion removal. The findings establish exosomal miR-92a-3p as a promising non-invasive biomarker for adenomyosis diagnosis via urinary testing and suggest miR-92a-3p antagonism as a potential therapeutic strategy to suppress infiltration and neovascularization in this chronic disease.

title: PTEN-mediated resistance in cancer: From foundation to future therapies
summary:
# Summary

PTEN functions as a critical tumor suppressor that negatively regulates the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway through its lipid phosphatase activity, which dephosphorylates PIP3 and thereby inhibits cell proliferation, growth, and survival while promoting apoptosis. Loss of PTEN function through various mechanisms—including mutations, homozygous and heterozygous deletions, promoter hypermethylation, and post-translational modifications—leads to constitutive PI3K/Akt pathway activation, resulting in uncontrolled cell survival, growth, angiogenesis, and oncogenesis. Multiple layers of epigenetic and post-transcriptional regulation modulate PTEN expression, with numerous microRNAs (miR-21, miR-221/222, miR-301a, miR-214, miR-494) directly downregulating PTEN, while others (miR-29, miR-101, miR-451) upregulate it, and competing endogenous RNAs such as long non-coding RNAs (PTENP1, HOTAIR, CASC2, MEG3, GAS5, XIST) sequester inhibitory microRNAs to enhance PTEN expression. Post-translational modifications including ubiquitination by E3 ligases (NEDD4-1, WWP2), aberrant phosphorylation, and context-dependent acetylation regulate PTEN protein stability and activity, collectively determining whether cancer cells maintain sensitive growth control or progress toward therapeutic resistance. Understanding these multi-level regulatory mechanisms provides a foundation for developing therapies that restore PTEN function or circumvent PTEN-mediated resistance in cancer treatment.

title: Combined targeting of TCF7L1/2, PTEN, CDK6, and BCCIP by microRNA miR‐29c‐3p is associated with reduced invasion and proliferation of endometriotic cells
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic estrogen-dependent disease characterized by ectopic endometrial growth, is associated with dysregulated microRNA expression, particularly miR-29c-3p. This study demonstrates that miR-29c-3p suppresses endometriotic cell proliferation and invasion by simultaneously targeting multiple critical regulators: the cell cycle proteins CDK6 and PTEN, the transcriptional factors TCF7L1 and TCF7L2, and the DNA repair protein BCCIP. Upregulation of miR-29c-3p in endometriotic cell lines reduced cell viability, inhibited S-phase progression, and decreased cell invasion without affecting migration, while increasing expression of the cell cycle inhibitor p21. Additionally, miR-29c-3p downregulates genes involved in epithelial-mesenchymal transition (E-cadherin and N-cadherin), matrix remodeling (COL4A1/A2), angiogenesis (VEGF-A), and progesterone resistance (FKBP4), suggesting a broad anti-endometriotic mechanism. These findings indicate that restoring miR-29c-3p expression through exosomal delivery or other therapeutic approaches could represent a novel multi-target strategy for treating endometriosis and its associated symptoms of pain and infertility.

title: LncRNA MALAT1’s role in the development of retinopathy: A review
summary:
# Summary

Long noncoding RNA MALAT1 plays a central role in diabetic retinopathy (DR) pathogenesis through multiple mechanisms involving gene expression regulation, competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) networks, and inflammatory signaling. Hyperglycemia induces MALAT1 upregulation in retinal cells, which subsequently promotes endothelial cell dysfunction, neovascularization, and angiogenesis by upregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, MCP-1) and angiogenic factors (VEGF, VEGF-A). MALAT1 functions as a ceRNA by binding various microRNAs (miR-124, miR-126-5p, miR-125b, miR-203a-3p, miR-320a) to modulate target gene expression and regulate critical pathways including the YAP/MALAT1/miR-200b-3p/VEGF-A axis and the Keap1/Nrf2 antioxidant defense system. MALAT1 knockdown ameliorates DR progression by suppressing retinal endothelial cell proliferation and migration, reducing inflammatory responses, preventing endoplasmic reticulum stress, and protecting mitochondrial homeostasis, suggesting MALAT1 as a promising therapeutic target for DR management.

title: Suppression of endometriosis by miRNA-34a via inhibition of matrix metalloproteinase-2: An alternative pathway to impede invasion
summary: This study demonstrates that miRNA-34a suppresses endometriosis progression by directly binding to the 3′UTR of matrix metalloproteinase-2 (MMP-2) and inhibiting its transcription. MiRNA-34a is significantly downregulated in endometriotic tissues, while MMP-2 is correspondingly elevated, establishing an inverse relationship between these molecules. Through direct targeting of MMP-2, miRNA-34a inhibits epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and cellular invasion by downregulating mesenchymal markers (n-cadherin, vimentin, and slug) while upregulating the epithelial marker e-cadherin. Experimental validation using miRNA-34a mimics reduced MMP-2 activity, invasion, migration, colony and spheroid formation, and cancer stem cell markers in endometriotic cell lines, while miRNA-34a inhibitors reversed these protective effects. The findings establish miRNA-34a as a key regulator of endometriosis pathophysiology, suggesting that restoring miRNA-34a expression represents a novel therapeutic strategy to prevent MMP-2-mediated invasion and disease progression.

title: Epigenetic Alterations in Ovarian Function and Their Impact on Assisted Reproductive Technologies: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review demonstrates that epigenetic alterations—including DNA methylation, histone modifications, and non-coding RNA regulation—are fundamental mechanisms controlling ovarian function and assisted reproductive technology (ART) outcomes. Specific microRNAs such as miR-27a-3p and miR-15a-5p dysregulate granulosa cell function and proliferation, leading to poor ovarian response and compromised oocyte maturation, while aberrant histone methylation patterns (particularly H3K27me3 enrichment) and global DNA hypomethylation are associated with ovarian aging and reduced ovarian reserve. Environmental stressors like bisphenol A exposure and psychological stress trigger abnormal DNA methylation that further impairs ovarian reserve and hormone signaling, whereas improper epigenetic regulation broadly correlates with adverse ART outcomes including embryonic arrest and reduced endometrial receptivity. These findings suggest that targeting epigenetic dysregulation through histone deacetylase inhibitors and miRNA-based therapies represents a promising therapeutic avenue for restoring ovarian function and improving fertility outcomes in women with compromised ovarian reserve, polycystic ovary syndrome, and early ovarian insufficiency.

title: MiR-205-5p and MiR-222-3p as Potential Biomarkers of Endometrial Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies miR-205-5p and miR-222-3p as potential biomarkers for endometrial cancer with opposing expression patterns: miR-205-5p is elevated in endometrial cancer tissue and associated with poor prognosis, migration, and invasion, while miR-222-3p is decreased in both tissue and serum samples. Multiple additional circulating microRNAs show dysregulation in endometrial cancer patients, including elevated hsa-miR-155, miR-15a-5p, miR-106b-5p, miR-107, and miR-21, which correlate with disease progression and lymph node metastasis. The paper contextualizes these findings within endometrial cancer heterogeneity, noting that type I estrogen-driven cancers develop through endometrial hyperplasia, while distinct molecular subtypes are driven by POLE mutations, microsatellite instability (with ARID5B, PTEN, and PIK3CA mutations), or TP53 mutations, with prognosis varying significantly by subtype. MicroRNAs are stabilized in circulation through packaging into exosomes or binding to AGO2 and HDL particles, making them promising non-invasive diagnostic and prognostic tools. Notably, silencing miR-205-5p enhances paclitaxel sensitivity in endometrial cancer cells, suggesting therapeutic potential beyond biomarker applications.

title: In Vitro Gene Expression Profiling of Quantum Molecular Resonance Effects on Human Endometrium Models: A Preliminary Study
summary:
# Summary

This in vitro study investigated the effects of quantum molecular resonance (QMR) on gene expression in Ishikawa endometrial cells, demonstrating that QMR exposure upregulates key receptivity genes including HOXA10, HOXA11, LIF, and integrin subunits ITGB3 and ITGAV in a power- and time-dependent manner. Single 20-minute QMR exposure significantly increased expression of these receptivity-associated genes, which are critical determinants of endometrial receptivity and embryo implantation potential. However, higher QMR power levels (16 power units) paradoxically downregulated these same genes, indicating an optimal therapeutic window at lower exposures (2-4 power units). Beyond individual gene effects, QMR upregulated multiple cellular pathways involved in cell adhesion, cell cycle progression, and stress response (proteasome and TNFR1 pathways), while downregulating the acute myocardial infarction pathway. These findings suggest that appropriately calibrated QMR exposure may enhance endometrial receptivity through coordinated activation of implantation-related genes and tissue remodeling pathways, with potential clinical applications for reproductive medicine, though further validation in vivo is necessary.

title: Diet-Induced Obesity Alters Granulosa Cell Transcriptome and Ovarian Immune Environment in Mice
summary:
# Summary

High-fat diet (HFD) induces diet-induced obesity in mice, which substantially alters the granulosa cell transcriptome and ovarian immune environment, with significant implications for reproductive dysfunction. HFD downregulates critical immediate early genes including Egr1, Fos, Fosb, and Junb—all key regulators of the ovulation cascade—while simultaneously upregulating inflammatory mediators Ptprc and Hpgds that promote an pro-inflammatory ovarian environment. The downregulation of Egr1 and Fos is particularly consequential, as these genes are activated by gonadotropins and are indispensable for normal ovulation; HFD suppresses the gonadotropin-induced upregulation of EGR1, thereby impairing the ovulatory response. Obesity-induced metabolic disturbances (hyperglycemia, hyperinsulinemia, lipotoxicity) and prolonged HFD exposure lead to visceral fat accumulation, impaired ovulation, delayed embryo development, and poor oocyte quality, collectively contributing to reduced fertility and increased risk of reproductive pathologies including polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis. These findings demonstrate that obesity compromises female reproductive capacity through coordinated suppression of ovulation-essential transcriptional programs and establishment of a pro-inflammatory ovarian milieu, linking metabolic dysfunction directly to endocrine and immune dysregulation in reproductive tissues.

title: MiR-103-3p regulates chondrocyte autophagy, apoptosis, and ECM degradation through the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway by targeting CPEB3
journal: Journal of Orthopaedic Surgery and Research
summary: This study demonstrates that miR-103-3p promotes chondrocyte pathology in osteoarthritis through the CPEB3/PI3K/Akt/mTOR axis. IL-1β stimulation upregulates miR-103-3p while downregulating its target CPEB3, leading to activation of PI3K/Akt/mTOR signaling that drives chondrocyte apoptosis and extracellular matrix degradation through increased ADAMTS5 and MMP13 expression while reducing collagen II and aggrecan. Inhibition of miR-103-3p reverses these IL-1β-induced pathological effects by restoring CPEB3 expression and suppressing the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway, simultaneously reducing apoptosis and enhancing protective autophagy marked by increased LC3, Beclin 1, and ATG5 levels. The study confirms direct targeting of CPEB3 by miR-103-3p via luciferase reporter assays and validates the pathway using specific inhibitors (3-MA, 740 Y-P) and CPEB3 knockdown approaches. These findings establish miR-103-3p as a key disease driver in osteoarthritis and suggest that miR-103-3p inhibition may represent a therapeutic strategy to preserve cartilage by promoting chondrocyte survival and matrix preservation.

title: Transcriptomic profile induced by calcitriol in CaSki human cervical cancer cell line
summary: This study demonstrates that calcitriol, the active metabolite of vitamin D, exerts potent anti-cancer effects in cervical cancer cells through comprehensive transcriptomic reprogramming. Calcitriol binds to the vitamin D receptor (VDR) in CaSki cervical cancer cells, forming a VDR-RXR heterodimer that regulates gene expression and suppresses multiple pro-tumorigenic processes including cell proliferation, migration, invasion, and angiogenesis while promoting apoptosis. The compound downregulates oncogenic genes including EPHA2, RARA, and matrix metalloproteinases (MMP7/MMP13), while upregulating tumor-suppressive genes and microRNAs such as miR-6129, miR-382-5p, miR-211, and miR-655 that target cancer-promoting pathways. Conversely, calcitriol represses pro-oncogenic microRNAs including miR-301a, miR-130a, and miR-1252, reducing their cancer-promoting activities. These findings link vitamin D deficiency to increased cervical cancer risk and identify calcitriol's mechanisms for preventing HPV-driven cervical cancer through coordinated regulation of coding genes and non-coding RNAs that collectively inhibit tumor progression.

title: LncRNAs in oncogenic microenvironment: from threat to therapy
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

Long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) emerge as critical regulators of cellular pathology by controlling fundamental processes including autophagy, inflammation, apoptosis, and gene expression through chromatin remodeling and epigenetic mechanisms. Dysregulation of specific lncRNAs such as HOTAIR, H19, and MALAT1 promotes pathological conditions including cancer development, endothelial dysfunction, and metabolic disease by upregulating pro-angiogenic factors, mitochondrial dysfunction, and oxidative damage. In metabolic disease contexts, lncRNAs function as competing endogenous RNAs by sequestering miRNAs; for example, exosome-transmitted LOC100132249 sponges miR-199a-5p to activate Wnt/β-catenin signaling and promote endothelial dysfunction in diabetic retinopathy, while TUG1 and TUNAR regulate insulin resistance and pancreatic β-cell function through miRNA-dependent pathways. MALAT1 additionally promotes endothelial-mesenchymal transition and lipid accumulation while engaging in cardiovascular pathology through interactions with multiple miRNAs. These findings establish lncRNAs as therapeutic targets, with strategies targeting specific lncRNA-miRNA axes showing potential to normalize dysregulated pathways and restore cellular homeostasis.

title: LINC01871 facilitates cervical cancer cell migration and immune escape by targeting miR‐873‐3p/MAP3K2 axis
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary: This study identifies LINC01871, a long noncoding RNA elevated in cervical cancer tissues and cell lines, as a critical regulator of tumor progression through a ceRNA mechanism involving the miR-873-3p/MAP3K2 axis. LINC01871 functions as a competing endogenous RNA that sequesters miR-873-3p, thereby preventing its suppression of MAP3K2, a kinase that activates MAPK signaling (ERK, JNK, p38 phosphorylation). LINC01871 silencing suppresses cervical cancer cell migration and invasion by inhibiting epithelial-mesenchymal transition markers (reducing Vimentin and N-cadherin while increasing E-cadherin) and simultaneously promotes immune attack by reducing the immune checkpoint molecules PD-L1 and CD47, which enhance macrophage-mediated phagocytosis. In vivo studies confirmed that LINC01871 knockdown reduces tumor growth and MAP3K2 expression while upregulating miR-873-3p in tumor tissue. These findings establish LINC01871 as a dual-function oncogenic lncRNA that facilitates both metastatic capacity and immune evasion in cervical cancer, suggesting it may be a therapeutic target for blocking cancer dissemination and enhancing anti-tumor immunity.

title: Clinical efficacy and pharmacological mechanism of Er Zhi Tian Gui formula in enhancing IVF-ET outcomes for diminished ovarian reserve
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the Er Zhi Tian Gui formula (EZTGF) improves IVF-ET outcomes in women with diminished ovarian reserve by modulating ovarian granulosa cell survival through the NEAT1/miR-10b-3p/FOXO3a regulatory axis. EZTGF treatment increases expression of the long non-coding RNA NEAT1 and the transcription factor FOXO3a while simultaneously suppressing the pro-apoptotic microRNA miR-10b-3p in granulosa cells. This molecular rebalancing inhibits granulosa cell apoptosis and promotes proliferation, resulting in improved embryo quality and higher proportions of high-quality embryos at the cleavage stage compared to placebo controls. The mechanism operates through NEAT1's competitive binding of miR-10b-3p (acting as a competing endogenous RNA), which liberates miR-10b-3p's target, FOXO3a, a key regulator of follicle maturation and apoptosis resistance. These findings reveal a novel pharmacological strategy for enhancing ovarian function in diminished ovarian reserve patients by targeting the lncRNA/microRNA/mRNA regulatory network governing oocyte granulosa cell fate.

title: Ovarian cancer and the heart: pathophysiology, chemotherapy-induced cardiotoxicity, and new therapeutic strategies
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian cancer is a lethal gynecologic malignancy driven by mutations in key tumor suppressors including p53, BRCA1/BRCA2, and oncogenic alterations in KRAS, BRAF, and the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway that promote cell survival and proliferation. MicroRNAs play critical regulatory roles in ovarian cancer pathogenesis, with elevated expression of miR-325, miR-429, miR-141, miR-182, and miR-214 promoting tumor initiation and chemoresistance, while downregulation of the tumor-suppressor miR-100 allows increased mTOR and PLK1 signaling. Long non-coding RNAs including lncRNA-H19, HCG18, HOTAIR, and KCNQ1OT1 facilitate cancer progression and chemoresistance through various mechanisms, including suppression of tumor-suppressive microRNAs and promotion of epithelial-mesenchymal transition. First-line chemotherapy with platinum-based drugs like carboplatin and paclitaxel is standard, though recurrent cancers are treated with doxorubicin, which exhibits cardiotoxicity through DNA intercalation, topoisomerase-II inhibition, mitochondrial cardiolipin binding, and reactive oxygen species production. Emerging therapeutic strategies including PARP inhibitors and phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors show promise in improving outcomes while mitigating chemotherapy-induced cardiotoxicity and promoting cancer cell apoptosis. Additionally, endometriosis is significantly associated with the development of endometrioid and clear cell ovarian carcinomas.

title: In silico screening system based on a transcription factors regulatory network only using transcriptomic data
summary: This study employs transcriptomic analysis and transcription factor regulatory networks to identify key molecular mechanisms relevant to reproductive tract biology and endometrial dysfunction. HMGA2 emerges as a critical uterus-specific transcription factor highly expressed in uterine epithelium where it regulates cell proliferation, angiogenesis, and epithelial junction maintenance, with its activity modulated by estrogen signaling. The research reveals distinct transcriptional programs controlling endothelial cell fate: ETS1 maintains endothelial barrier function while ELK1 suppresses endothelial-to-mesenchymal transition through fibroblast growth factor signaling, whereas TGFβ-induced SMAD2/3 activation promotes this transition. Additionally, the study identifies core transcription factors including DLX5 and DLX6 that regulate uterine gland development, while TCF3 mediates Wnt signaling-induced mesodermal differentiation through loss of pluripotency markers like POU5F1 and SOX2. This integrated transcriptomic approach successfully maps a regulatory network that illuminates how subtle changes in key transcription factor activity can reorganize cellular differentiation and tissue remodeling processes relevant to endometrial pathophysiology.

title: Sperm-carried IGF2: towards the discovery of a spark contributing to embryo growth and development
summary: This study demonstrates that insulin-like growth factor 2 (IGF2), a paternally expressed imprinted gene carried in sperm, plays a critical role in early embryo development and morphokinetics during assisted reproductive technology procedures. Higher sperm IGF2 mRNA levels correlate with accelerated embryo cell divisions and improved morphology, with an IGF2 mRNA index greater than 4.9 predicting successful blastocyst formation by day 5. IGF2 expression is regulated by H19, a long noncoding RNA controlled through methylation of the H19 gene at chromosome 11p15.5; abnormal methylation patterns in infertile males lead to decreased sperm IGF2 expression. IGF2 activates IGF1R signaling to promote both fetal and placental growth, with gene expression analysis revealing that IGF2 mRNA injection upregulates pathways controlling embryo size, mass, and biological quality. The article further integrates evidence that sperm-delivered regulatory RNAs influence multiple developmental processes including trophoblast function, supported by TLR4, EGFR, and thyroid hormone signaling pathways. These findings suggest that sperm IGF2 mRNA represents a measurable paternal biomarker for embryo developmental potential in fertility assessments.

title: METTL3 obstructs vascular smooth muscle cells osteogenic reprogramming by methylating Runx2 in chronic kidney disease
summary:
# Summary

In chronic kidney disease, METTL3 suppresses vascular calcification by negatively regulating the osteogenic transcription factor Runx2 through N6-methyladenosine (m6A) RNA methylation. METTL3 deposits m6A modifications on Runx2 mRNA, which promotes its recognition and degradation by the m6A reader protein YTHDF2, thereby reducing Runx2 stability and expression. This inhibition of Runx2 prevents the osteogenic reprogramming of vascular smooth muscle cells (VSMCs)—a pathological process in which these cells lose contractile markers (α-SMA, SM22α) and acquire bone-forming markers (osteopontin, collagen type I α1) that drive pathological vascular calcification. Conversely, METTL3 depletion increases Runx2 expression and mRNA stability, promoting VSMC osteogenic trans-differentiation and calcification progression; METTL3 overexpression reverses this phenotype. Serum METTL3 and m6A levels are significantly reduced in CKD patients with vascular calcification, and METTL3 shows diagnostic potential (AUC = 0.83) for predicting calcification severity. These findings establish the METTL3-m6A-YTHDF2-Runx2 pathway as a critical regulatory axis controlling VSMC phenotypic plasticity and vascular calcification in CKD.

title: Menstrual blood serum extracellular vesicles reveal novel molecular biomarkers and potential endotypes of unexplained infertility
summary: This study identifies menstrual blood serum extracellular vesicles (EVs) as a novel source of molecular biomarkers for unexplained infertility (uIF), revealing distinct immunological and proteomic signatures that stratify patients into different endotypes. Menstrual blood from infertile women shows elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-1β, CXCL8) and reduced anti-inflammatory mediators (IL-2, interferon-γ), alongside hormonal dysregulation characterized by lower FSH and progesterone levels. Comprehensive proteomic analysis of menstrual blood EVs reveals that uIF is associated with dysregulation of cell adhesion, immune response, apoptosis, oxidative stress, and lipid metabolism pathways, with reduced extracellular matrix organization and downregulation of proteins involved in proteolysis and signal transduction. The study identifies specific protein biomarkers for different uIF endotypes, including altered immune response patterns, and demonstrates that oviductal and endometrial EVs regulate sperm function and embryo-endometrium communication through delivery of adhesion molecules and functional proteins like PMCA4a. These findings suggest that EV-based proteomics can stratify uIF patients into clinically meaningful subgroups with distinct molecular mechanisms, enabling potential personalized therapeutic interventions targeting inflammation, oxidative stress, or implantation defects.

title: Latest Update on lncRNA in Epithelial Ovarian Cancer—A Scoping Review
summary: Long noncoding RNAs (lncRNAs) are critical regulators of epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC) progression, functioning through multiple mechanisms including chromatin modification, transcriptional control, and post-transcriptional processing to influence gene expression. Key lncRNAs such as XIST, H19, NEAT1, and MALAT1 promote cancer development by enhancing cell proliferation, invasion, migration, and chemoresistance, with MALAT1 additionally driving epithelial-mesenchymal transition in cervical cancer models. LncRNAs act as competing endogenous RNAs ("sponges") that interact with microRNAs to regulate cellular processes including metastasis, apoptosis, angiogenesis, and inflammation, exemplified by NEAT1's sponging of miR-365 and UCA1's interaction with the miR-654-5p/SIK2 axis. While oncogenic lncRNAs like HOTAIR promote tumor growth and invasion, tumor suppressor lncRNAs such as MEG3 inhibit cancer cell proliferation and induce apoptosis through p53-dependent pathways. Multiple lncRNA families, including SNHG and LINC members, specifically modulate chemotherapeutic sensitivity to drugs like paclitaxel, carboplatin, and cisplatin, suggesting potential therapeutic targets for improving EOC treatment outcomes. Dysregulated lncRNA expression is also implicated in endometriosis pathogenesis, highlighting the broader clinical relevance of these regulatory molecules beyond cancer contexts.

title: Endometrial Cancer Is Associated with Altered Metabolism and Composition of Fatty Acids
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial cancer is characterized by a distinctive fatty acid metabolism dysregulation that varies significantly with cancer stage. Early-stage tumors (IA-IB) exhibit markedly elevated de novo lipogenesis driven by upregulation of ACACA and FASN enzymes, resulting in increased total lipid content and accumulation of monounsaturated and very long-chain fatty acids while saturated fatty acid levels decrease. Key metabolic alterations include upregulation of SCD1 for monounsaturated fatty acid synthesis and ELOVL1 for very long-chain fatty acid elongation, coupled with downregulation of CPT1a and CD36, suppressing fatty acid oxidation and transport. Notably, high expression of elongase enzymes ELOVL2, ELOVL4, and ELOVL1 correlates with worse overall survival, disease-specific survival, and progression-free survival, establishing lipid metabolism dysregulation as a prognostic factor. The monounsaturated fatty acid oleic acid demonstrates therapeutic potential by activating PTEN/AKT/mTOR signaling to inhibit tumor growth and cell invasion, while systemic metabolic abnormalities including elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL, and hyperglycemia associate with endometrial cancer risk, particularly in early-stage disease linked to obesity.

title: Extracellular Vesicle-Derived microRNA Crosstalk Between Equine Chondrocytes and Synoviocytes—An In Vitro Approach
summary: This study demonstrates that extracellular vesicles (EVs) mediate critical intercellular communication between chondrocytes and synoviocytes, with microRNA cargo playing a central role in joint pathology. EVs from IL-1β-stimulated synovial cells carry modified miRNA cargo that aggravates joint inflammation through macrophage activation and promotes both cartilage destruction and extracellular matrix breakdown. The research identified distinct miRNA signatures: five miRNAs (miR-21, miR-221, miR-222, miR-100, miR-26a) linked to joint homeostasis, while nine miRNAs (including miR-27b, miR-23b, and miR-31) were differentially expressed and associated with cell migration, apoptosis, and inflammatory responses. Notably, mechanically regulated miRNAs including miR-221-3p and miR-222 are downregulated in osteoarthritis and their loss contributes to cartilage degradation, whereas miR-100-5p and miR-26a overexpression attenuate disease progression. These findings establish EV-mediated miRNA transfer as a fundamental mechanism linking synovial inflammation to chondrocyte injury and suggest that specific miRNAs may serve as therapeutic targets for joint disease intervention.

title: Exosome-based approaches in cancer along with unlocking new insights into regeneration of cancer-prone tissues
summary:
# Summary

Exosomes, nanosized extracellular vesicles secreted by most eukaryotic cells, serve as critical mediators in cancer biology through multiple mechanisms including intercellular communication, immune regulation, and tissue remodeling. Cancer cell-derived exosomes promote tumor progression by suppressing anti-tumor immunity—inhibiting NK and T cell activity while stimulating myeloid-derived suppressor cell proliferation—and by facilitating angiogenesis and metastasis through transport of bioactive molecules and specific microRNAs like miR-500a-5p and miR-580-5p. Conversely, therapeutic exosomes derived from dendritic cells, mesenchymal stem cells, and immune cells demonstrate considerable promise as delivery vehicles for anticancer drugs, therapeutic microRNAs, and gene therapy components, with multiple approaches successfully treating melanoma, lung cancer, breast cancer, and gastrointestinal malignancies. Notably, exosomal microRNAs such as miR-30c, miR-497, and engineered inhibitors of oncogenic miRNAs can suppress tumor growth and metastasis by modulating critical signaling pathways including Wnt/β-Catenin and NF-κB, while surface-modified exosomes enhance drug accumulation and overcome chemotherapy resistance. These findings establish exosome-based approaches as versatile platforms bridging cancer immunotherapy and regenerative medicine, with potential to unlock new therapeutic strategies for cancer-prone tissues.

title: Proteomic Analysis of Human Follicular Fluid-Derived Exosomes Reveals That Insufficient Folliculogenesis in Aging Women is Associated With Infertility
summary:
# Summary

This proteomic study reveals that follicular fluid-derived exosomes play a critical role in regulating folliculogenesis and oocyte quality, with age-related dysfunction contributing to infertility in aging women. Exosomes from young women activate the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway and facilitate follicle maturation through key proteins including ENO1, HSP90B1, fetuin-B, C7, and APOC4, which are associated with successful in vitro fertilization outcomes. In contrast, aging women exhibit reduced PI3K/AKT signaling and dysregulated exosomal protein profiles characterized by elevated inflammation, decreased metabolic function, increased B-cell activation, and enhanced pathogen-associated responses. The application of young donor-derived exosomes successfully promoted follicular maturation in aging mouse models, suggesting that exosomal therapeutics could be leveraged to treat age-related reproductive decline. These findings establish exosomal proteins as key mediators of reproductive aging and identify potential biomarkers and therapeutic targets for managing infertility in older women.

title: Coactosin-like F-actin binding protein (Cotl1) plays a key role in adipocyte differentiation and obesity
summary: Coactosin-like F-actin binding protein (Cotl1) emerges as a critical regulator of adipocyte differentiation and obesity development through its control of actin filament dynamics. Cotl1 expression increases during adipocyte differentiation and weight gain, where it stabilizes F-actin and regulates the expression of key adipogenic transcription factors including PPARγ, C/EBPα, and SREBF1. Cotl1-deficient mice display remarkable resistance to high-fat diet-induced obesity, exhibiting reduced weight gain, decreased hepatic steatosis, improved glucose tolerance, and enhanced insulin sensitivity compared to wild-type controls. Additionally, Cotl1 knockout mice show substantially improved lipid profiles with reduced triglycerides, cholesterol, and LDL levels alongside elevated protective HDL levels, along with normalized plasma leptin and insulin concentrations. These findings establish Cotl1 as a potential therapeutic target for obesity and associated metabolic disorders, as its genetic ablation prevents multiple aspects of diet-induced metabolic dysfunction through disruption of the actin-mediated signaling cascade governing adipogenesis.

title: Granulosa cell transcription is similarly impacted by superovulation and aging and predicts early embryonic trajectories
summary: This study reveals that superovulation and aging similarly dysregulate granulosa cell transcription, with profound consequences for oocyte development and embryonic potential. Both conditions disrupt key pathways including meiosis, mitochondrial metabolism, DNA damage response, and hormonal signaling, with superovulation impairing the normal luteinizing hormone surge response that typically upregulates amphiregulin (Areg) while downregulating estrogen receptor 2 (Esr2) and natriuretic peptide receptor 2 (Npr2) in granulosa cells. Superovulation causes widespread transcriptomic dysregulation affecting over 3,500 genes in oocytes and 2,765 genes in granulosa cells, while simultaneously disrupting critical post-transcriptional processes including polyadenylation, de-adenylation, and transcript degradation, and compromising the spindle assembly checkpoint through downregulation of Mad2l1 and Mad2l2. The bidirectional communication within cumulus-oocyte complexes is disrupted, as evidenced by the splitting of superovulated COCs into two distinct transcriptional clusters with heterogeneous cell-cell interactions, compared to the homogenous clustering of naturally ovulated COCs.
