Notably, granulosa cell transcriptional states prospectively predict oocyte quality and early embryonic developmental trajectories, offering a non-invasive biomarker approach for evaluating reproductive competence. These findings demonstrate that granulosa cell transcriptional markers could serve as predictive indicators of developmental success and illuminate shared mechanistic pathways underlying reduced fertility from both assisted reproduction and aging.

title: miR-27a rs895819 Polymorphism and Recurrent Pregnancy Loss in Caucasian Women: A Novel Genetic Risk Factor in a Challenging Fertility Dilemma
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study identifies the miR-27a rs895819 A>G polymorphism as a novel genetic risk factor for recurrent pregnancy loss in Caucasian women, with the GG genotype conferring a 6.7-fold increased risk while the AA genotype is protective. The polymorphism occurs in the stem-loop structure of miR-27a and alters miRNA processing by Dicer, resulting in differential expression levels that influence critical reproductive processes including placental development, trophoblast invasion, and endometrial decidualization. MicroRNAs, secreted by preimplantation embryos and blastocysts, regulate ovarian function, steroid hormone synthesis, and folliculogenesis, and dysregulated miRNA expression disrupts these mechanisms to compromise pregnancy maintenance and endometrial receptivity. The G allele may confer some protective effects through associations with higher circulating folate levels, which is critical for pregnancy maintenance. These findings suggest that SNPs in miRNA genes can alter mRNA binding affinity and miRNA maturation, potentially leading to implantation failure and impaired placental development in recurrent pregnancy loss.

title: The role of exosomal lncRNAs in mediating apoptosis and inflammation in UV-induced skin photoaging
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates how exosomal long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) mediate skin photoaging in response to UV radiation exposure. Chronic UV exposure, particularly UVA and UVB radiation, causes premature skin aging characterized by wrinkles, dryness, collagen degradation, and elastic fiber degeneration through multiple mechanisms including DNA damage, reactive oxygen species (ROS) production, and activation of cell cycle arrest pathways like p16(INK4a)/Rb and p53/p21(WAF1). Exosomes function as critical intercellular communicators that transport bioactive molecules, including lncRNAs, which regulate UV-induced cellular responses by enhancing DNA repair, suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokines, modulating oxidative stress pathways, and promoting collagen production. UV radiation alters lncRNA expression patterns, enabling these molecules to activate p53-mediated stress responses and modulate genes involved in collagen metabolism and extracellular matrix degradation, with specific lncRNAs like lnc-CD1D-2 being upregulated by ROS to promote melanin synthesis. The study demonstrates that exosomal lncRNAs are central mediators of the complex interplay between apoptosis, inflammation, and oxidative stress in photoaged skin, suggesting potential therapeutic targets for mitigating UV-induced skin damage.

title: Hypoxic endometrial epithelial cell-derived microRNAs effectively regulate the regenerative properties of mesenchymal stromal cells
summary:
# Summary

Hypoxic endometrial epithelial cells (EECs) secrete exosomes containing microRNAs miR-214-5p and miR-21-5p that regulate mesenchymal stromal cell (MSC) regenerative properties through the STAT3 signaling pathway. These miRNAs suppress MSC migration and promote their differentiation toward endometrial epithelial-like cells, effects that can be reversed by miRNA inhibition, which enhances MSC migratory capacity and wound healing ability. The mechanistic pathway involves miR-21-5p and miR-214-5p targeting PIAS3 and STAT3, where silencing of these miRNAs differentially affects STAT3/PIAS3 expression to favor EEC differentiation, confirmed by altered expression of stemness markers and commitment to osteogenic and adipogenic lineages. STAT3 inhibition blocks both MSC migration and EEC differentiation, establishing STAT3 as a central signaling hub. This work demonstrates that hypoxic endometrial damage-derived exosomal miRNAs represent a cell-cell communication mechanism that coordinates MSC behavior to support endometrial tissue regeneration, with potential implications for treating endometrial damage-associated infertility.

title: Exosomal miR-137-3p targets UBE3C to activate STAT3, promoting migration and differentiation into endometrial epithelial cell of human umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cells under hypoxia
summary:
# Summary

Thin endometrium, a major cause of recurrent implantation failure and infertility, can be therapeutically addressed through exosome-mediated stem cell regeneration. This study demonstrates that exosomes derived from hypoxia-damaged endometrial epithelial cells (EECD-ex) enhance human umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cell (hucMSC) migration and differentiation into endometrial epithelial cells through elevated miR-137-3p expression. The mechanistic pathway involves miR-137-3p directly binding to and downregulating UBE3C mRNA, which subsequently promotes STAT3 signaling activation; this pathway drives the upregulation of epithelial markers (CK19 and CD9) while suppressing stromal markers (CD13 and vimentin). Pharmacological inhibition of STAT3 or overexpression of UBE3C significantly attenuates miR-137-3p-induced hucMSC migration and differentiation, confirming the critical role of the miR-137-3p/UBE3C/STAT3 axis. These findings establish exosomal miR-137-3p as a key molecular mediator in endometrial regeneration and suggest that miR-137-3p-enriched exosomes may represent a novel therapeutic strategy for treating thin endometrium-associated reproductive failure.

title: Mitigating chemotherapy-induced granulosa cell damage: role of hUCMSC-EVs in regulating the lncRNA HCP5-miR-20a-5p-YAP1 network
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that human umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cell-derived extracellular vesicles (hUCMSC-EVs) protect granulosa cells from chemotherapy-induced damage through delivery of the long non-coding RNA HCP5. The protective mechanism operates via a competing endogenous RNA network in which HCP5 sponges miR-20a-5p, thereby preventing miR-20a-5p from inhibiting YAP1, a critical transcriptional regulator required for granulosa cell proliferation and DNA repair. Cyclophosphamide chemotherapy suppresses HCP5 expression and upregulates miR-20a-5p in granulosa cells, leading to YAP1 downregulation, increased apoptosis (elevated Bax/Bcl-2 ratio), and DNA damage signaling (elevated γH2AX and cleaved PARP). Treatment with hUCMSC-EVs reverses these damage signatures by restoring the HCP5-miR-20a-5p-YAP1 axis, thereby improving granulosa cell viability and preventing chemotherapy-induced premature ovarian failure. In vivo experiments confirmed that hUCMSC-EVs successfully localize to ovarian tissue and prevent cyclophosphamide-induced POF through modulation of this regulatory network, suggesting therapeutic potential for protecting female fertility in cancer patients undergoing chemotherapy.

title: Unveiling the Role of PNMA2 in Endometriosis: From Proliferation and Apoptosis to Immunomodulation
journal: Journal of Cellular and Molecular Medicine
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that PNMA2, a paraneoplastic antigen family member, is abnormally elevated in endometriosis tissues and cell lines, where it acts as a key driver of disease pathology. PNMA2 promotes endometrial cell proliferation, migration, and survival while suppressing apoptosis through inhibition of the mitochondrial apoptotic pathway and activation of autophagy; knockdown of PNMA2 upregulates pro-apoptotic proteins (P53, BAX, c-PARP) and downregulates autophagy markers (LC3, Beclin-1, ATG3). Beyond cell-intrinsic effects, PNMA2 expression correlates strongly with infiltration of multiple immune cell types including NK cells, B cells, T cells, and mast cells, suggesting its role in endometriosis-associated immunomodulation. The research establishes PNMA2 as a potential therapeutic target, as its suppression redirects cellular outcomes toward apoptosis and away from the pro-survival autophagy that characterizes endometriotic lesions. These findings connect PNMA2 to endometriosis progression and the disease's known risk for malignant transformation, particularly endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer.

title: A systematic review and meta-analysis of microRNAs in the diagnosis of early diabetic kidney disease
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluates microRNAs (miRNAs) as diagnostic biomarkers for early-stage diabetic kidney disease (DKD), a major microvascular complication of diabetes that can progress to end-stage renal disease. MiRNAs are short non-coding RNA molecules that regulate gene expression and control critical cellular processes including proliferation, apoptosis, and growth, and they circulate stably in biological fluids such as blood and urine, making them accessible for clinical detection. The analysis identifies several miRNAs with significant diagnostic value in DKD, particularly miR-192, miR-29c, miR-21, miR-216a, and miR-377, which show abnormal expression patterns in early disease stages, with urine-derived miRNAs demonstrating superior sensitivity (0.82) compared to blood-derived miRNAs (0.68). Multi-biomarker combinations—such as serum miR-21 with Smad1 and urinary neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin (NGAL), or MALAT1 with miR-29—provide significantly enhanced diagnostic efficacy for early DKD detection beyond traditional markers like urinary microalbumin. These findings suggest that circulating miRNAs represent promising non-invasive diagnostic tools that could complement or potentially replace renal biopsy as the gold standard for DKD diagnosis and assessment of disease progression.

title: Extracellular vesicles: Roles in oocytes and emerging therapeutic opportunities
summary:
# Summary

Extracellular vesicles, particularly exosomes derived from follicular fluid, play critical roles in oocyte quality and intercellular communication during folliculogenesis through cargo delivery of microRNAs and proteins. Multiple exosomal miRNAs regulate key signaling pathways governing oocyte development: miR-148a-3p and miR-21-5p enhance antioxidant capacity and glucose uptake respectively, while miR-31-5p promotes progesterone synthesis via WNT/β-catenin signaling. In pathological conditions including endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome, dysregulated exosomal miRNA profiles impair oocyte quality—iron overload in endometriosis triggers abnormal exosome release from granulosa cells that disrupts calcium and MAPK signaling, while elevated miR-143-3p and miR-122-5p in PCOS and endometriosis respectively suppress glycolysis and promote granulosa cell apoptosis. Emerging therapeutic strategies employ mesenchymal stem cell-derived exosomes to restore oocyte quality by delivering beneficial miRNAs such as miR-146a-5p and miR-320a that activate protective signaling pathways and enhance mitochondrial function, offering promising interventions for reproductive disorders associated with poor oocyte quality.

title: Advancing Ischemic Stroke Prognosis: Key Role of MiR-155 Non-Coding RNA
summary:
# Summary

MiR-155, a pro-inflammatory microRNA elevated in ischemic stroke, plays a central role in stroke pathogenesis through multiple interconnected mechanisms. This miRNA promotes oxidative stress, apoptosis, and neuroinflammation by targeting key regulatory proteins including HIF-1α, SIRT1, DUSP14, and Rheb, while simultaneously activating the TLR4/MyD88 and Ras/NF-κB signaling pathways. Importantly, inhibition of miR-155 or miR-155-5p significantly improves stroke outcomes by reducing oxidative stress, apoptosis, pro-inflammatory cytokine production, and brain edema while restoring neurological function. Therapeutic approaches leveraging long non-coding RNAs such as OIP5-AS1 and Oprm1, which suppress miR-155 expression, or direct miR-155 inhibitors show promising neuroprotective effects in preclinical stroke models. These findings establish miR-155 as a critical therapeutic target and potential biomarker for ischemic stroke prognosis and treatment response.

title: MicroRNA-based biomarkers in pain
summary:
# Summary

Dysregulated circulating microRNAs (miRNAs) represent promising noninvasive biomarkers for chronic pain conditions, including neuropathic pain. These miRNAs regulate gene expression by binding to the 3' untranslated regions of target mRNAs and are stabilized in bodily fluids through encapsulation in exosomes and extracellular vesicles or association with lipoproteins and argonaute proteins. Specific miRNA signatures, including miR-103, miR-124a, miR-203, and miR-7a, have been implicated in pain pathogenesis and show potential for patient stratification across different pain phenotypes. In complex regional pain syndrome, circulating miRNA profiles correlate with elevated inflammatory markers including vascular endothelial growth factor, interleukin-1 receptor antagonist, and monocyte chemotactic protein-1, demonstrating the utility of miRNA-based approaches for both mechanistic understanding and clinical diagnosis. Exosomes themselves play dual roles—dendritic cell and macrophage-derived exosomes can suppress inflammatory pain responses, while other exosomes may transport pathogenic components. High-precision quantitative PCR detection of these miRNA signatures enables sensitive monitoring of treatment response and disease progression in pain conditions.

title: Exploring miR-301a-3p and osteosarcoma: from expression differences to mechanism of action
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that miR-301a-3p functions as a tumor suppressor in osteosarcoma through a miR-301a-3p/MAFB regulatory axis. miR-301a-3p is significantly downregulated in osteosarcoma tissues and cells, and this low expression is associated with advanced TNM staging, lung metastasis, and poor patient prognosis. Mechanistically, miR-301a-3p directly targets and downregulates MAFB, a transcription factor that is conversely elevated in osteosarcoma; ectopic miR-301a-3p expression inhibits osteosarcoma cell proliferation, migration, and invasion, while MAFB overexpression reverses these inhibitory effects. Additionally, MAFB promotes cancer stemness and tumor-associated macrophage polarization, linking it to osteosarcoma progression. These findings establish miR-301a-3p as a potential prognostic biomarker and suggest that therapeutic strategies targeting the miR-301a-3p/MAFB axis, potentially combined with MAFB inhibitors, may provide novel treatment approaches for osteosarcoma patients.

title: MicroRNA biology and pain
journal: Progress in molecular biology and translational science
summary:
# Summary

MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are small noncoding RNAs approximately 22 nucleotides in length that regulate approximately 60% of human genes through posttranscriptional mechanisms, including translational repression and mRNA degradation via binding to complementary sequences in the 3' untranslated regions of target mRNAs. The miRNA processing pathway involves sequential cleavage by the Drosha/microprocessor complex in the nucleus and by Dicer in the cytoplasm, with alternative pathways such as mirtrons bypassing Drosha processing; processed miRNAs are incorporated into the RNA-induced silencing complex (RISC) containing argonaut, TRBP, and other essential proteins. Dysregulation of miRNA expression is associated with pathogenesis of human diseases, including cancer, where over 50% of miRNA genes localize to fragile sites and cancer susceptibility loci. MiRNAs are released into circulation through multiple mechanisms including encapsulation in exosomes, where specific proteins like hnRNPA2B1 control RNA sorting, and these circulating miRNAs exhibit resistance to degradation, enabling their utility as biomarkers for disease diagnosis and monitoring. Therapeutic manipulation of miRNA function can be achieved through multiple approaches including synthetic mimics for overexpression and anti-miRNA oligonucleotides or miRNA sponges for inhibition, with expression profiling accomplished using microarrays, RNA sequencing, and quantitative PCR methodologies.

title: Screening for biomarkers of bronchopulmonary dysplasia: a bioinformatics analysis
summary:
# Summary

This bioinformatics study identifies miR-9-5p and GCH1 as key biomarkers in bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD) pathogenesis through a ferroptosis-dependent mechanism. Hyperoxia exposure upregulates miR-9-5p in lung epithelial cells, which in turn downregulates GCH1, the rate-limiting enzyme that produces the antioxidant tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4). This cascade leads to elevated reactive oxygen species, malondialdehyde, and iron accumulation, collectively promoting ferroptosis and oxidative injury. Additionally, miR-9-5p suppresses the inflammatory TLR4/NF-κB signaling pathway by targeting CXCL11, suggesting dual protective and pathogenic roles. The study demonstrates that GCH1 overexpression or pharmacological interventions like caffeine can mitigate hyperoxic lung injury by restoring BH4 levels and reducing oxidative damage, pointing toward miR-9-5p and GCH1 as potential therapeutic targets for preventing BPD in vulnerable preterm infants.

title: LncRNA NEAT1 participates in diminished ovarian reserve by affecting granulosa cell apoptosis and estradiol synthesis via the miR-204-5p/ESR1 axis
summary: This study elucidates the role of long noncoding RNA NEAT1 in diminished ovarian reserve through a regulatory axis involving granulosa cells, the miR-204-5p/ESR1 pathway, and estradiol synthesis. NEAT1 expression is significantly reduced in granulosa cells of women with diminished ovarian reserve and correlates positively with markers of ovarian function including antral follicle count and anti-Müllerian hormone. The mechanistic findings show that NEAT1 acts as a molecular sponge that sequesters miR-204-5p in the cytoplasm, thereby preventing miR-204-5p from downregulating its target gene ESR1 (estrogen receptor-α). Through elevated ESR1 expression, the pathway promotes estradiol biosynthesis by upregulating key steroidogenic enzymes (StAR and CYP19A1) and activates the downstream MAPK/ERK signaling cascade, which collectively drives granulosa cell proliferation and inhibits apoptosis. Functional validation demonstrated that inhibiting miR-204-5p reverses the detrimental effects on granulosa cell viability and steroid hormone production, while silencing ESR1 abolishes these benefits, confirming the causal role of this axis. These findings suggest that restoring NEAT1 expression or suppressing miR-204-5p could represent therapeutic strategies to preserve ovarian function and prevent follicular atresia in women with diminished ovarian reserve.

title: A Comprehensive Review on LncRNAs/miRNAs‐DNMT1 Axis in Human Cancer: Mechanistic and Clinical Application
journal: Journal of Cellular and Molecular Medicine
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review elucidates the mechanistic interplay between long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs), microRNAs (miRNAs), and DNA methyltransferase 1 (DNMT1) in cancer pathogenesis. DNA methylation, catalyzed by DNMT1 with cofactor assistance from UHRF1, regulates chromatin structure and gene expression while suppressing tumor suppressor genes, driving tumorigenesis and cancer aggressiveness across multiple cancer types including cervical, breast, and hepatocellular carcinomas. Non-coding RNAs reciprocally regulate this epigenetic axis: specific lncRNAs (NEAT1, ATB, GAS5, and KIF9-AS1) interact with DNMT1 to modulate its activity and localization, while miRNAs simultaneously govern epigenetic modifications and target genes involved in cell proliferation, apoptosis, and metastasis. The review highlights exemplary regulatory circuits, such as the FOXO3a/FOXM1/SOX2 pathway in breast cancer where DNMT1-mediated suppression of FOXO3a promotes aggressive phenotypes, and the DNMT1-RAI2 axis in hepatocellular carcinoma where lncRNA-recruited DNMT1 inhibits tumor suppressor expression. These findings underscore that dysregulation of the lncRNA/miRNA-DNMT1 axis represents a critical mechanism driving abnormal methylation patterns and aberrant gene expression, offering multiple therapeutic targets for cancer intervention.

title: Analysis of lcnRNAHomebox transcript antisense RNA (HOTAIR) expression in Polish women with endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This study examined lncRNA HOTAIR expression in Polish women with endometriosis and identified it as a key contributor to disease pathogenesis. HOTAIR expression is significantly elevated in endometriosis tissues, particularly in advanced stage IV disease, compared to normal endometrium. The mechanisms underlying HOTAIR's pathogenic role involve exosomal HOTAIR promoting angiogenesis and endometrial stromal cell migration and invasion through the miR-761/HDAC1 axis, while HOTAIR also acts as a competing endogenous RNA by binding miR-519b-3p to suppress its regulatory effects on stromal cell invasiveness. Suppression of HOTAIR upregulates protective genes HOXA5 and downregulates matrix metallopeptidases MMP2 and MMP9, indicating that HOTAIR normally represses these genes through regulatory axes with HOXD10 and HOXA5, which inhibit proliferation, migration, invasion, and angiogenesis. Genetic polymorphisms rs1838169 and rs17720428 in the HOTAIR locus may increase endometriosis susceptibility by elevating HOTAIR expression levels, establishing HOTAIR as both a mechanistic driver and potential biomarker of endometriosis progression.

title: Enhancing in vitro osteogenic differentiation of mesenchymal stem cells via sustained dexamethasone delivery in 3D-Printed hybrid scaffolds based on polycaprolactone-nanohydroxyapatite/alginate-gelatin for bone regeneration
summary:
# Summary

This study developed 3D-printed hybrid scaffolds combining polycaprolactone-nanohydroxyapatite with alginate-gelatin hydrogels to deliver dexamethasone (DEX) for bone regeneration using endometrial mesenchymal stem cells. DEX, a synthetic glucocorticoid, promotes osteogenic differentiation and mineralization while reducing inflammation through upregulation of bone-related genes including osteonectin and COL1A1, and stimulation of osteoblast development. The hybrid scaffold design leverages the mechanical properties and drug delivery capacity of polycaprolactone microparticles, the biocompatibility of alginate-gelatin, and the osteoinductive properties of nanohydroxyapatite to create an optimized microenvironment for cell proliferation and differentiation. DEX-loaded scaffolds demonstrated controlled drug release and significantly enhanced osteogenic differentiation compared to control scaffolds, as evidenced by increased expression of osteogenic markers and mineralization. Endometrial stem cells proved particularly valuable for this application due to their ease of harvest, high proliferative capacity, and potential to promote both osteogenesis and angiogenesis. These results demonstrate that drug-loaded 3D-printed hybrid nanocomposite scaffolds represent a promising approach for advancing bone tissue engineering and regenerative medicine applications.

title: Aberrant downregulation of Y-box binding protein 1 expression impairs the cell cycle in an m5C-dependent manner in human granulosa cells from patients with primary ovarian insufficiency
journal: Cellular and Molecular Life Sciences: CMLS
summary: This study reveals that Y-box binding protein 1 (YBX1), an m5C RNA reader, is significantly reduced in granulosa cells from patients with primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) and plays a critical role in controlling cell cycle progression through an m5C-dependent mechanism. YBX1 knockdown impairs granulosa cell proliferation by blocking G1/S transition and reducing expression of key cell cycle regulators including CDK4, CDK6, Cyclin D1, and genes like CCND1, CCNE1, and RCC1, while YBX1 overexpression promotes proliferation and increases these same regulatory proteins. The study demonstrates that YBX1 functions as an m5C reader that directly binds and stabilizes cell cycle-associated transcripts bearing 5-methylcytosine modifications, and that YBX1 knockdown causes widespread m5C methylome remodeling with hypomethylation of sites enriched in DNA replication and cell cycle pathways. Additionally, YBX1 mRNA expression in granulosa cells positively correlates with anti-Müllerian hormone levels and in vitro fertilization outcomes while negatively correlating with follicle-stimulating hormone levels, establishing YBX1 as a biomarker for ovarian function. These findings suggest that aberrant downregulation of YBX1-mediated m5C-dependent gene expression represents a key molecular mechanism underlying granulosa cell dysfunction in POI.

title: The in vivo effects of knockdown of long non-coding RNA XIST on fibroid growth and gene expression
journal: FASEB journal : official publication of the Federation of American Societies for Experimental Biology
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the long non-coding RNA XIST, which is elevated in uterine fibroids, functions as a competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) that sequesters miR-29c and miR-200c, thereby preventing these microRNAs from suppressing their target genes involved in extracellular matrix production and cell proliferation. XIST knockdown increased miR-29c and miR-200c expression, which in turn downregulated multiple fibroid-promoting genes including collagen (COL3A1), fibronectin (FN1), TGF-β3, SPARC, and CDK2, leading to a 15% reduction in tumor weight and decreased cell proliferation markers (Ki67 and E2F1). The study further revealed that XIST knockdown suppressed the TDO2-AhR-CYP1B1 axis, an important pathway in tryptophan metabolism dysregulation observed in fibroids. Estrogen and progesterone upregulate XIST expression, linking hormonal signaling to this pathogenic mechanism. These findings identify XIST as a key therapeutic target for fibroid management through modulation of the miR-29c/miR-200c regulatory network.

title: Endometriosis‐derived exosomes encapsulated miR‐196a‐5p mediate macrophage polarization through regulation of the Hippo pathway
journal: Journal of Cell Communication and Signaling
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that endometriosis-derived exosomes contribute to immune imbalance by suppressing the microRNA miR-196a-5p, which normally activates the Hippo pathway to promote protective M1-type macrophage polarization. Exosomes from endometriosis patients are depleted of miR-196a-5p and instead promote M2-type macrophage polarization through Hippo pathway inactivation, leading to increased expression of M2 markers (CD206, CD163) while reducing M1 markers (iNOS, CD86). M2-type macrophages subsequently promote proliferation and migration of endometrial cells (12Z cells), exacerbating disease progression. Restoration of miR-196a-5p in endometriosis-derived exosomes reverses this pathologic polarization by reactivating the Hippo pathway, restoring M1 macrophage dominance and suppressing endometrial cell growth and metastatic potential. These findings identify serum exosomal miR-196a-5p as a key regulatory node in endometriosis pathogenesis and suggest that miR-196a-5p-enriched exosomes could serve as a therapeutic intervention to restore immune balance and alleviate disease symptoms.

title: MicroRNA Signatures in Endometrial Receptivity—Unlocking Their Role in Embryo Implantation and IVF Success: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

MicroRNAs play critical regulatory roles in endometrial receptivity and embryo implantation through multiple interconnected pathways essential for reproductive success. Key miRNAs including miR-145, miR-30d, miR-223-3p, and miR-125b influence implantation-related pathways such as HOXA10 and LIF-STAT3 signaling, with dysregulated expression contributing to inadequate decidualization, immunological imbalance, and poor angiogenesis. Dysregulated miRNAs are strongly associated with recurrent implantation failure and endometriosis-associated infertility, with specific mechanisms including miR-135a/b inhibition of HOXA10, miR-125b and miR-124-3p suppression of LIF, and altered expression of angiogenic regulators like miR-27a, miR-20a, and miR-126 that target VEGFA and HIF-1α. Beyond individual miRNA effects, these molecules function within competing endogenous RNA networks regulated by long non-coding RNAs (H19, NEAT1) and circular RNAs (circ_0038383), while genetic polymorphisms in miR-146a, miR-196a2, and miR-125a further increase reproductive risks. The integration of miRNA regulation with epigenetic modifications such as DNA methylation of miRNA promoters underscores the complex multilayered control governing endometrial remodeling, immunological tolerance, angiogenesis, and stromal cell differentiation during the implantation window.

title: Exosomal MicroRNAs as Epigenetic Biomarkers for Endometriosis: A Systematic Review and Bioinformatics Analysis
summary: This systematic review and bioinformatics analysis identifies exosomal microRNAs as critical epigenetic biomarkers in endometriosis pathogenesis, with multiple miRNAs showing consistent dysregulation across studies. Key dysregulated miRNAs include miR-200c-3p (consistently upregulated), miR-22-3p, miR-320a, miR-320b, and miR-1273g-3p (with conflicting directional effects across studies), along with upregulated miRNAs such as miR-210-3p, miR-20b-5p, miR-625-5p, miR-155-5p, and miR-146a-5p. These exosomal miRNAs regulate critical cellular processes including apoptosis, differentiation, and proliferation through their involvement in the PI3K/Akt, MAPK, and TGF-β signaling pathways, which collectively promote cell proliferation, migration, and inflammatory responses characteristic of endometriosis. The clinical relevance of these findings is underscored by endometriosis associations with dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility, making exosomal miRNAs attractive candidates for disease biomarkers given their stability in blood and resistance to degradation. The heterogeneous findings across studies highlight the need for standardized methodologies to establish a robust miRNA signature for endometriosis diagnosis and monitoring.

title: Drug Screening of Primary Human Endometriotic Cells Based on Micro‐Encapsulating Microfluidic Chip
summary: This study developed a microfluidic chip-based platform for drug screening using primary human ectopic endometrial stromal cells (hESCs) encapsulated in biocompatible carboxymethyl cellulose-alginate microcapsules. The researchers evaluated multiple therapeutic agents against endometriosis, finding that dienogest effectively inhibited cell proliferation across multiple patient samples, while dydrogesterone showed variable effects, and novel drugs including cilengitide and wortmannin demonstrated promising anti-proliferative mechanisms. Cilengitide, an integrin antagonist with pro-apoptotic and anti-angiogenic properties, downregulated BMP4 expression and inhibited cell proliferation, whereas wortmannin, a phosphoinositol-3 kinase inhibitor with anti-inflammatory activity, upregulated pro-apoptotic genes BBC3 and BIM—which are notably reduced in endometriosis tissues. The study validated endometriosis-associated markers including elevated CD44 and CD10 expression in patient-derived spheroids and demonstrated normalization of serum CA125 levels following treatment in clinical follow-up. This microfluidic-based personalized drug screening approach enables identification of patient-specific therapeutic responses and may improve treatment efficacy for the estimated 190 million women worldwide suffering from endometriosis-related symptoms including dysmenorrhea and infertility.

title: The role of long non-coding ribonucleic acid HOXA11-AS in endometriosis therapy
summary:
# Summary

HOXA11-AS is a long non-coding RNA that plays a critical role in endometriosis pathophysiology by promoting endometrial proliferation, cell invasion, and migration while simultaneously preventing decidualization and normal endometrial development. Elevated HOXA11-AS expression in endometriotic lesions drives disease progression by upregulating pro-proliferative and pro-invasive genes including ITGB3, AKT1, MMP2, and MMP9, which collectively enhance cell adhesion, growth, angiogenesis, and invasion. Progesterone normally suppresses HOXA11-AS expression and promotes decidualization; however, progesterone resistance in endometriosis impairs this regulatory mechanism, allowing pathological HOXA11-AS activity to persist. Notably, progestin therapy effectively reduces HOXA11-AS expression, suggesting that therapeutic suppression of this lncRNA represents a central mechanism for preventing endometriosis growth and invasion. The reciprocal relationship between HOXA11-AS and HOXA11 is also significant, as HOXA11-AS overexpression suppresses HOXA11, a gene essential for normal uterine development and endometrial receptivity. These findings establish HOXA11-AS as a key therapeutic target for endometriosis treatment and provide mechanistic insights into how progesterone-based therapies exert their clinical benefits.

title: CEBPB promotes transformation of endometrial complex atypical hyperplasia to endometrial cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies CEBPB (CCAAT/enhancer-binding protein beta) as a critical transcription factor driving the malignant transformation of endometrial complex atypical hyperplasia (CAH) to endometrial cancer. CEBPB is significantly overexpressed in CAH tissues compared to normal endometrium, and its expression decreases as disease regresses, supporting its role in disease progression. Functional studies demonstrate that CEBPB knockdown in primary CAH cultures suppresses proliferation, epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition, migration, and invasion while simultaneously promoting apoptosis, indicating that CEBPB actively promotes the malignant phenotype. Mechanistically, CEBPB promotes the expression of mesenchymal markers (Vimentin) while repressing epithelial markers (CK8/18), consistent with its pro-EMT activity. The findings suggest CEBPB could serve as a biomarker for identifying CAH patients at high risk for endometrial cancer progression and represent a potential therapeutic target for preventing malignant transformation.

title: miR-17-5p is highly expressed in patients with lung cancer and promoted lung cancer by targeting SIK1
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that miR-17-5p functions as an oncogene in lung adenocarcinoma by directly targeting and suppressing SIK1, a tumor suppressor protein. miR-17-5p is significantly upregulated in lung cancer tissues and cell lines compared to normal lung tissue, where it binds to the 3' untranslated region of SIK1 mRNA, leading to downregulation of SIK1 at both transcriptional and translational levels. Overexpression of miR-17-5p promotes multiple hallmarks of cancer progression including cell proliferation, invasion, and migration while simultaneously inhibiting apoptosis; conversely, miR-17-5p inhibition reverses these effects and restores SIK1 expression. The clinical significance of these findings is underscored by the inverse correlation between miR-17-5p expression and patient survival, as well as evidence that miR-17-5p may serve as a diagnostic biomarker and therapeutic target in lung cancer. These results establish the miR-17-5p/SIK1 regulatory axis as a key mechanism driving lung adenocarcinoma pathogenesis.

title: Estrogen inhibits hepatocellular carcinoma progression dependent on HOXA11-AS/HOXA11
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that estrogen suppresses hepatocellular carcinoma progression through a regulatory axis involving the long non-coding RNA HOXA11-AS and its target gene HOXA11. Estrogen promotes apoptosis and reduces cell viability in HCC cells, effects that are dependent on the HOXA11-AS/HOXA11 interaction, where HOXA11-AS stabilizes HOXA11 mRNA by preventing RNase degradation. Notably, both HOXA11-AS and HOXA11 are elevated in HCC tissues and associated with poor prognosis, including reduced overall survival, increased recurrence, and vascular invasion. The mechanistic basis involves HOXA11's ability to directly bind the c-MET promoter and drive transcription of this oncogenic kinase; estrogen counteracts this by inhibiting c-MET phosphorylation and suppressing the downstream c-MET/AKT/mTOR pathway. Knockdown of HOXA11-AS significantly inhibited HCC cell proliferation, migration, and invasion while enhancing estrogen-induced apoptosis, suggesting that disrupting this axis could represent a therapeutic strategy for HCC.

title: Dysregulation of miR-330-3p is Involved in the Occurrence and Development of Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension Caused by Congenital Heart Disease
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies miR-330-3p dysregulation as a key pathogenic factor in pulmonary arterial hypertension associated with congenital heart disease (CHD-PAH). Serum miR-330-3p levels are significantly elevated in CHD-PAH patients compared to healthy controls and show diagnostic potential for disease stratification. Mechanistically, hypoxia upregulates miR-330-3p in human pulmonary artery smooth muscle cells (HPASMCs), which then directly targets and downregulates the transcription factor KLF10, thereby promoting pathological cell proliferation, migration, and inflammatory cytokine production (IL-6 and TNF-α). Knockdown of miR-330-3p reverses these hypoxia-induced pathological effects, while simultaneous knockdown of KLF10 ablates the protective benefits, confirming the miR-330-3p–KLF10 regulatory axis drives disease progression. These findings suggest that miR-330-3p represents both a biomarker for CHD-PAH diagnosis and a potential therapeutic target for intervention.

title: The Whisper of the Follicle: A Systematic Review of Micro Ribonucleic Acids as Predictors of Oocyte Quality and In Vitro Fertilization Outcomes
summary: This systematic review elucidates microRNAs (miRNAs) as critical regulators of ovarian function and predictors of reproductive outcomes through multiple mechanistic pathways. MiRNAs, which are expressed in granulosa cells, theca cells, and oocytes, regulate gene expression post-transcriptionally by binding to the 3' untranslated regions of target mRNAs to induce translational repression or mRNA destruction, and their levels are dynamically regulated by hormonal stimuli including FSH and LH. In polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), dysregulation of miR-132, miR-320, miR-222, and miR-146a correlates with decreased steroidogenesis, insulin resistance, and follicular inflammation, ultimately disrupting folliculogenesis and contributing to female infertility through persistent anovulation and hyperandrogenism. Conversely, elevated miR-202-5p and miR-224 in high-quality embryos and successful in vitro fertilization cycles promote granulosa cell proliferation and estrogen synthesis through the TGF-β/Smad pathway and aromatase regulation. MiRNA dysregulation impacts critical signaling cascades including PI3K/AKT (which promotes follicular survival), WNT/β-catenin (which regulates follicle maturation and ovulation), and MAPK/ERK (which mediates ovulatory signaling), collectively demonstrating that miRNA profiling in follicular fluid represents a promising biomarker strategy for predicting oocyte quality and fertility outcomes.

title: Exosomal Communication Between Cumulus–Oocyte Complexes and Granulosa Cells: A New Molecular Axis for Oocyte Competence in Human-Assisted Reproduction
summary:
# Summary

Exosomal microRNAs (ex-miRs) secreted by granulosa and cumulus cells function as critical paracrine mediators regulating oocyte competence and follicular development in human reproduction. Key ex-miRs including miR-21, miR-34c, miR-143-3p, miR-155-5p, miR-339-5p, and miR-424-5p control distinct cellular processes—such as apoptosis, glycolysis, cell cycle progression, and mitochondrial function—through modulation of the PI3K-AKT, TGF-β/SMAD, and related signaling pathways. The exosomal miRNA profile in follicular fluid correlates directly with oocyte quality and fertilization success, with miR-21 particularly enhancing cumulus-oocyte complex survival and meiosis II oocyte yield by promoting anti-apoptotic signaling and preventing follicular atresia. Dysregulation of exosomal miRNAs is associated with reproductive disorders including polycystic ovarian syndrome, diminished ovarian reserve, and poor ovarian response. These findings underscore exosomal ex-miRs as biomarkers and potential therapeutic targets for improving assisted reproductive outcomes by optimizing the intercellular communication network within the follicular microenvironment.

title: Embryo-derived trypsin-induced calcium entry is inhibited by endometrial infertility factor, LEFTY2
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

This study elucidates a molecular mechanism of embryo implantation failure in unexplained infertility by demonstrating that LEFTY2, an infertility-associated protein produced by decidualizing endometrial stromal cells, inhibits the trypsin-induced calcium signaling cascade essential for successful pregnancy. Embryo-derived trypsin activates a pathway wherein it cleaves the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC), causing cell membrane depolarization that opens L-type voltage-gated calcium channels and triggers sustained intracellular calcium elevation. This calcium increase subsequently promotes COX2-dependent prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) release, which facilitates implantation and decidualization. LEFTY2 pathologically disrupts this pathway by downregulating L-type calcium channel expression and directly inhibiting trypsin-induced calcium entry, preventing the necessary calcium-PGE2 signaling cascade. Supporting this mechanism, embryos with higher trypsin activity show significantly better implantation potential, while experimental elevation of LEFTY2 in the mouse uterus causes implantation failure. These findings establish LEFTY2-mediated suppression of trypsin-calcium signaling as a key mechanism underlying unexplained infertility and suggest potential therapeutic targets for improving reproductive outcomes.

title: Circulating exosome-derived miR-191-5p is a novel therapeutic biomarker for radiotherapy in esophageal squamous cell carcinoma patients
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies circulating exosomal miR-191-5p as a novel biomarker for predicting radiotherapy resistance in esophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC) patients. Elevated miR-191-5p promotes ESCC cell proliferation, invasion, and migration, while simultaneously conferring resistance to radiation-induced apoptosis by directly targeting and downregulating death-associated protein kinase 1 (DAPK1), which normally activates pro-apoptotic signaling through the MAPK-JNK pathway. Mechanistically, miR-191-5p binds to DAPK1's 3' UTR to suppress its expression, thereby inhibiting JNK pathway activation and reducing cellular apoptosis following irradiation. High circulating exosomal miR-191-5p expression correlates with lower five-year progression-free survival rates in ESCC patients undergoing radiotherapy and enriches for radiation resistance genes identified through TCGA database analysis. These findings establish tumor-derived exosomal miR-191-5p as a minimally invasive, circulating biomarker for predicting treatment response and prognosis in ESCC radiotherapy.

title: Extracellular vesicles in reproductive biology and disorders: a comprehensive review
summary:
# Summary

Extracellular vesicles (EVs), including exosomes and prostasomes, are critical mediators of intercellular communication across reproductive biology, regulating key processes from gametogenesis through implantation by modulating inflammation, angiogenesis, androgen production, and immune tolerance. Dysfunctional EV activity is associated with major reproductive disorders including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, male infertility, and recurrent pregnancy loss. In the male reproductive tract, seminal plasma EVs and epididymosomes enhance sperm maturation and motility through delivery of functional proteins and miRNAs, while prostasomes facilitate capacitation and the acrosome reaction. In female reproduction, follicular fluid-derived EVs support oocyte competence and maturation through miRNA signaling, including miR-17/miR-92-mediated enhancement of oocyte diameter and miR-146a-5p/miR-21-5p-driven oocyte development. Conversely, dysregulated miRNA expression in EVs from individuals with PCOS—such as elevated miR-424, which inhibits granulosa cell proliferation—demonstrates how aberrant EV-miRNA signaling contributes to reproductive pathology.

title: Role and potential mechanisms of miR-100 in different diseases (Review)
summary:
# Summary

This review examines miR-100 and its variant miR-100-5p across multiple disease contexts, revealing pleiotropic roles depending on cellular context and target genes. In cancer cells, miR-100 broadly promotes proliferation, invasion, and migration while modulating apoptosis. The mechanistic studies show that miR-100-5p downregulates MTMR3 to activate PI3K/AKT and ERK signaling, promoting epidermal stem cell proliferation and wound healing, while simultaneously suppressing myofibroblast differentiation through tribbles pseudokinase 2 downregulation. In bone and joint pathology, miR-100-5p is elevated in osteoporosis where it inhibits osteogenic differentiation and bone formation through TMEM135 and KDM6B suppression, while in osteoarthritis, miR-100-5p is reduced but when delivered via exosomes promotes cartilage homeostasis by suppressing mTOR and activating protective autophagy. Additionally, miR-100-5p exhibits significant neuroprotective properties in neurodegenerative diseases through multiple pathways: it reduces oxidative stress via NOX4 inhibition and antioxidant factor upregulation, dampens neuroinflammation by suppressing TLR7/NF-κB signaling, and improves motor outcomes in Parkinson's disease and spinal cord injury. These findings underscore miR-100's context-dependent therapeutic potential across diverse pathologies through its capacity to modulate cell proliferation, differentiation, autophagy, and inflammatory responses.

title: The bovine oviductal environment and composition are negatively affected by elevated body energy reserves
summary:
# Summary

Elevated body energy reserves (HBER) in cattle impair oviductal function through multiple mechanisms involving insulin resistance and altered extracellular vesicle composition. High body energy reserves lead to oviductal insulin resistance and hyperinsulinemia, which negatively affect embryo metabolism and reduce reproductive success. HBER animals demonstrate increased concentration and size of extracellular vesicles in the ampulla and isthmus, respectively, along with altered microRNA profiles carried by these vesicles, including upregulation of miRNAs associated with insulin and endocrine resistance (bta-miR-106a and related miRNAs). Extracellular vesicles mediate gametic-embryonic communication and development through their cargo of microRNAs that regulate cellular growth, migration, differentiation, and metabolism. The differential gene expression between HBER and moderate body energy reserve (MBER) animals reveals distinct transcriptional signatures, with MBER animals showing greater ampullary vascularization and upregulation of genes supporting normal oviductal function. These findings suggest that excessive body energy reserves compromise reproductive competence by triggering metabolic stress that increases extracellular vesicle secretion and alters their molecular cargo, ultimately impairing early embryonic development.

title: The Role of miRNA in Endometriosis-Related Infertility—An Update
summary: Dysregulated microRNAs (miRNAs) play a central role in endometriosis pathogenesis and associated infertility by controlling cellular proliferation, migration, and progesterone sensitivity. Specific miRNA abnormalities contribute to the disease through distinct mechanisms: downregulation of miR-370-3p increases estrogen biosynthesis, decreased miR-142-3p promotes lesion growth and metastasis, and dysregulation of miR-29c and miR-194-3p drives progesterone resistance via FKBP4 signaling. The disease manifests clinically through chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility linked to blocked fallopian tubes and diminished ovarian reserve, alongside comorbidities including rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease. Endometriosis-associated infertility stems from impaired endometrial receptivity caused by aberrant miRNA expression patterns during the menstrual cycle, particularly involving miR-543, miR-135a/b, and the let-7 family. Therapeutic strategies targeting specific miRNAs—such as miR-142-3p injection to shrink lesions or aromatase inhibitors to restore let-7 levels—represent promising approaches to restore reproductive function and alleviate inflammatory and immunological dysregulation characteristic of endometriosis.

title: Unveiling the intricacies: small interfering RNA targeting Snail-1 unravels dynamics in endometrial carcinoma cell behavior
summary: This study demonstrates that Snail-1 is a critical transcription factor driving epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) in endometrial carcinoma cells through suppression of E-cadherin and upregulation of invasion-promoting factors including matrix metalloproteinase-9, vimentin, and fibronectin. Targeted silencing of Snail-1 using specific siRNA at 60 pmol/mL dosage significantly reduced cell viability and migration in HEC-1A endometrial carcinoma cells while simultaneously increasing E-cadherin expression and promoting apoptosis. The siRNA treatment also downregulated key signaling molecules including MMP-9 (over 50% reduction), vimentin, Notch1, and the phosphoinositide 3-kinase/protein kinase B and extracellular signal-regulated kinase pathways, while upregulating the tumor-suppressive microRNA-34a. These findings collectively establish Snail-1 as a promising therapeutic target for endometrial carcinoma, with siRNA-mediated knockdown effectively reversing EMT phenotypes and suppressing the invasive and metastatic potential of cancer cells.

title: Ping-Chong-Jiang-Ni Formula effectively inhibits migration and invasion of endometriosis 12Z cell line via TRIP13
journal: Journal of Food and Drug Analysis
summary:
# Summary

The Ping-Chong-Jiang-Ni Formula (PCJNF), a traditional Chinese medicine preparation, effectively suppresses endometriosis by inhibiting the TRIP13 gene, which is significantly upregulated in ectopic endometrial lesions. PCJNF reduces ectopic endometrial stromal cell proliferation, promotes apoptosis, and suppresses migration and invasion—key pathological processes in endometriosis development—through activation of the JNK signaling pathway. The formula also alleviates endometriosis-associated pain by reducing inflammatory mediators (TNF-α and NGF) and downregulating TRPV1, PKC, and other pain-related signaling molecules. TRIP13 knockdown alone reduces cell migration and invasion while increasing epithelial marker E-cadherin and decreasing mesenchymal marker vimentin, demonstrating TRIP13's central role in the epithelial-mesenchymal transition associated with endometriosis pathogenesis. When PCJNF treatment is combined with TRIP13 knockdown, there is further suppression of migration and invasion capabilities, suggesting PCJNF's mechanism involves both TRIP13-dependent and independent pathways. These findings establish PCJNF as a promising therapeutic agent targeting the molecular drivers of endometrial cell invasiveness and pain in endometriosis.

title: TRIB3 regulates Cx43 expression in human granulosa cells via the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway: an in vitro study
summary: This in vitro study demonstrates that TRIB3 upregulates Connexin 43 (Cx43) expression in human granulosa cells through inhibition of the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway, with pathophysiological implications for obesity-related fertility decline. Elevated free fatty acids, particularly palmitic acid, increase TRIB3 protein expression in granulosa cells, which in turn stabilizes β-catenin and enhances Cx43 levels. While Cx43 is essential for gap junction communication and oocyte development, TRIB3 overexpression paradoxically reduces granulosa cell viability and proliferation while increasing apoptosis, ultimately impairing oocyte maturation rates. Women with elevated BMI exhibit higher serum free fatty acids, elevated TRIB3 and Cx43 expression in granulosa cells, and significantly reduced fertilization rates and top-quality embryo production. These findings suggest that obesity-induced elevation of free fatty acids drives a TRIB3-mediated dysregulation of gap junction homeostasis, resulting in impaired granulosa cell function and compromised reproductive outcomes.

title: Comprehensive analysis of tRNA-derived fragment expression in endometriosis using PANDORA-seq technology
summary:
# Summary

This study used PANDORA-seq technology to comprehensively analyze tRNA-derived fragment (tRF) expression in endometriosis, revealing significant dysregulation of tRFs in both eutopic and ectopic endometrial tissues compared to normal controls. Notably, 1,374 tRFs were upregulated and 555 downregulated in ectopic tissue, while tRNA-Gly-GCC showed the highest expression in ectopic endometrial tissue. The differentially expressed tRFs regulate genes enriched in multiple endometriosis-related signaling pathways, including MAPK, Ras, p53, autophagy, and apoptotic pathways, as well as involvement in spliceosome and mitophagy processes. Key tRFs including tRF-32-I2VHJYOZ4E8B4 were consistently downregulated in both endometriosis tissue types, while others showed associations with human tumoral diseases. These findings establish tRFs as non-invasive biomarkers and highlight their role in endometriosis pathogenesis through multi-pathway dysregulation, supporting their potential as diagnostic and therapeutic targets.

title: Upregulation of miR-183 inhibits the invasion and migration of endometrial stromal cells in endometriosis patients by downregulating Ezrin
summary: Endometriosis pathogenesis involves dysregulation of microRNA-183 (miR-183), which is markedly reduced in ectopic endometrial tissues compared to normal tissue. miR-183 functions as a suppressor of endometrial stromal cell (ESC) invasiveness by directly targeting and downregulating Ezrin, a key component of the Rho/ROCK signaling pathway that controls cytoskeleton remodeling and cell motility. Overexpression of miR-183 inhibits ESC invasion, migration, and apoptosis while promoting cell adhesion through inactivation of the Rho/ROCK-Ezrin axis. Conversely, ectopic ESCs exhibit elevated Ezrin and RhoA expression, heightening their invasive and migratory capacity—pathological hallmarks that contribute to endometriosis symptoms including dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, and infertility. These findings identify miR-183-mediated suppression of the Rho/ROCK-Ezrin pathway as a critical regulatory mechanism in endometriosis, suggesting that restoring miR-183 expression could represent a therapeutic approach to inhibit pathological ESC behavior.

title: Expression analysis of plasma extracellular vesicle associated candidate MiRNAs in endometriosis using integrative bioinformatics and experiential data
summary: This integrative bioinformatics study identifies four candidate microRNAs (miR-451a, miR-23b, miR-148a, and miR-100) circulating in plasma-derived extracellular vesicles as significantly downregulated in endometriosis patients. These miRNAs suppress multiple pathogenic pathways: miR-451a inhibits macrophage migration inhibitory factor (MIF), which normally promotes endometriotic cell growth and angiogenesis; miR-23a/b target steroidogenic factor-1 (SF-1), reducing the expression of enzymes (stAR and CYP19A1) that drive local estrogen synthesis; miR-100 suppresses COX-2, which contributes to inflammation and proliferation; and miR-148a downregulates HLA-G and apoptotic regulators (BCL-2, caspase-3/9) while participating in estrogen-mediated signaling. The dysregulation of these miRNAs collectively promotes multiple hallmarks of endometriosis including lesion implantation, angiogenesis, cell proliferation, and cellular adhesion. These findings suggest that plasma EV-miRNA profiling could serve as a non-invasive biomarker panel for endometriosis diagnosis and potentially guide therapeutic interventions targeting miRNA restoration or their downstream pathways.

title: Cervical Cancer Biomarkers in Non-Cervical Samples: Emerging Tools for Diagnosis and Prognosis
summary:
# Summary

This review examines cervical cancer biomarkers detectable in non-cervical samples, particularly focusing on circulating miRNAs, long non-coding RNAs, and proteins. High-risk HPV genotypes 16 and 18 drive cervical cancer through viral proteins E6 and E7, which inhibit tumor suppressors p53 and Rb respectively. Multiple miRNA signatures have emerged as diagnostic and prognostic tools: elevated miRNAs (miR-1290, miR-146a-5p, miR-21-5p) mark cancer presence, while a urinary panel of miR-145-5p, miR-218-5p, and miR-34a-5p achieves 100% diagnostic sensitivity. Conversely, miR-497 and reduced miR-651 levels promote tumor suppression and chemotherapy sensitivity. Beyond miRNAs, long non-coding RNAs (CCAT2, LINC01133) and proteins (TNC, TPM3, CCT3) are elevated in cervical cancer, with several independently predicting survival outcomes. HPV-induced expression of complement regulatory proteins CD55 and CD59 facilitates chemoresistance, while HPV DNA and E7 circulating cell-free DNA in serum and urine provide non-invasive diagnostic markers. These circulating biomarkers represent emerging tools for cervical cancer detection and prognosis in accessible body fluids.

title: Potential MiRNA therapies for premature ovarian failure: new challenges and opportunities
summary:
# Summary

Premature ovarian failure (POF) is characterized by decreased ovarian function in women under 40, leading to amenorrhea, infertility, and systemic complications including osteoporosis and cardiovascular disease. The condition involves elevated follicle-stimulating hormone and reduced estradiol and anti-Müllerian hormone levels, driven by granulosa cell apoptosis and follicular atresia. MicroRNAs (miRNAs) show significant therapeutic potential for POF by preventing granulosa cell apoptosis, enhancing hormonal secretion, reducing oxidative stress, and promoting angiogenesis through multiple signaling pathways. Specific miRNAs such as miR-144-5p and miR-144-3p regulate apoptosis via the PI3K/AKT and p38 MAPK pathways, while miR-146a and miR-10a suppress pro-apoptotic signaling through NF-κB and Bim downregulation. Exosomal delivery of miRNAs from bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells and amniotic fluid stem cells effectively normalizes hormone levels and reduces granulosa cell apoptosis, offering a promising therapeutic approach that overcomes the immunogenicity and inflammatory limitations of viral vectors and synthetic delivery systems.

title: Exosome-mediated miRNA delivery: a molecular switch for reshaping neuropathic pain therapy
summary: Neuropathic pain is a chronic, refractory disorder arising from nerve injury that involves multiple interconnected pathological mechanisms, including peripheral and central sensitization, neuroinflammation, neuronal hyperexcitability, and impaired neural repair. MicroRNAs serve as pivotal post-transcriptional regulators in neuropathic pain pathogenesis, modulating these key pathologies through effects on neuroinflammation, neuronal excitability, and nerve repair processes. Exosomes have emerged as transformative nanovehicles for precise miRNA delivery due to their unique capacity to cross the blood-brain barrier, protect cargo from degradation, exhibit low immunogenicity, and achieve tissue-homing specificity. Exosome-mediated miRNA delivery effectively alleviates neuropathic pain by targeting core signaling pathways, including the TLR4/NF-κB axis, while specialized systems like RNAi-Tim3-Exo@SF hydrogel demonstrate improved motor function through Tim3 regulation of axonal regeneration, angiogenesis, and inflammatory microenvironment modulation. This approach represents a paradigm shift in neuropathic pain therapy by overcoming the bioavailability constraints that limit conventional treatments. The evidence suggests exosome-mediated miRNA delivery serves as a molecular switch capable of reshaping therapeutic outcomes for this challenging chronic pain condition.

title: Systematic review of extracellular vesicle-derived microRNAs involved in organ fibrosis: implications for arthrofibrosis therapy
summary:
# Summary

Extracellular vesicles (exosomes) serve as critical mediators of intercellular communication by delivering microRNAs that regulate multiple fibrosis-related pathways. The exosomal lipid membrane protects miRNAs from degradation, enabling them to modulate protein-encoding gene expression and control cellular processes including inflammation, differentiation, extracellular matrix composition, and apoptosis through binding to messenger RNAs. Anti-fibrotic miRNAs such as let-7, miR-26a, miR-29, and miR-146a suppress myofibroblast differentiation and fibrotic markers by inhibiting key signaling pathways including TGF-β/Smad, CTGF, and NF-κB, while pro-fibrotic miR-21 and miR-192 promote fibrosis through activation of fibroblasts and upregulation of collagen and α-SMA. Stem cell-derived exosomes enriched in anti-fibrotic miRNAs demonstrate therapeutic potential across multiple organ systems, alleviating fibrosis, inflammation, and associated dysfunction by targeting fibrogenic transcription factors and signaling cascades. These findings suggest that exosomal miRNA delivery represents a promising therapeutic strategy for organ fibrosis, with particular relevance for endometrial fibrosis and adhesion formation where dysregulated miRNA expression contributes to pathogenesis.

title: Decidual stromal cells-derived exosomes incured insufficient migration and invasion of trophoblast because of abnormal ubiquitination and degradation of Snail mediated by miR-92b-3p/USP28
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a critical mechanism by which exosomes derived from decidual stromal cells (DSCs) in unexplained recurrent spontaneous abortion (URSA) impair trophoblast function and compromise pregnancy success. URSA-DSC-derived exosomes transfer elevated miR-92b-3p into trophoblasts, where it directly targets and downregulates USP28, a deubiquitinase essential for stabilizing the transcription factor Snail. The suppression of USP28 leads to abnormal ubiquitination and degradation of Snail, thereby inhibiting epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and consequently reducing trophoblast migration and invasion capacity. In vivo experiments demonstrated that URSA-DSC-exosomes promote embryo absorption in mice, linking the impaired trophoblast function to pregnancy failure. These findings establish that disrupted miR-92b-3p/USP28/Snail signaling represents a novel pathogenic pathway in URSA, offering potential therapeutic targets for restoring adequate trophoblast-decidual communication at the maternal-fetal interface.

title: Molecular function validation and prognostic value analysis of the cuproptosis-related gene ferredoxin 1 in papillary thyroid carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies ferredoxin 1 (FDX1) as a critical regulator of cuproptosis, a copper-dependent cell death mechanism, in papillary thyroid carcinoma (PTC). FDX1 expression is significantly reduced in PTC tissues and tumor cells compared to normal thyroid tissue, and lower FDX1 levels correlate strongly with tumor recurrence, extrathyroidal extension, and poor prognosis. FDX1 functions as an upstream regulator of protein lipoylation and cuproptosis signaling; its overexpression suppresses cell proliferation and invasion while promoting cell death, partly through modulation of epithelial-mesenchymal transition markers and downregulation of the DNA repair gene RAD51. The study identified a six-gene cuproptosis-related signature (HIGD1A, SPCS2, PPIL1, CLCNKB, FHL1, and IQGAP3) that predicts PTC prognosis, with higher signature scores associated with elevated immunosuppressive cells, immune checkpoint expression, and activation of oncogenic pathways. These findings establish cuproptosis dysregulation as a mechanism driving PTC progression and suggest FDX1-based prognostic models could improve clinical risk stratification and guide therapeutic strategies targeting this pathway.

title: MiR-499a-5p Promotes the Proliferation and Migration of Uterine Leiomyoma Cells by Activating the Wnt/β-Catenin Signaling Pathway
summary:
# Summary

MiR-499a-5p is significantly elevated in uterine leiomyoma (ULM) tissues and cells, where it promotes tumor cell proliferation and migration while suppressing apoptosis through activation of the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway. Knockdown of miR-499a-5p inhibits cell proliferation, induces apoptosis, and causes G0/G1 cell cycle arrest, accompanied by reduced expression of pathway effectors including β-catenin, Cyclin D1, and c-myc. Conversely, miR-499a-5p overexpression enhances proliferation, promotes S-phase entry, and upregulates these same downstream targets, demonstrating the oncogenic role of this microRNA in ULM pathogenesis. These findings establish miR-499a-5p as a key regulatory molecule in uterine leiomyoma development operating through canonical Wnt/β-catenin signaling, suggesting it may represent a therapeutic target for this hormone-dependent smooth muscle tumor.

title: miRNA in Endometriosis—A New Hope or an Illusion?
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth that causes infertility and chronic pelvic pain in women of reproductive age, with significant impacts on mental health and economic activity. MicroRNAs (miRNAs) have emerged as key regulators in endometriosis pathogenesis, controlling inflammation, cell proliferation, angiogenesis, and extracellular matrix remodeling through modulation of critical genes including estrogen receptors (ERα/β), KRAS, CYP19, IL-6, and HOXA genes. Multiple miRNAs show consistent dysregulation in endometriosis across studies, with miR-125b-5p and miR-451a typically upregulated while miR-3613-5p, let-7b, and miR-143-3p are downregulated, positioning these molecules as potential non-invasive biomarkers for diagnosis. MiRNAs regulate endometriosis development through diverse mechanisms including translation inhibition, chromatin modifications, and modulation of signaling pathways such as mTOR and VEGF, which control cell growth and migration. However, miRNA expression patterns are significantly influenced by menstrual cycle phase, estrogen and progesterone levels, and genetic, environmental, and lifestyle factors, which complicate the establishment of standardized diagnostic panels. While miRNAs represent promising candidates for developing non-invasive diagnostic and therapeutic tools to replace invasive laparoscopy, careful standardization and multi-ethnic validation studies are essential to translate these findings into clinical practice.

title: The Prognosis of Endometriosis Correlates With Elevated Expression of LncRNA-ANRIL
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a nonmalignant gynecological condition characterized by cell invasion, metastasis, and recurrence, presenting clinically with heavy menstrual bleeding, dysmenorrhea, infertility, and dyspareunia. This study identifies elevated expression of the long noncoding RNA ANRIL in endometriotic tissues, with expression levels correlating positively with disease stage and severity, being particularly elevated in ectopic lesions compared to eutopic endometrium. ANRIL promotes endometriosis pathogenesis through multiple mechanisms, including activation of inflammatory pathways via NF-κB and STAT1, facilitation of epithelial-mesenchymal transition through TGF-β and Wnt/β-catenin dysregulation, and promotion of cell invasion and apoptosis resistance. ANRIL knockdown experiments demonstrated markedly reduced invasive capacity of endometrial cells, establishing ANRIL as essential for cell invasion in endometriosis. These findings suggest that ANRIL may serve as a prognostic biomarker for endometriosis progression and could represent a potential therapeutic target, particularly relevant given the high recurrence rates associated with current hormone therapy and surgical treatments.

title: Tcf21 modulates fibroblast activation and promotes cardiac fibrosis after injury via Pdgfrb signaling
summary:
# Summary

Tcf21, a basic helix-loop-helix transcription factor essential for cardiac fibroblast development, directly promotes cardiac fibrosis by regulating fibroblast activation and extracellular matrix production through Pdgfrb signaling. Chromatin immunoprecipitation-sequencing analysis revealed that Tcf21 directly binds to pro-fibrotic genes including COL1A1, COL3A1, IL6, and PDGFRB, with transactivation potential for collagen genes while regulating Pdgfrb expression indirectly. The Pdgfrb-Erk signaling pathway is critical for Tcf21-mediated fibroblast proliferation, as Tcf21 deletion attenuates Erk phosphorylation and reduces fibroblast proliferation capacity. Inducible Tcf21 knockout mice were remarkably resistant to isoproterenol-induced cardiac fibrosis, exhibiting reduced fibrosis area, decreased fibroblast proliferation, and suppressed expression of myofibroblast markers and collagen genes. These findings establish Tcf21 as a key transcriptional regulator controlling the transformation of cardiac fibroblasts into activated myofibroblasts, positioning it as a potential therapeutic target for cardiac fibrosis management.

title: Role of extracellular vesicle-carried ncRNAs in the interactive ‘dialogue’ within the brain and beyond: emerging theranostic epigenetic modifiers in brain-derived nanoplatforms
summary:
# Summary

Extracellular vesicles (EVs), particularly exosomes, mediate critical intercellular communication in the central nervous system by serving as carriers of non-coding RNAs (ncRNAs) including microRNAs, long non-coding RNAs, and circular RNAs that regulate gene expression post-transcriptionally. Under physiological conditions, EVs maintain CNS homeostasis through coordinated delivery of their cargo, while in response to injury they facilitate tissue repair and modulate immune responses, with the capacity to cross the blood-brain barrier and influence peripheral immunity. The selective packaging of ncRNAs into EVs involves sophisticated sorting mechanisms mediated by proteins such as tetraspanins, hnRNPs, and AGO2, with m6A methylation enhancing ncRNA recognition by YTHDF family proteins to promote incorporation into exosomes. EVs also display cell-type-specific protein markers that reflect their cellular origin—neuronal-derived EVs contain GAP43 and neuroligin-3, astrocyte-derived EVs contain GLAST, and microglia-derived EVs contain TMEM119—enabling their use as biomarkers and therapeutic vehicles. Dysregulation of EV-mediated communication contributes to neurodegeneration, as exemplified by elevated levels of phosphorylated tau, amyloid-beta, and neurogranin in neuronal-derived EVs from Alzheimer's disease patients, suggesting EVs serve as promising theranostic tools for both diagnostics and epigenetic-based therapeutics in neurological disease.

title: Exosomal miR-218 secreted from endothelial progenitor cells mitigates acute lung injury in sepsis mice by inhibiting HMGA1 in alveolar macrophages
summary:
# Article Summary

This study demonstrates that exosomal miR-218 secreted from endothelial progenitor cells (EPCs) mitigates sepsis-induced acute lung injury in mice through a miR-218/HMGA1 regulatory axis. Exosomes carrying miR-218 accumulate in lung tissue and are taken up by alveolar macrophages, where miR-218 directly binds to and downregulates HMGA1, a transcriptional regulator that promotes pro-inflammatory macrophage polarization via NF-κB-CCL2 signaling. The protective effects of miR-218 were confirmed by demonstrating that miR-218 mimics suppressed M1 macrophage polarization while miR-218 inhibitors enhanced it, and that HMGA1 overexpression reversed the beneficial effects of miR-218. Additionally, EPC-derived exosomes broadly reduced pro-inflammatory cytokine expression including TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β, while also alleviating pulmonary edema, capillary leakage, and inflammatory cell infiltration. These findings suggest that EPC-derived exosomal miR-218 represents a promising therapeutic approach for sepsis-related lung injury through immunomodulation of macrophage function.

title: Unraveling the regulatory mechanisms of fasting-induced molting on follicle development in laying hens
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how fasting-induced molting (FIM) regulates follicle development in laying hens by enhancing laying rate and extending the laying cycle through coordinated molecular mechanisms. FIM activates the PI3K-AKT signaling pathway, which is central to promoting primordial follicle recruitment and activation while reducing ovarian collagen deposition. Collagen type I alpha 1 (COL1A1) emerged as a key regulatory factor that promotes ovarian cell proliferation and follicle development by activating PI3K-AKT signaling and upregulating growth factors GDF9 and BMP4, alongside increasing expression of proliferation markers PCNA and CDK2. During fasting, levels of reproductive hormones FSH, LH, and AMH decreased significantly, with the reduction in AMH particularly important for relieving inhibition of primordial follicle activation. Multiple extracellular matrix proteins including fibronectin, various collagen subtypes, and integrins form an interconnected regulatory network supporting follicle development. Upon refeeding, collagen fiber content rebounded and egg production recovered to exceed pre-fasting levels by day 33, demonstrating the reversibility of FIM-induced ovarian remodeling and the critical role of metabolic state in controlling ovarian function.

title: Emerging roles of tsRNAs in programmed cell death and disease therapeutics: challenges, opportunities, and future directions
summary:
# Summary

tRNA-derived small RNAs (tsRNAs) have emerged as critical regulators of programmed cell death, with diverse roles in disease pathogenesis and therapeutic potential. These molecules are generated through cleavage of precursor or mature tRNAs by specific nucleases including ELAC2, RNase Z, RNase L, Dicer, and angiogenin, and they regulate multiple biological processes including gene expression, translational control, and epigenetic modification. Individual tsRNAs demonstrate opposing effects depending on context: tsRNA-3043a promotes apoptosis in ovarian granulosa cells via FLT1 targeting and contributes to premature ovarian insufficiency, while tsRNA-04002 inhibits apoptosis through PRKCA targeting to delay intervertebral disc degeneration. Beyond apoptosis, tsRNAs regulate distinct forms of programmed cell death including autophagy, necroptosis, pyroptosis, and ferroptosis, with tsRNA-3029b inhibiting ferroptosis to alleviate depression and tRF-22-8BWS7K092 activating the Hippo pathway to worsen acute lung injury. These molecular regulators show promise as biomarkers for early disease detection and therapeutic targets across multiple conditions including cancer, cardiovascular disease, neurodegenerative disorders, and inflammatory diseases.

title: Asynchrony Between Endometrial miRNA- and mRNA-Based Receptivity Stages Associated with Impaired Receptivity in Recurrent Implantation Failure
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that asynchronous timing between microRNA and mRNA expression patterns in the endometrium is associated with impaired receptivity and reduced pregnancy outcomes in patients with recurrent implantation failure. Using complementary diagnostic tools—the endometrial receptivity array (ERA) for mRNA profiling and microRNA-based assessment (MIRA)—researchers identified three patient groups: those with synchronized expression ("Equal" group), advanced miRNA expression ("Fast" group), and delayed miRNA expression ("Slow" group). Patients in the Slow group, characterized by lagged miRNA profiles relative to mRNA, exhibited significantly lower pregnancy rates (54.5%) compared to synchronized groups, higher prior implantation failure counts, and altered expression of 15 miRNAs predominantly involved in aging-related pathways. Key miRNAs regulating endometrial receptivity include the let-7 family, miR-30 family, miR-145-5p, and miR-222-3p, which control critical processes through targets like leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF) and CDKN1C/p57kip2 that govern decidualization and embryo attachment. The findings suggest that precise temporal coordination between miRNA and mRNA expression is essential for successful implantation, and that miRNA-based assessment provides diagnostic value beyond traditional mRNA profiling alone in identifying receptivity defects.

title: MicroRNAs in long COVID: roles, diagnostic biomarker potential and detection
summary:
# Summary: MicroRNAs in Long COVID

MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are small non-coding regulatory molecules that control post-transcriptional gene expression by modulating approximately 8,000 genes, functioning as key players in epigenetic regulation. During SARS-CoV-2 infection and Long COVID, dysregulated miRNA expression patterns contribute to pathogenesis through multiple mechanisms, including altered production of pro-inflammatory cytokines like IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α, which drive the chronic inflammation and immune dysregulation characteristic of Long COVID. Specific miRNA signatures are implicated in disease severity and organ-system complications: miR-155, miR-146a, and miR-21 are induced following toll-like receptor activation and promote excessive inflammatory responses in severe COVID-19, while miR-200c and miR-200 family members are elevated and suppress ACE2 expression. Conversely, miR-29 family members and miR-21 directly target and inhibit the SARS-CoV-2 genome to restrict viral replication. The therapeutic potential of miRNA modulation is demonstrated by tocilizumab, which reduces miR-21 expression and provides cardioprotective effects in COVID-19-related myocarditis. Advanced sequencing technologies such as Oxford Nanopore sequencing enable comprehensive miRNA profiling to uncover novel regulatory interactions, positioning dysregulated miRNAs as promising biomarkers for Long COVID diagnosis and therapeutic intervention.

title: MicroRNA participates in embryo implantation by modulating endometrial tolerance in sows during peri-implantation period
summary:
# Summary
