Specific circRNAs demonstrate opposing roles: circ_0007534 promotes chemoresistance and cancer progression via the miR-625-ZEB2 axis, while CDR1as reduces chemoresistance by inhibiting miR-1299 and enhancing cisplatin efficacy, and Circ-Foxo3 inhibits cell cycle progression by interacting with CDK2 and p21. CircRNAs are transported in exosomes between cells, mediating chemoresistance and remodeling the tumor microenvironment. CircRNA expression profiles significantly differ between endometrial cancer and healthy tissue, with certain circRNAs like RP11-255H23.4 and HSPG2 downregulated in tumors, making them valuable biomarkers associated with TNM staging and metastatic potential. These findings position circRNAs as promising therapeutic targets for developing targeted strategies to overcome chemoresistance in endometrial cancer.

title: Dynamic alternative polyadenylation in ovaries of Muscovy ducks across different laying stages based on full-length transcriptomics
summary:
# Summary

This study examined alternative polyadenylation (APA) dynamics in duck ovaries across different laying stages using full-length transcriptomics and identified stage-specific APA as a critical post-transcriptional regulatory mechanism controlling follicular development and atresia. Differentially used APA sites enriched pathways governing cell cycle regulation, ubiquitin-mediated proteasomal degradation, and energy metabolism, with stage-specific APA events particularly influencing lipid and purine metabolism. Hub miRNAs including gga-miR-1770_L-2R-1_1ss4CA and cgr-miR-149-5p_R+3 were integrated into mRNA-miRNA networks regulating follicular dynamics, while specific factors like LIF promoted granulosa cell apoptosis and inhibited cell cycle progression. Core polyadenylation factors including ZC3H3 and CPSF7 were upregulated to modulate APA regulation, and alterations in 3' UTR length mediated by APA affected miRNA binding site availability. The findings demonstrate that APA-mediated control of mRNA stability and translation represents a fundamental mechanism governing ovarian follicle selection and fertility, with implications for understanding metabolic dysfunction associated with reproductive disorders including endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome.

title: Enhancing Osteogenesis in Dental Implants: ZNF710-AS1 and miR-146a-5p in Smad Signaling Pathway Activation
journal: International Journal of Molecular and Cellular Medicine
summary:
# Summary

This study elucidates a competing endogenous RNA (ceRNA) mechanism whereby the long non-coding RNA ZNF710-AS1 promotes osteogenic differentiation in periodontal ligament stem cells by sequestering miR-146a-5p and miR-146b-5p, thereby preventing their inhibition of BMP6 and subsequent activation of the Smad signaling pathway. ZNF710-AS1 acts as a molecular sponge that titrates away these microRNAs from their endogenous targets, allowing increased BMP6 expression and downstream osteogenic gene activation. Conversely, when miR-146a-5p and miR-146b-5p are overexpressed, they directly inhibit ZNF710-AS1 and target BMP6 mRNA, suppressing the osteogenic differentiation program. Although miR-146a-5p exhibits context-dependent roles—promoting osteo/odontogenic differentiation in some dental stem cells while inhibiting it in bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells—its anti-inflammatory effects through TRAF6/NF-κB pathway inhibition are consistently beneficial for peri-implant osteogenesis and dental implant stability. These findings highlight the intricate regulatory network among non-coding RNAs in controlling bone formation and provide potential therapeutic targets for enhancing osseointegration in dental implant applications.

title: Placental mesenchymal stem cell exosomes drive macrophage M2 polarization via the miR-146a-5p/TRAF6 axis to ameliorate preeclampsia
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that placental mesenchymal stem cell (PMSC)-derived exosomes ameliorate preeclampsia (PE) through delivery of miR-146a-5p, which suppresses the TRAF6/NF-κB inflammatory axis and promotes anti-inflammatory macrophage M2 polarization. PE-derived PMSCs exhibit functional impairment characterized by reduced proliferation, migration, and angiogenic capacity along with substantially decreased miR-146a-5p expression. PMSC-derived exosomes carrying miR-146a-5p directly downregulate TRAF6 and phosphorylated NF-κB, thereby reducing pro-inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-α) while enhancing anti-inflammatory signaling (IL-10) and shifting macrophage polarization from the pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype toward the reparative M2 phenotype. In a mouse PE model, exosome treatment reversed key pathological features including elevated blood pressure, proteinuria, fetal growth restriction, and trophoblast apoptosis while restoring angiogenic factor expression (VEGF and PIGF). These findings establish miR-146a-5p-mediated immune modulation as a critical therapeutic mechanism and suggest PMSC-derived exosomes as a promising cell-free therapeutic strategy for PE by restoring placental homeostasis through macrophage reprogramming.

title: Prediction of microRNAs affecting the syncytin-1 (HERV-W) and syncytin-2 (HERV-FRD) genes regulation in endometriosis and miscarriage
summary: This study identifies microRNA-mediated regulation of syncytin genes as a critical mechanism linking endometriosis and miscarriage. Specifically, miR-509-3p and miR-625-5p were identified as key regulators of Syncytin-1 (HERV-W) and Syncytin-2 (HERV-FRD), which are essential fusion proteins expressed in placental trophoblasts and critical for placental development. Dysregulation of these miRNAs in endometriotic tissue leads to aberrant expression patterns that promote abnormal cell proliferation, migration, and invasion through alterations in epithelial-mesenchymal transition and angiogenesis. Reduced Syncytin-1 expression impairs trophoblast invasion and fusion, processes tightly regulated by decidualization and EMT, thereby contributing to both pregnancy complications and endometriosis pathology. The study demonstrates that miRNA dysregulation represents a common molecular pathogenic mechanism underlying both diseases, with multiple miRNA families (including miR-145, miR-200, and miR-17-5p/20a) participating in endometrial remodeling and trophoblast dysfunction. These findings suggest that targeting miRNA-mediated Syncytin dysregulation could represent a therapeutic approach for managing both endometriosis and miscarriage risk.

title: Epigenetic mechanisms in maternal-fetal crosstalk: inter- and trans-generational inheritance
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the epigenetic mechanisms governing maternal-fetal communication and their trans-generational inheritance, with particular emphasis on extracellular vesicles and microRNAs as key mediators. DNA methylation, histone post-translational modifications, and non-coding RNAs regulate gene expression throughout embryonic development, with TET enzymes and DNMTs coordinately managing the distinct methylation patterns of paternal and maternal genomes following fertilization. During early development, the let-7 miRNA family and other miRNAs are transferred via maternal extracellular vesicles to suppress oncogenic pathways (c-myc, mTORC1/2) and inhibit nutrient transporters, collectively promoting blastocyst dormancy through mTOR inhibition while preserving pluripotency. The establishment of receptive endometrium involves coordinated secretion of cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, IL-10, TNF-α), growth factors (VEGF, G-CSF, LIF), and embryo-derived extracellular vesicles that collectively promote trophoblast migration, invasion, spiral artery transformation, and angiogenesis essential for successful implantation. These findings underscore how epigenetic regulation and intercellular communication through extracellular vesicles orchestrate the critical maternal-fetal crosstalk necessary for proper embryonic development and implantation.

title: Targeting cancer via macrophage-derived exosomal miRNAs: implications for tumor progression and resistance
summary:
# Summary

Macrophage-derived exosomes (MDEs) function as critical mediators of tumor progression through selective packaging and delivery of microRNAs that regulate cancer cell behavior. M1-polarized macrophages preferentially package anti-tumor miRNAs like miR-155-5p and miR-let-7a-5p, which inhibit cancer cell growth and invasion by downregulating oncogenic targets such as BCL2L1 and activating pro-apoptotic pathways. Conversely, M2 tumor-associated macrophages enrich exosomes with oncogenic miRNAs including miR-501-3p, miR-221-3p, and miR-29a-3p that promote tumor growth, invasion, angiogenesis, and immune evasion by triggering the TGF-β cascade and expanding regulatory T cell populations. The selective sorting of miRNAs into exosomes involves multiple mechanisms including recognition by RNA-binding proteins like hnRNPA2B1 and Ago2, ESCRT machinery-mediated cargo packaging, and modulation by environmental factors such as hypoxia and inflammatory cytokines like TNF-α and IL-6. MDEs reshape the tumor microenvironment by promoting extracellular matrix remodeling, angiogenesis, and metastatic capacity, establishing them as promising therapeutic targets for cancer intervention through manipulation of exosomal miRNA cargo.

title: Extracellular vesicles in reproductive medicine: from “animal-led” to “plant-enabled”
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

Extracellular vesicles (EVs) function as critical mediators of intercellular communication throughout reproductive physiology, regulating follicular development, embryo implantation, gametogenesis, fertilization, and maternal-fetal immune tolerance through delivery of bioactive cargo including microRNAs. Specific EV subtypes exhibit specialized roles: oocyte-derived EVs deliver miR-205 to suppress SOCS2 and maintain follicular integrity, cumulus cell-derived EVs transfer miR-224 to promote oocyte maturation via Smad4 targeting, and oviductal EVs enhance embryonic genome activation and cell lineage specification. Dysregulation of EV biogenesis and aberrant miRNA profiles contribute to reproductive pathologies including polycystic ovary syndrome, endometriosis, premature ovarian failure, and recurrent miscarriage, with PCOS pathogenesis particularly involving S100-A9–mediated inflammation and dysregulated steroid synthesis. Plant-derived EVs represent an emerging therapeutic approach to treat infertility, reflecting a paradigm shift toward green precision medicine, while MSC-derived EVs show promise in restoring normal granulosa cell function through miR-323-3p delivery to enhance steroidogenesis and suppress apoptosis.

title: Circular RNA FTO functions as a hsa-miR-141-3p sponge to regulate the growth and migration abilities of human retinal endothelial cells via up-regulating ZEB1
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that circular RNA FTO (circFTO) promotes proliferative diabetic retinopathy (PDR) through a miRNA-mediated regulatory axis in human retinal endothelial cells. CircFTO is significantly elevated in PDR patients and hyperglycemia-treated cells, where it functions as a competitive "sponge" that binds and sequesters hsa-miR-141-3p, preventing this miRNA from targeting its downstream effector ZEB1. By reducing hsa-miR-141-3p availability, circFTO allows increased ZEB1 expression, which promotes epithelial-mesenchymal transition and drives retinal endothelial cell proliferation, migration, and tube formation. Conversely, circFTO knockdown suppresses these pathological processes by restoring hsa-miR-141-3p activity, leading to ZEB1 downregulation and increased E-cadherin expression. These findings identify the circFTO/hsa-miR-141-3p/ZEB1 regulatory axis as a critical mechanism underlying PDR pathogenesis and suggest circFTO as a potential biomarker and therapeutic target for managing this severe diabetic complication.

title: ESR1 Regulates Fecundity and Functions in Sheep Endometrial Stromal Cells
summary:
# Summary

ESR1, encoding estrogen receptor alpha (ERα), is highly expressed in sheep endometrium and is significantly upregulated in high-fecundity animals, where it localizes to endometrial stromal cells and glandular epithelium. ESR1 plays critical roles in regulating endometrial stromal cell proliferation, apoptosis, and migration—processes essential for embryo implantation, placentation, and reproductive success. ESR1 knockdown inhibits cell proliferation by reducing PCNA expression and suppresses migration while simultaneously triggering apoptosis through upregulation of caspase and p53/p21 signaling pathways, alongside G0/G1-phase cell cycle arrest via downregulation of cyclin-CDK complexes. These findings demonstrate that ESR1 functions as a pivotal regulator of endometrial stromal cell fate, linking estrogen signaling to the cellular processes underlying uterine receptivity and fecundity in sheep. The study establishes ESR1 as a key molecular determinant of reproductive competence through its control of stromal cell dynamics that support successful embryo development.

title: Shaoyao gancao decoction induces autophagy by modulating miR-21 and inhibiting the AKT/mTOR signaling pathway in adenomyosis-derived ectopic endometrial stromal cells
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that Shaoyao Gancao Decoction (SGD), a traditional Chinese herbal formula, treats adenomyosis by reversing pathological signaling in ectopic endometrial stromal cells. In adenomyotic lesions, elevated miR-21 expression suppresses PTEN, which normally inhibits the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway, resulting in aberrant activation of this pro-proliferative cascade and impaired autophagy. Treatment with SGD downregulates miR-21 and its downstream targets (phosphorylated AKT and mTOR), while simultaneously upregulating PTEN and autophagy-related proteins. Through these coordinated molecular changes, SGD restores the negative regulation of PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling and promotes autophagy-mediated degradation of ectopic endometrial cells, thereby suppressing their proliferation and promoting apoptosis. These findings reveal a novel therapeutic mechanism for SGD targeting the miR-21/PTEN/PI3K/AKT/mTOR axis in adenomyosis pathogenesis.

title: Expanding Horizons: Next‐Generation and Interdisciplinary Advances in the Applications of Extracellular Vesicles
summary:
# Summary: Extracellular Vesicles in Biomedical Applications

Extracellular vesicles (EVs), including exosomes, function as critical mediators of intercellular communication across diverse biological systems by delivering bioactive cargo such as microRNAs, cytokines, and proteins. Beyond their fundamental role in cellular communication, EVs from various microbial sources—bacterial, fungal, and archaeal—participate in pathogenic processes including biofilm formation, antibiotic resistance, horizontal gene transfer, and immune evasion, while also contributing to tissue damage in autoimmune diseases like systemic lupus erythematosus and systemic sclerosis. Conversely, mesenchymal stem cell-derived EVs (MSC-EVs) demonstrate significant therapeutic potential by suppressing T-cell activation and autoantibody production, thereby reducing symptoms in SLE, vitiligo, systemic sclerosis, and psoriasis through anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory mechanisms. Additionally, EVs from specialized sources including adipose-derived stem cells, bone marrow-derived stem cells, and probiotic bacteria such as Lactobacillus species promote wound healing and attenuate atopic dermatitis by stimulating fibroblast and endothelial cell proliferation while suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokine production. These findings establish extracellular vesicles as dual-natured entities with both pathogenic roles in infectious and autoimmune diseases and significant therapeutic applications in regenerative medicine and inflammatory skin conditions.

title: Potential association of certain microRNA gene polymorphisms with recurrent pregnancy loss susceptibility in Saudi women
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated the association between microRNA gene polymorphisms and recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL) susceptibility in Saudi women. The heterozygous genotypes of miR-146a-C>G and miR-149-T>C polymorphisms were significantly associated with increased RPL risk (odds ratios of 2.29 and 2.67, respectively), while the miR-10A A>T polymorphism showed no genetic variation in the study population. MicroRNAs regulate critical reproductive processes including oogenesis, embryo implantation, follicular cell proliferation, and oocyte maturation through post-transcriptional control of target genes such as FAS, FOXL2, CCND2, AKT1, and E2F1, which influence cell survival, apoptosis, and cell cycle progression. The miR-146a variant affects immune-inflammatory pathways via NF-κB signaling and FAS regulation, while miR-149 functions as a pro-apoptotic miRNA by downregulating AKT1 and E2F1, and miR-323b dysregulation reduces expression of Pax8, a transcription factor essential for embryo development. These findings suggest that specific miRNA polymorphisms contribute to RPL susceptibility through disrupted regulation of genes governing reproductive and developmental processes.

title: piR-hsa-022095 Drives Hypertrophic Scar Formation via KLF11-Dependent Fibroblast Proliferation
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies piR-hsa-022095 as a key driver of hypertrophic scar formation through a novel regulatory axis involving the transcription factor KLF11. Sequencing analysis revealed that piR-hsa-022095 is significantly upregulated in hypertrophic scars, and functional studies demonstrate that this piRNA promotes fibroblast proliferation, migration, and invasion while suppressing apoptosis. Mechanistically, piR-hsa-022095 directly targets the 3' UTR of KLF11 mRNA to repress its expression, thereby removing KLF11-mediated inhibition of pro-proliferative genes such as SAMD7 and c-MYC within the TGF-β signaling pathway. Conversely, piR-hsa-022095 inhibition induces G0/G1 cell-cycle arrest, reduces fibroblast viability, and promotes apoptosis—effects that can be partially reversed by KLF11 knockdown. The identification of the piR-hsa-022095–KLF11 axis reveals a previously unrecognized regulatory pathway in hypertrophic scar pathogenesis and suggests that targeting this piRNA-mediated mechanism could represent a therapeutic strategy for fibrotic skin conditions.

title: Expression of New Gene Markers Regulating Protein Metabolism in Porcine Ovarian Granulosa Cells In Vitro
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates gene expression patterns regulating protein metabolism in porcine ovarian granulosa cells cultured in vitro over 144 hours. The research identifies distinct temporal expression patterns, with genes such as LOX, FN1, and ANKRD1 consistently upregulated across all time points, supporting extracellular matrix cross-linking, cellular adhesion, and differentiation processes essential for granulosa cell function. Conversely, GARNL3 and ARRDC4 remain downregulated throughout, reducing cell signaling capacity. The data reveal that follicle-stimulating hormone activates FSH receptors to promote granulosa cell proliferation and differentiation, while the MAPK and TGF-β signaling pathways emerge as central regulators of multiple cellular processes including proliferation, apoptosis, and granulosa cell-oocyte interactions. Proteoglycans, particularly decorin and perlecan, modulate growth factor activity by binding FGF and TGF-β, thereby influencing follicle dynamics and cumulus-oocyte complex organization. Additionally, specialized genes including SERPINB2, CHI3L1, and SLC1A1 support granulosa cell survival, tissue repair, and amino acid metabolism, collectively enhancing oocyte competence and maturation during in vitro culture adaptation.

title: Potential function of miRNA as a diagnostic biomarker for human ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are emerging as important diagnostic biomarkers for ovarian cancer, functioning as non-coding regulators at the post-transcriptional level to control gene expression and cancer progression. While the traditional marker CA-125 is associated with ovarian cancer stage, it lacks specificity as it is elevated in multiple malignancies and benign gynecological conditions including endometriosis, adenomyosis, and uterine fibroids. Distinct miRNA expression signatures characterize epithelial ovarian cancer tissues, with specific miRNAs such as miR-141, miR-200a/b/c, miR-182-5p, miR-183-5p, and miR-96-5p upregulated, while miR-125b-1, miR-140, miR-145, miR-199a, and miR-1271-5p are downregulated compared to healthy controls. Multiple cancer-suppressive miRNAs have been identified that inhibit ovarian cancer progression through direct targeting of key oncogenic proteins: miR-34a suppresses HDAC1 and reduces cisplatin resistance, miR-199b-3p and miR-205 target ZEB1 to prevent epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and miRNAs including miR-613, miR-125b-5p, and miR-193a-5p suppress cell proliferation and metastasis by regulating KRAS, CD147, and RAB11A respectively. These specific miRNA signatures hold promise for improving early detection and monitoring of ovarian cancer while potentially guiding therapeutic strategies.

title: 细胞外囊泡在早期胚胎发育中的作用及应用于辅助生殖技术
summary:
# Summary

Extracellular vesicles (EVs), including exosomes, microvesicles, and apoptotic bodies, serve as critical mediators of intercellular communication during early embryonic development and reproductive processes by transporting proteins, nucleic acids, and lipids that regulate gene expression and cellular signaling. EVs derived from different female reproductive tissues exhibit tissue-specific functions: follicular EVs promote oocyte maturation and regulate epigenetic modifications like DNA methylation through miRNAs such as bta-miR-631 and miR-34c, while fallopian tube EVs regulate sperm function through calcium homeostasis proteins (PMCA4) and modulate embryonic development by upregulating pluripotency genes (POU5F1, SOX2, NANOG) and metabolic enzymes. Uterine EVs support embryo quality and pregnancy recognition through the delivery of interferon-tau (IFNΤ), a key pregnancy signal that maintains corpus luteum function, while simultaneously suppressing apoptosis-related genes and endoplasmic reticulum stress markers. These EVs also deliver miRNAs including miR-17-5p, miR-148b, and let-7 that modulate critical signaling pathways (PI3K/Akt, TGF-β, mTORC1/2) controlling cell proliferation, differentiation, and embryonic competence. The functional relevance of reproductive tract EVs suggests their therapeutic potential for improving embryo quality in assisted reproductive technologies through supplementation strategies.

title: Transcriptomic analysis identifies CXCL12 as a novel candidate gene for litter size in rabbits
summary:
# Summary

Transcriptomic analysis of rabbit ovarian tissue identified CXCL12 as a novel candidate gene associated with litter size, with higher expression levels in rabbits with larger litters. CXCL12 promotes granulosa cell proliferation and inhibits apoptosis by binding to the CXCR4 receptor and activating the JAK/STAT signaling pathway, while simultaneously upregulating cell cycle-promoting genes (PCNA, CCND1, CDK2) and anti-apoptotic factors (Bcl-2) while downregulating pro-apoptotic Bax. Functional studies demonstrated that overexpressing CXCL12 significantly increased granulosa cell proliferation rates at 24 and 48 hours, whereas knockdown experiments showed the opposite effect, confirming CXCL12's central role in regulating ovarian granulosa cell dynamics. Beyond fertility, CXCL12 also regulates expression of reproductive development genes including TAF4B, CITED1, WNT2, WNT10B, and HSD17B1, and plays broader roles in oocyte maturation, cumulus expansion, and oxidative stress protection. These findings establish CXCL12 as a promising molecular marker for selective breeding programs aimed at improving reproductive productivity in rabbits.

title: Exploring exosomal lncRNAs: unlocking the molecular code of tumor metastasis and drug resistance
summary: Exosomal long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) emerge as critical mediators of tumor metastasis and drug resistance through multiple molecular mechanisms. LncRNAs regulate gene expression at both transcriptional and post-transcriptional levels, controlling cell proliferation, differentiation, and apoptosis, while functioning as competing endogenous RNAs that sequester microRNAs from their targets. Specific exosomal lncRNAs promote metastasis via distinct pathways: HCG18 activates PI3K/Akt signaling and pseudopodia formation, USP30-AS1 stimulates epithelial-mesenchymal transition through Wnt/β-catenin activation, and MEG3 upregulates matrix metalloproteinases for extracellular matrix degradation. Additionally, exosomal lncRNAs suppress anti-tumor immunity by inhibiting T cell activation, enhancing myeloid-derived suppressor cell immunosuppression via MALAT1/STAT3 signaling, and polarizing macrophages toward an immunosuppressive M2 phenotype through the H19/miR-152-3p/MAP3K1 axis. In drug resistance, multiple lncRNAs confer therapeutic resistance through distinct mechanisms: lncARSR and H19 act as ceRNAs to bypass chemotherapy sensitivity, AFAP1-AS1 enhances ERBB2 translation to overcome trastuzumab resistance, and PVT1 upregulates MDR1 drug efflux pumps. These findings position exosomal lncRNAs as promising therapeutic targets for combating tumor progression and reversing treatment resistance.

title: Circulating potential biomarkers in polycystic ovary syndrome and gynecologic cancers: Diagnostic insights from a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis identifies circulating microRNAs and long non-coding RNAs as promising biomarkers for polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and gynecologic cancers, with particular diagnostic utility demonstrated through pooled meta-analyses. In PCOS, miR-222-3p emerged as the superior biomarker with an AUC of 0.947, while miR-4488 and miR-151a-5p also showed excellent diagnostic potential with sensitivities ≥0.81. For ovarian cancer, the miR-200 family (miR-200a, miR-200b, miR-200c) and miR-21 demonstrated strong discriminatory power with AUCs ranging from 0.79 to 0.86, whereas miR-27a, miR-145, and miR-150-5p were associated with endometrial cancer diagnosis. Mechanistically, these circulating biomarkers regulate cancer progression through diverse pathways: miR-1246 promotes chemoresistance via Cav1 targeting, miR-25 inhibits apoptosis through Bim suppression, and miR-93 activates pro-proliferative signaling via PTEN inhibition. The findings establish that PCOS carries elevated risk for both endometrial and ovarian cancers, and circulating exosomal miRNAs represent non-invasive diagnostic tools with clinical potential for cancer screening and early detection in at-risk populations.

title: Role of small extracellular-vesicle microRNA-302f in endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer
summary: This study investigates how microRNA-302f (miR-302f) packaged in small extracellular vesicles contributes to the malignant transformation of endometriosis into ovarian cancer. The research demonstrates that miR-302f is significantly elevated in benign endometriosis but substantially reduced in endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer (EAOC) samples, suggesting its protective role against malignant progression. Functionally, miR-302f inhibits cancer cell migration and proliferation by directly downregulating PDGFRA and FGF7, genes known to promote tumor progression and drug resistance. The chronic inflammatory microenvironment of endometriosis—characterized by inflammation, oxidative stress, and hormonal stimulation—drives malignant transformation, while extracellular vesicles regulate this tumor microenvironment through intercellular communication. These findings identify miR-302f as a potential therapeutic target, with restoration of miR-302f expression potentially offering a novel strategy to prevent or treat endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer by suppressing PDGFRA-mediated tumor development.

title: Applications of Exosomes in Female Medicine: A Systematic Review of Molecular Biology, Diagnostic and Therapeutic Perspectives
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review examines exosomes as mediators of intercellular communication in female reproductive medicine, with particular emphasis on their roles in endometriosis pathogenesis and therapeutic potential. Exosomes are nanoscale extracellular vesicles that regulate critical reproductive processes including oocyte maturation, embryo–endometrial crosstalk, and placental development, with their biogenesis controlled by ESCRT-dependent and ESCRT-independent pathways involving proteins like tetraspanins (CD9, CD63, CD81) and ceramide. Dysregulated exosomal signaling is implicated in multiple gynecological disorders, particularly endometriosis, alongside polycystic ovary syndrome and premature ovarian insufficiency, while altered exosomal signaling contributes to obstetric complications including preeclampsia, gestational diabetes mellitus, and preterm birth. Endometrial stromal cell-derived exosomes modulate embryo implantation and endometrial receptivity through CD9 and CD81-enriched signatures, suggesting their critical role in reproductive success. The review highlights that exosome-mediated signaling is tightly regulated by hypoxia, inflammation, oxidative stress, and hormonal changes—environmental factors relevant to endometriosis pathology. Therapeutically, mesenchymal stem cell-derived exosomes show promise in restoring ovarian function and improving fertility outcomes, positioning exosomes as both biomarkers for diagnosis and targets for therapeutic intervention in female reproductive disorders.

title: miR-3613-5p promotes lung cancer progression by targeting and regulating XPO6
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that miR-3613-5p acts as an oncogenic microRNA in lung cancer by directly targeting and suppressing XPO6, a nuclear export protein involved in multiple cellular processes. miR-3613-5p is significantly elevated in lung cancer tissues and cell lines, with expression levels correlating strongly with smoking history, TNM stage, tumor size, lymph node metastasis, and poor overall survival. The functional mechanism involves miR-3613-5p binding to XPO6, which reduces XPO6 expression and subsequently promotes lung cancer cell proliferation, migration, and invasion. Restoration of XPO6 expression reverses these cancer-promoting effects, establishing XPO6 as a tumor suppressor in this pathway. Multivariate analysis identified miR-3613-5p as an independent prognostic factor for lung cancer prognosis, suggesting its potential utility as a non-invasive biomarker for patient risk stratification. These findings indicate that the miR-3613-5p/XPO6 axis represents a key regulatory mechanism in lung cancer progression with implications for therapeutic intervention.

title: miRNA expression profiles in serum exosome from patients with hepatitis B virus related hepatocellular carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This study identified distinct microRNA expression signatures in serum exosomes from hepatitis B virus-related hepatocellular carcinoma patients. Seventeen microRNAs including the let-7 family members (let-7a-5p, let-7d-5p, let-7f-5p, let-7g-5p) and others (miR-142-5p, miR-15b-5p, miR-16-5p, miR-182-5p, miR-20a-5p, miR-215-5p, miR-23a-3p, miR-26b-5p, miR-27a-3p, miR-374a-5p, miR-451a, miR-584-5p, miR-98-5p) were significantly upregulated in hepatocellular carcinoma compared to hepatic cirrhosis and normal controls, while five microRNAs (miR-100-5p, miR-122-5p, miR-192-5p, miR-200a-3p, miR-483-5p) were downregulated. MicroRNA-122, a liver-specific microRNA, plays a central regulatory role by targeting CUX1 and controlling multiple pathways including the miR-122/CUX1/miR-214/ZBTB20 axis that regulates AFP expression and the miR-122/CUX1/Rho A pathway controlling tumor aggressiveness. Notably, miR-122 silencing leads to increased arginine levels and sorafenib resistance, establishing it as a key biomarker for therapy response. These exosomal miRNA signatures represent stable, blood-based bio

title: Screening and analysis of plasma small extracellular vesicle MiRNAs at various estrous cycle phases of goat
summary:
# Summary

This study profiled plasma small extracellular vesicle (sEV) microRNAs across different estrous cycle phases in goats to identify stage-specific biomarkers of reproductive function. The researchers discovered that sEV miRNAs are dynamically regulated throughout the estrous cycle and identified specific miRNAs—including miR-215-5p, miR-192-5p, miR-122, and miR-107-3p—that are significantly elevated during the post-estrus phase, while miR-200c and miR-150 show higher expression during the diestrus period. Pathway analysis revealed that differentially abundant miRNAs target genes involved in critical reproductive signaling cascades, including GnRH signaling, Wnt signaling, oxytocin signaling, and AMPK pathways, which collectively regulate follicular development, oocyte maturation, and ovulation. The identified miRNAs function in intercellular communication by negatively regulating target gene expression and modulating immune responses, suggesting their potential utility as non-invasive biomarkers for monitoring estrous cycle progression and reproductive status in livestock.

title: Endometrial Expression of Angiogenesis-related Factors Following Ovarian Stimulation and Epigallocatechin Gallate Administration
journal: Iranian Journal of Nursing and Midwifery Research
summary: This study examines how ovarian stimulation and the green tea polyphenol epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) regulate endometrial angiogenesis through microRNA and growth factor signaling. Ovarian stimulation with human menopausal gonadotropin and human chorionic gonadotropin (HMG/HCG) upregulates the angiomiR miR-16-5p, which targets vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) mRNA, leading to increased VEGF protein expression, endothelial cell proliferation (marked by CD31-positive cells), and elevated steroid hormones (estradiol and progesterone). EGCG administration counteracts these effects by downregulating miR-16-5p expression and subsequently reducing VEGF protein levels, endothelial cell proliferation, and serum hormone concentrations, while also inhibiting VEGF receptors and multiple growth factor signaling pathways including PI3K, MAPK, and FAK. The dysregulation of miR-16-5p is associated with endometriosis and other reproductive pathologies, suggesting that EGCG's ability to suppress this angiomiR may offer therapeutic potential. These findings indicate that miR-16-5p mediates the effects of both ovarian stimulation and EGCG on endometrial angiogenesis and receptivity, establishing microRNA regulation as a critical mechanism linking hormonal stimulation to endometrial vascularization.

title: Prediction of high-quality human oocytes and embryos through the analysis of gene expression in cumulus cells
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that cumulus cell gene expression profiles serve as reliable biomarkers for predicting oocyte and embryo quality during assisted reproductive technology (ART). The TGF-β family member GDF9 acts as a master regulator, stimulating expression of multiple downstream effectors including PTGS2, HAS2, and GREM1, all of which promote cumulus expansion and follicular development. Elevated expression of these GDF9-target genes—particularly PTGS2 (prostaglandin E2 synthesis), HAS2 (hyaluronic acid biosynthesis and extracellular matrix remodeling), and GREM1 (BMP signaling antagonist)—correlates strongly with high-quality oocytes, superior embryo development, and improved pregnancy and live birth outcomes. Additional cumulus cell markers including VCAN, PTX3, and ADAMTS1 further support embryo quality assessment through their roles in extracellular matrix formation and reorganization, with lower HSPG2 expression also indicating oocyte maturity. Collectively, these findings establish cumulus cell transcriptomics as a non-invasive molecular approach for identifying viable embryos and optimizing patient selection in fertility treatment.

title: Inactivation of KLF4 promotes T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia and activates the MAP2K7 pathway
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that KLF4 functions as a tumor suppressor in T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (T-ALL) by repressing the MAP2K7-JNK-ATF2 signaling pathway. KLF4 is frequently inactivated in T-ALL patients through DNA methylation, and its loss cooperates with activating NOTCH1 mutations to promote aggressive leukemia development with increased leukemia-initiating cell frequency and enhanced G1-to-S cell cycle progression. The authors identify a critical regulatory mechanism where KLF4 directly binds the MAP2K7 promoter to suppress MAP2K7 expression; loss of KLF4 leads to aberrant MAP2K7-JNK pathway activation, which drives T cell proliferation and leukemic expansion. Pharmacological inhibition of JNK with multiple inhibitors, including JNK-IN-8, CC401, and AS602801, effectively reduces leukemic T cell expansion and induces cytotoxicity in both cell-based and patient-derived xenograft models. These findings establish the KLF4-MAP2K7-JNK axis as a critical regulatory pathway in T-ALL pathogenesis and identify JNK inhibitors as potential therapeutic agents, particularly for patients with low KLF4 expression or therapy-resistant disease.

title: Paracrine effect of human stem cell-derived progenitor cells on remodeling of the vagina
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that conditioned media from human stem cell-derived progenitor smooth muscle cells (pSMC-CM) promotes vaginal tissue regeneration through paracrine mechanisms, with multiple individual cell lines showing distinct but complementary effects. pSMC-CM broadly upregulates extracellular matrix proteins including elastin, collagen I/III, and associated enzymes (elastase, α2-macroglobulin), while also enhancing smooth muscle actin expression and contractile function in both cultured vaginal fibroblasts and surgically injured rat vaginas. These findings are clinically relevant because pelvic organ prolapse is associated with deficiencies in smooth muscle content and ECM alterations, conditions that pSMC-CM treatment appears to reverse. The paracrine factors secreted by these progenitor cells engage multiple biological pathways including estrogen signaling and promote both ECM remodeling and contractile protein assembly, positioning stem cell-derived secreted factors as a potential therapeutic approach for vaginal tissue regeneration and pelvic floor dysfunction without requiring cell transplantation.

title: Ectopic endometrial mesenchymal stem cell-derived exosomal miR-4466 promotes angiogenesis by targeting RUNX1 in adenomyosis
summary:
# Summary

Ectopic endometrial mesenchymal stem cells (Ec-eMSCs) produce exosomes enriched in miR-4466, which promote pathological angiogenesis in adenomyosis through a regulatory axis involving RUNX1 and VEGFA. miR-4466 directly targets and suppresses RUNX1, a transcription factor that normally inhibits VEGFA expression, thereby derepressing pro-angiogenic signaling and enhancing endothelial cell proliferation, migration, invasion, and tube formation. Blocking exosome synthesis with GW4869 significantly attenuates these pro-angiogenic effects, confirming that exosomal miR-4466 mediates paracrine communication between ectopic endometrial cells and vascular endothelium. The elevated microvascular density and abnormal angiogenesis observed in adenomyotic lesions are mechanistically linked to this exosomal miR-4466/RUNX1/VEGFA pathway, identifying miR-4466 as a potential therapeutic target for interrupting the vascular remodeling that underlies adenomyosis-associated symptoms including abnormal uterine bleeding and infertility.

title: Serum miRNA-based diagnostic models for endometriosis: from discovery to validation
summary: This study developed and validated serum microRNA-based diagnostic models for endometriosis by identifying specific miRNA signatures in circulation. An optimal 11-miRNA panel including miR-140-3p, miR-181a-5p, miR-192-5p, and others was constructed for general endometriosis detection, while a refined 6-miRNA model (miR-192-5p, miR-30b-5p, miR-335-5p, miR-338-3p, miR-486-5p, and miR-652-3p) demonstrated superior performance for diagnosing the more severe deep infiltrating endometriosis subtype. The study revealed distinct dysregulation patterns, with six miRNAs (miR-26a-5p, miR-342-3p, miR-335-5p, miR-181a-5p, miR-30b-5p, and miR-340-5p) significantly reduced in endometriosis while miR-486-5p was elevated. Mechanistically, reduced miR-340-5p promotes endometrial cell proliferation, invasion, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition through MAPK/ERK pathway activation, while elevated miR-486-5p drives similar pathological processes by suppressing PTEN and activating PI3K/Akt signaling. These circulating miRNA signatures provide validated, non-invasive biomarkers for endometriosis diagnosis and reveal key molecular mechanisms driving the disease's pathology.

title: Endometriosis-derived exosomal MicroRNA-125b-5p downregulates phosphatidylinositol 3-Kinase/Protein Kinase B signaling pathways via vascular endothelial growth factor to decelerate endometriosis progression
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that endometriosis-derived exosomal miR-125b-5p functions as a suppressive regulator of endometriosis progression by directly targeting vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and subsequently inhibiting the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway. MiR-125b-5p is significantly downregulated in endometriosis patients and endometrial stromal cells, and its overexpression potently suppresses cell proliferation, migration, and invasion while inhibiting angiogenesis—processes critical for ectopic endometrial lesion establishment and maintenance. The mechanistic pathway involves miR-125b-5p binding to the 3' UTR of VEGF mRNA, reducing both VEGF expression and downstream PI3K/AKT activation, thereby restraining the pathogenic cellular behaviors associated with endometriosis. Conversely, multiple other exosomal miRNAs including miR-3141, miR-223-3p, miR-142-5p, and miR-1246 are upregulated in endometriosis, suggesting dysregulation of circulating exosomal miRNA profiles contributes to disease pathogenesis. These findings highlight exosomal miRNAs as key mediators of intercellular communication in the endometriosis microenvironment and identify miR-125b-5p as a potential biomarker and therapeutic target for decelerating endometriosis progression.

title: Serum Reproductive Hormone Profiles and Endometrial Expression of miR-16-5p, VEGF Gene, and Protein, and CD31-Positive Cells following Progesterone and EGCG Administration
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how progesterone and epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), a green tea polyphenol, modulate endometrial angiogenesis through the miR-16-5p/VEGF axis. miR-16-5p is an angiomiR that directly targets VEGF mRNA and is dysregulated in endometriosis, preeclampsia, and endometrial cancer; angiogenesis itself is essential for endometrial receptivity and successful pregnancy. The research demonstrated that progesterone administration upregulated miR-16-5p expression and increased VEGF protein levels and CD31-positive endothelial cells, supporting endometrial vascularization and receptivity. In contrast, EGCG treatment significantly downregulated miR-16-5p, reduced VEGF protein expression, and decreased CD31-positive cells, thereby inhibiting angiogenesis through multiple mechanisms including suppression of VEGF signaling pathways and receptors. Additionally, EGCG reduced circulating progesterone and estradiol levels compared to control and progesterone-treated groups, suggesting it may counteract hormone-dependent angiogenic effects. These findings reveal that miR-16-5p functions as a critical angiogenic regulator in the endometrium and that combined progesterone/EGCG approaches could potentially modulate pathological angiogenesis in endometriosis while supporting normal endometrial function.

title: tsRNA-Ala-3–0030 drives ovarian cancer progression by suppressing ZNF70
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies tsRNA-Ala-3–0030 as a critical oncogenic regulator in ovarian cancer that drives malignant progression by directly suppressing the tumor suppressor ZNF70. The tsRNA is significantly upregulated in ovarian cancer tissues and promotes proliferation, migration, and invasion through post-transcriptional silencing of ZNF70 via binding to its 3'-UTR, a mechanism analogous to microRNA-mediated gene regulation. ZNF70, a KRAB zinc-finger protein that mediates transcriptional repression through KAP1-dependent chromatin remodeling, is substantially reduced in ovarian cancer and its restoration effectively reverses the oncogenic effects of tsRNA-Ala-3–0030. Functionally, inhibition of tsRNA-Ala-3–0030 markedly reduces tumor growth and proliferation markers (Ki67) while restoring ZNF70 expression, demonstrating therapeutic potential. Low ZNF70 expression correlates with poor patient prognosis and advanced FIGO stage disease, establishing both molecules as prognostically relevant biomarkers. These findings suggest that tsRNA-targeted antagomirs may represent a novel precision oncology strategy for ovarian cancer treatment.

title: Tissue-derived extracellular vesicles: comparing Ts-EVs and Te-EVs in extraction, characteristics and research trends
summary:
# Summary

Tissue-derived extracellular vesicles (Ti-EVs) are nanovesicles that mediate intercellular communication and reflect the physiological or pathological state of their source tissues, making them valuable for both research and clinical applications. The article reviews extraction methods for tissue-derived EVs (Ts-EVs) versus cultured cell-derived EVs (Te-EVs), employing enzymatic digestion with collagenase D, DNase I, and papain followed by enrichment via ultracentrifugation, size-exclusion chromatography, or density gradient centrifugation. Ti-EVs carry bioactive cargo including miRNAs and proteins that mediate diverse therapeutic and pathological effects—for example, mitochondrial transfer via brain EVs enhances ATP production in hypoxic cells, while specific miRNAs like miR-146a-5p reduce neuronal spine density, and AD-derived brain EVs promote lipogenic differentiation in stem cells through miR-483-5p-mediated inhibition of Igf2. Tissue-specific EVs demonstrate broad clinical relevance across multiple diseases: liver Ti-EVs reduce necrosis and promote recovery, adipose Ti-EVs inhibit prostate cancer metastasis, and cardiac and kidney Ti-EVs contain disease-associated miRNAs and proteins linked to ischemia-reperfusion injury and diabetic nephropathy. This comprehensive overview highlights Ti-EVs as promising biomarkers and therapeutic agents across neurodegenerative, cardiovascular, metabolic, and oncologic diseases.

title: LncRNAs in Ovarian Cancer: Emerging Insights and Future Perspectives in Tumor Biology and Clinical Applications
summary: Long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) regulate ovarian cancer through multiple molecular mechanisms including chromatin remodeling, transcriptional regulation, RNA splicing, and mRNA stability. Dysregulation of lncRNAs drives ovarian cancer initiation, progression, metastasis, and chemoresistance by promoting epithelial-mesenchymal transition, angiogenesis, and cell proliferation while suppressing DNA damage responses and apoptosis. Specific lncRNAs such as HOTAIR, LINC00511, TP73-AS1, LINC01210, and LINC00702 exert oncogenic effects primarily through interactions with the polycomb repressive complex 2 (PRC2) component EZH2, which facilitates H3K27 trimethylation and epigenetic silencing of tumor suppressors like p21 and KLF transcription factors. These dysregulated lncRNAs demonstrate diagnostic and prognostic potential for ovarian cancer patients. Emerging RNA-targeting technologies including CRISPR-Cas13 offer promising therapeutic strategies for selective degradation and silencing of oncogenic lncRNAs, providing new avenues for precision treatment approaches in ovarian cancer.

title: Clinical Applications of Extracellular Vesicles: Promises and Pitfalls
summary:
# Summary

Extracellular vesicles (EVs), particularly exosomes, function as critical mediators of intercellular communication and show substantial promise for clinical applications in fertility and reproductive medicine due to their biocompatibility, stability, and capacity for drug delivery and tissue repair. EVs derived from reproductive tissues—including follicular fluid, endometrial cells, oviducts, and placenta—regulate key reproductive processes such as folliculogenesis, oocyte maturation, embryo implantation, and maternal-fetal communication by modulating trophoblast behavior and hormone production. In reproductive pathologies, aberrant EV profiles are associated with infertility, polycystic ovary syndrome, endometriosis, and pregnancy complications; for example, EVs from women with endometriosis or recurrent implantation failure display reduced levels of cell adhesion and embryonic invasion capacity, while elevated placental EVs correlate with preeclampsia and gestational diabetes mellitus. Therapeutically, mesenchymal stem cell-derived EVs demonstrate immunomodulatory effects by promoting regulatory T-cell and macrophage differentiation while suppressing effector T-lymphocytes and natural killer cells, alongside direct anti-inflammatory and tissue-repair activities mediated through the NLRP3 and PD-L1 pathways. These findings establish EVs as both biomarkers for diagnosing reproductive disorders and promising therapeutic vehicles for precision medicine approaches in endometriosis and related infertility conditions.

title: Identification of candidate microRNA biomarkers of endometriosis in different bodily fluids
summary:
# Summary

This study identified dysregulated microRNAs (miRNAs) in multiple bodily fluids as potential noninvasive biomarkers for endometriosis diagnosis. Serum, saliva, and vaginal mucus were analyzed for miRNA content, with serum containing the highest abundance and diversity of miRNAs, including thirteen differentially expressed species. Key candidate biomarkers include downregulated miR-200a-3p and miR-200b-3p in serum, upregulated miR-219b-5p in serum, and miR-1304-3p as the only miRNA dysregulated across both serum and vaginal mucus. Integrated pathway analysis revealed that dysregulated miRNAs target proteins involved in critical endometriosis-associated processes including apoptosis, Wnt signaling, autophagy, and cellular senescence, with specific targets like CD44 and WNK2 implicated in epithelial-mesenchymal transition and Wnt pathway dysregulation. The miR-200 family's role in regulating epithelial-mesenchymal transition and their targeting of PTEN underscore their significance in endometriosis pathogenesis. These findings establish a foundation for developing fluid-based miRNA biomarker panels to improve endometriosis detection and monitoring while revealing the molecular mechanisms underlying the disease.

title: HAGLR promotes endometriosis progression via the miR-185-5p/VEGFA axis and serves as a potential diagnostic biomarker
summary: This study identifies the long non-coding RNA HAGLR as a key driver of endometriosis progression through a competing endogenous RNA mechanism. HAGLR is significantly elevated in endometriotic tissues and associated with advanced disease stages and deep infiltrating endometriosis, serving as an independent risk factor for the condition. Mechanistically, HAGLR functions by sponging miR-185-5p, which normally suppresses VEGFA expression; elevated HAGLR thus relieves miR-185-5p-mediated inhibition of VEGFA, leading to increased vascular endothelial growth factor that promotes endometrial stromal cell proliferation, migration, and invasion. Functional studies using the 12Z endometrial cell line demonstrated that HAGLR knockdown reduces these aggressive cellular behaviors, while VEGFA overexpression rescues the inhibitory effects, confirming the miR-185-5p/VEGFA axis as critical to HAGLR's pathogenic role. Additionally, HAGLR demonstrates strong diagnostic potential with an area under the curve of 0.83 and high sensitivity and specificity, positioning it as a promising biomarker for endometriosis detection and disease stratification.

title: Aberrant STAT3 activation and overproduction of IL-21 in systemic lupus erythematosus: role of miR-155 and miR-21 in target genes SOCS1, PTEN and PIAS3
summary:
# Summary

Systemic lupus erythematosus is characterized by aberrant microRNA regulation that drives pathogenic IL-21 overproduction and STAT3 pathway hyperactivation. Both miR-155 and miR-21 are significantly elevated in SLE patients and suppress negative regulators of STAT3 signaling, namely SOCS1, PTEN, and PIAS3, through direct targeting. This suppression of inhibitory molecules allows unopposed IL-21 signaling through the IL-21R/STAT3 axis, amplifying the production of IL-21 by T follicular helper cells and other T helper subsets. Elevated IL-21 subsequently promotes B cell activation and differentiation into autoantibody-producing plasma cells, driving the exaggerated humoral immune response central to SLE pathogenesis. The dysregulation of these miRNA-target gene networks represents a critical molecular mechanism linking inflammatory microRNAs to aberrant STAT3 activation and autoimmune pathology in lupus.

title: Human Endometriotic Lesion‐Derived Small Extracellular Vesicles Impair Macrophage Function in the Peritoneal Microenvironment
summary:
# Summary

Small extracellular vesicles derived from ectopic endometrial tissue (ECT EEO-sEVs) and peritoneal fluid (PF-sEVs) of endometriosis patients suppress macrophage phagocytosis through CD47/SIRP-α signaling, thereby impairing the immune microenvironment and facilitating lesion persistence. These vesicles display distinct surface marker profiles characterized by increased expression of adhesion molecules CD44 and CD29, which promote lesion establishment in the peritoneal microenvironment. The study identified CD133/1, EpCAM, CD9, CD81, and CD63 as dominant markers on endometrial-derived vesicles, establishing their epithelial origin and distinguishing them from other cell types in peritoneal fluid. Additionally, endometrial stromal cell-derived sEVs transfer bioactive molecules including miR-22-3p and long non-coding RNAs that enhance endometrial cell proliferation, migration, and invasion through SIRT1/NF-κB pathway activation. These findings demonstrate that endometriosis-derived small extracellular vesicles mediate pathogenic cellular interactions in the peritoneal microenvironment, offering potential therapeutic targets for preventing immune dysfunction and lesion growth in this chronic pelvic condition affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age.

title: MicroRNA Expression Profile in Endometriosis and Endometriosis-Associated Ovarian Cancer—Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis confers approximately a 2.5-fold increased risk of ovarian cancer, with dysregulated microRNA (miRNA) expression playing a pivotal role in this malignant transformation. MicroRNAs regulate critical biological pathways including proliferation, apoptosis, migration, cell cycle control, differentiation, and angiogenesis; dysregulation of these molecules contributes to both endometriosis development and progression toward endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer (EAOC). Upregulated miRNAs promote tumorigenesis by suppressing tumor suppressors such as PTEN, BRCA1, and PDCD4 while simultaneously activating oncogenic pathways like PI3K/AKT signaling and the epithelial-mesenchymal transition, processes in which the miRNA-200 family plays a particularly crucial role. Despite the clear mechanistic importance of miRNA dysregulation in EAOC, the field faces significant limitations including inconsistent miRNA patterns across studies, heterogeneous methodologies, varying reference genes, and tissue-specific expression differences that prevent robust cross-study comparisons and development of predictive biomarkers. Advanced approaches such as Next-Generation Sequencing combined with standardized protocols may overcome these limitations and enable miRNA profiling to serve as a clinical diagnostic tool for endometriosis and its malignant transformation.

title: Clinical Implications of Extracellular Vesicles in Endometriosis: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a debilitating gynecological condition characterized by ectopic endometrial growth that causes pelvic pain, infertility, dysmenorrhea, and gastrointestinal and urological complications, while also increasing the lifetime risk of ovarian cancer. Extracellular vesicles (EVs), functioning as critical intercellular communicators, play a central role in endometriosis pathogenesis by carrying molecular cargo including microRNAs, long noncoding RNAs, and tRNA-derived fragments that regulate angiogenesis, immunomodulation, and fibrosis through signaling pathways such as MAP kinase and PI3K-AKT. Specific exosomal miRNAs including miR-22-3p, miR-320a, and miR-202-3p are significantly upregulated in patient-derived biofluids and show promise as diagnostic biomarkers for endometriosis severity and disease detection, while lncRNAs and tRNA fragments dysregulated in plasma and vaginal discharge may differentiate early-stage lesions. Therapeutically, exosomes derived from menstrual blood stem cells demonstrate potential in treating endometriosis by reducing inflammation, suppressing cell proliferation and migration, inhibiting angiogenesis, and promoting apoptosis in ectopic endometrial cells. These findings establish EVs and their cargo molecules as key mechanistic drivers of endometriosis and promising targets for both non-invasive diagnosis and novel therapeutic interventions.

title: Sox6, A Potential Target for MicroRNAs in Cardiometabolic Disease
summary: Sox6, a transcription factor in the SoxD family, plays critical roles in adult tissue homeostasis and regeneration while being tightly regulated by microRNAs including miR-208a, miR-208b, miR-499, miR-19a, and miR-802. Abnormal Sox6 expression is associated with multiple cardiometabolic diseases including diabetes, cardiomyopathy, hypertension, and heart failure, with miR-208a and miR-499 emerging as key negative regulators of Sox6 in cardiac contexts. The RNA-binding protein Trbp is essential for maintaining normal heart function partly through its role in regulating miR-208a expression, and Trbp knockout leads to reduced miR-208a levels, elevated Sox6 expression, and subsequent cardiomyopathy and heart failure. Therapeutic approaches targeting this regulatory axis show promise, with miR-208a overexpression successfully rescuing heart function in Trbp-deficient mice by suppressing Sox6, while miR-19a-containing exosomes and miR-499 overexpression protect cardiomyocytes from hypoxia-reoxygenation injury through Sox6 downregulation. These findings establish the miRNA-Sox6 regulatory network as a fundamental mechanism in cardiometabolic disease pathogenesis with potential therapeutic implications.

title: Nanodiamond-based microRNA delivery system promotes pluripotent stem cells toward myocardiogenic reprogramming
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that nanodiamonds serve as an effective delivery vehicle for miR-181a to enhance cardiomyogenic differentiation of induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs). The mechanism centers on miR-181a's direct targeting of the Hox-A11 3' untranslated region, which suppresses Hox-A11 protein expression and relieves its inhibition of MyoD, a master regulator of muscle differentiation. Nanodiamond-mediated delivery of miR-181a upregulates multiple cardiac markers including cTnT, MLC2a, MHC, MCK, and HCN4, while suppression of Hox-A11 restores MyoD expression and cardiomyogenic capacity. In functional studies, nanodiamond-miR-181a-treated iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes exhibited enlarged beating areas and reconstructed beating function compared to controls. These findings suggest that the nanodiamond-miR-181a delivery system may provide an efficient method for generating functional cardiomyocytes with therapeutic potential for treating myocardial ischemia and heart failure.

title: Kruppel like factor 12 gene regulates ovarian granulosa cells proliferation and apoptosis via the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that Kruppel-like factor 12 (KLF12) is highly expressed in ovarian tissues and functions as a negative regulator of granulosa cell proliferation and steroid hormone production through the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway. KLF12 overexpression suppresses the phosphorylation of PI3K and Akt, which in turn inhibits the expression of key steroidogenic genes (CYP11A1, CYP19A1, and StAR) and reduces estradiol and progesterone synthesis and secretion. Simultaneously, KLF12 overexpression promotes granulosa cell apoptosis by upregulating pro-apoptotic markers (Bax, Caspase-3, Caspase-9, and P53) while inhibiting anti-apoptotic proteins (BCL-2) and proliferation markers (PCNA, CCNB1, CCNA2, and CDK4). Conversely, KLF12 knockdown enhances PI3K/Akt pathway activation, increases steroid hormone production, promotes cell cycle progression, and reduces apoptosis. These findings establish KLF12 as a critical transcriptional regulator of ovarian granulosa cell physiology with potential implications for understanding follicular development and reproductive function.

title: IRF6 induces endothelial dysfunction through the transcriptional activation of NDRG1 and aggravates low shear stress-mediated atherosclerosis
summary: This study identifies interferon regulatory factor 6 (IRF6) as a critical mediator of low-shear stress (LSS)-induced atherosclerosis through transcriptional activation of NDRG1. In both in vivo atherosclerotic mouse models and human aortic endothelial cells exposed to LSS, IRF6 expression was significantly elevated and promoted endothelial dysfunction, inflammatory responses, and oxidative stress. Mechanistically, IRF6 directly binds to the NDRG1 promoter and upregulates NDRG1 expression, which in turn suppresses endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS) and increases adhesion molecule expression (VCAM1, ICAM1, E-selectin) and macrophage recruitment. Importantly, IRF6 knockdown prevented plaque formation, lipid deposition, and monocyte adhesion while restoring eNOS expression, but these protective effects were reversed by NDRG1 overexpression, confirming NDRG1 as a key downstream effector. These findings establish the IRF6–NDRG1 axis as a therapeutic target for mitigating LSS-mediated atherosclerosis development and suggest that suppressing IRF6 signaling could protect against vascular endothelial dysfunction and atherosclerotic lesion progression.

title: Long non-coding RNA LINC00858 exerts a tumor-promoting role in colon cancer via HNF4α and WNK2 regulation
summary: This study reveals that the long non-coding RNA LINC00858 functions as a tumor promoter in colon cancer through a regulatory pathway involving the transcription factor HNF4α and the growth suppressor WNK2. LINC00858 is highly expressed in colon cancer tissues and cell lines, where it binds to and upregulates HNF4α, which subsequently downregulates WNK2 expression. Since WNK2 normally inhibits cell proliferation by suppressing the MEK1/ERK1/2 signaling cascade, the LINC00858-mediated reduction in WNK2 leads to pathway activation and enhanced cancer cell proliferation. Functionally, LINC00858 overexpression promotes colon cancer cell proliferation, invasion, migration, and angiogenesis, with high expression correlating with poor tumor differentiation, advanced TNM staging, and lymph node metastasis. These findings establish LINC00858 as a biomarker and potential therapeutic target in colon cancer, consistent with its tumor-promoting roles demonstrated in other malignancies including osteosarcoma and non-small cell lung cancer.

title: Valvular Leaflets Are Not Innocent Bystanders: Divergent Fibrotic Remodeling Accompanies Functional Mitral and Tricuspid Regurgitation
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that valve leaflets actively drive fibrotic remodeling in functional mitral regurgitation (FMR) and tricuspid regurgitation (FTR) rather than serving as passive bystanders. In FMR, suppressed retinoic acid metabolism promotes transition of antifibrotic valvular interstitial cells to a neutral phenotype, while fibrotic leaflet remodeling independently predicts disease severity. In FTR, impaired interferon (IFN) signaling instead drives cells toward a profibrotic phenotype and promotes endothelial-to-mesenchymal transition, with CD45+ valve endothelial cells significantly upregulated in myocardial infarction-induced FMR. The study identifies distinct molecular pathways underlying each condition: FMR involves matrix remodeling and leaflet adaptive growth that responds to losartan treatment, while FTR severity correlates with PDK4 upregulation. Therapeutic targeting of IFN signaling activation or PDK4 modulation shows promise for reducing FTR severity, offering mechanistic insights into treating these prevalent complications of chronic heart failure.

title: Epitranscriptomic modifications in embryonic development: insights into natural and ART-induced mechanisms and implications
summary: N6-methyladenosine (m6A) is a critical epitranscriptomic modification that regulates multiple reproductive processes including gametogenesis, embryonic development, pregnancy, and placental function through the coordinated actions of writer proteins (METTL3, METTL14, WTAP), reader proteins (YTHDF2, YTHDC1, YTHDC2), and eraser proteins (FTO, ALKBH5). In female reproduction, m6A controls oocyte maturation, cytoplasmic maturation, and embryonic genome activation; deficiencies in m6A-regulatory machinery such as ALKBH5 deletion or YTHDC1 loss result in meiotic arrest and female infertility, while abnormal m6A levels are associated with premature ovarian insufficiency. In males, m6A is essential for spermatogenesis and male fertility, with deletions of METTL3, METTL4, or WTAP causing spermatogonia stem cell depletion and impaired spermatogenesis. Assisted reproductive technology (ART) stressors including oxidative imbalance, hormonal stimulation, and cryopreservation disrupt RNA methylation patterns, leading to altered embryo competence, impaired placental function, reduced lineage specification, and increased risk of congenital defects and imprinting disorders in offspring. These findings highlight that epitranscriptomic modifications are fundamental regulators of reproductive success and that ART-induced alterations in RNA methylation represent a significant mechanism underlying adverse reproductive and developmental outcomes.

title: Advances in the biological functions of miR-205 in various diseases (Review)
summary:
# Summary

miR-205 is a microRNA encoded on chromosome 1q32.2 that functions as a critical post-transcriptional regulator of gene expression across diverse biological processes including cell proliferation, differentiation, apoptosis, and epithelial homeostasis. The microRNA's expression is tightly controlled by multiple transcriptional regulators (p53, p63, p73, and Sp1) and epigenetic modifications (DNA methylation and histone modifications), with dysregulation associated with malignant tumors, cardiovascular diseases, and metabolic disorders. In endometrial biology specifically, miR-205 is elevated in endometrial epithelial cells following ovulation but reduced in infertile women's endometrial tissues, suggesting it plays a critical role in modulating endometrial receptivity and embryo implantation. Mechanistically, miR-205 regulates numerous target genes including ZEB2, thrombospondin-1, Smad2, VEGFA, and HDAC2, thereby controlling fibrosis, angiogenesis, and tissue repair processes. Beyond its canonical roles in tumorigenesis, miR-205 participates in attenuating myocardial injury, suppressing atrial fibrosis, and modulating pancreatic islet function, highlighting its pleiotropic effects across non-cancerous diseases. This review demonstrates that miR-205 represents a multifunctional regulator whose dysregulation contributes to therapeutic resistance and multiple pathological conditions, making it a promising therapeutic target across diverse disease contexts.

title: Tumor exosomes impact functional hallmarks of cancer
summary:
# Summary

Cancer-derived exosomes serve as critical intercellular communication vehicles that transfer oncogenic proteins, metabolites, and nucleic acids to promote cancer progression through coordinated molecular reprogramming of both tumor and stromal compartments. Tumor-derived exosomes drive proliferative signaling, metabolic rewiring, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and immune evasion while reprogramming immune and stromal compartments to support malignancy. The hypoxic tumor microenvironment significantly enhances exosome production and biogenesis through HIF-1α-mediated upregulation of genes including Rab27A and Rab22A, while hypoxia-conditioned exosomes become selectively enriched in pro-malignant cargo such as VEGF, TGF-β, ANGPTL4, and oncogenic microRNAs (miR-210, miR-21, miR-135b) that enhance angiogenesis, migration, survival, and metastatic potential in recipient cells. Exosomal integrins and tetraspanins determine organotropism and facilitate selective cellular uptake, while exosomal miRNAs and growth factors (EGF, FGF, HGF, VEGF) activate MAPK/ERK and PI3K/AKT signaling to suppress cell cycle regulators. Rab27A represents a key therapeutic target, as its knockdown substantially reduces exosome release, restricts tumor growth, and inhibits metastasis.

title: EV-derived small non-coding RNAs from porcine follicular fluid regulate follicle development using Pandora sequence
summary:
# Summary

Extracellular vesicles (EVs) in porcine follicular fluid contain diverse small non-coding RNAs—including piRNAs, miRNAs, snoRNAs, and tsRNAs—that orchestrate oocyte developmental competence in a size-dependent manner. Large follicles display elevated levels of specific miRNAs (miR-10b, miR-29b, miR-100, miR-221-3p) and piRNAs (piR-1060463, piR-1205752) alongside snoRNAs, while small follicles are enriched in tsRNA variants, particularly 3' and 5' tsRNAs. The EV-derived small non-coding RNAs target genes involved in critical developmental pathways including oocyte meiosis, insulin signaling, Notch signaling, and mitochondrial transport. Notably, dysregulated expression patterns of exosomal small non-coding RNAs have been linked to impaired oocyte quality in pathological conditions such as endometriosis and PCOS, with reduced miR-29b levels specifically correlating with diminished granulosa cell viability and altered steroidogenesis. These findings establish follicular fluid EV-derived small non-coding RNAs as key regulators of oocyte quality and potential non-invasive biomarkers for predicting developmental competence.

title: MicroRNA differential expression analysis in canine visceral hemangiosarcoma formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded tissues
summary:
# Summary

This study identified dysregulated microRNA signatures in canine visceral hemangiosarcoma tissues using differential expression analysis of formalin-fixed samples. A comprehensive analysis revealed 67 miRNAs dysregulated in splenic hemangiosarcoma and 71 in cardiac hemangiosarcoma, with 18 miRNAs shared between both tumor types, suggesting organ-specific and common pathogenic mechanisms. Key miRNAs including the tumor-suppressive miR-214, which promotes apoptosis via the COP1-p53 pathway, and the pro-angiogenic miR-126, which is elevated in hemangiosarcoma tissues, emerged as critical regulators of tumor biology. Pathway enrichment analysis demonstrated that dysregulated miRNAs converge on major oncogenic signaling cascades including MAPK, Rap1, Ras, TGF-β, PI3K-Akt, and HIF-1 pathways, which collectively regulate tumorigenesis, angiogenesis, and immune evasion. These miRNA signatures show potential diagnostic and prognostic value as biomarkers for visceral hemangiosarcoma, offering insights into the molecular mechanisms driving this aggressive endothelial malignancy and supporting their use in clinical assessment alongside conventional treatment approaches such as surgery and doxorubicin chemotherapy.

title: Look Who Is Talking: Extracellular Vesicles as Mediators of Intercellular and Interorgan Communication
summary:
# Summary

Extracellular vesicles (EVs), including exosomes, function as critical mediators of cell-to-cell and interorgan communication under both physiological and pathological conditions, carrying cargo such as mRNA and microRNAs from donor to recipient cells. EV biogenesis involves multiple molecular pathways, including ESCRT-dependent mechanisms where ESCRT complexes orchestrate intraluminal vesicle formation through ubiquitin recognition and cargo selection, as well as ESCRT-independent pathways such as the Syndecan-Syntenin-Alix axis and ceramide-driven budding initiated by nSMase2. miRNA loading into exosomes is regulated by RNA-binding proteins like hnRNPA2B1 and Ago2, with N6-methyladenosine modifications directing miRNA recognition and sorting specificity. Following multivesicular body trafficking via Rab GTPases and cortactin, EVs are released and internalized into recipient cells through clathrin-mediated endocytosis, caveolin-mediated pathways, phagocytosis, or macropinocytosis, thereby triggering downstream signaling cascades. EVs derived from immune cells, hepatocytes, and tumor cells have distinct functional roles, promoting inflammatory responses, immune modulation, and tumor progression respectively, establishing EVs as important biomarkers and mediators in pathological conditions including metabolic syndromes, autoimmune diseases, and cancers.

title: Human Corneal Stromal Stem Cell Treatment Reduces Established Opacities in Chronic Corneal Scarring
summary: Human corneal stromal stem cells (hCSSCs) effectively reduce established corneal opacities and chronic fibrosis through a multifaceted mechanism involving both direct cellular activity and paracrine signaling. Upon pro-inflammatory priming, hCSSCs upregulate matrix metalloproteinase (MMP) activity and increase the MMP2/TIMP2 ratio, enabling active degradation of pathological extracellular matrix and disruption of fibrotic tissue. Simultaneously, hCSSCs secrete the regenerative cytokine transforming growth factor β3 (TGFβ3) and exosome-packaged miR-29a, which suppress myofibroblast differentiation and collagen overproduction while downregulating fibrosis-associated genes including collagen type III, fibronectin, and α-smooth muscle actin. Additionally, hCSSC-derived exosomes mediate tumor necrosis factor-inducible gene 6 (TSG-6) signaling to attenuate innate immune activation and persistent inflammatory signaling characteristic of chronic scarring. These findings demonstrate that hCSSC-based therapy can rebalance proteolytic activity and reprogram the chronically fibrotic microenvironment toward a regenerative state, offering a promising cell-based approach to restore corneal transparency and resolve established scarring.

title: Extracellular vesicles derived from TNF-α-preconditioned mesenchymal stem cells mitigate inflammatory retinal injury
journal: Extracellular Vesicles and Circulating Nucleic Acids
summary: TNF-α preconditioning of mesenchymal stem cells enhances the therapeutic capacity of their derived small extracellular vesicles (T-sEV) by enriching anti-inflammatory microRNAs, particularly miR-146a-5p, which suppress pro-inflammatory gene expression including Cd86 and Il1r1. When internalized by macrophages, T-sEV effectively suppress M1 polarization and pro-inflammatory activation, thereby reducing the chronic, low-grade inflammation (parainflammation) that drives neurodegeneration in retinal diseases. In a sodium iodate-induced retinal degeneration model, intravitreal T-sEV treatment markedly reduced retinal macrophage infiltration, preserved retinal function measured by electroretinography amplitudes, and maintained structural integrity without adverse systemic effects. This neuroprotective effect counteracts the pathogenic inflammatory cascade triggered by complement dysregulation and oxidative stress that normally leads to loss of photoreceptors and retinal pigment epithelium. The findings demonstrate that TNF-α preconditioning paradoxically activates compensatory anti-inflammatory and pro-survival programs in MSCs, providing a bioengineering strategy to enhance the therapeutic efficacy of cell-derived extracellular vesicles for inflammatory retinal diseases.

title: The Effect of Curcumin on Angiogenic and Proliferative Factors in Human Endometriotic Cells
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a gynecological disorder characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth involving abnormal cell adhesion, invasion, proliferation, and neoangiogenesis, with affected women experiencing pelvic pain and infertility. The pathology depends on elevated vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and dysregulated long noncoding RNA H19, which typically show decreased expression in the eutopic endometrium of endometriosis patients alongside reduced insulin-like growth factors (IGF1 and IGF2) that normally regulate endometrial cell growth and differentiation. In cultured endometriotic stromal cells, curcumin demonstrated potent therapeutic potential by inhibiting cellular proliferation, migration, and angiogenesis through dose-dependent mechanisms, while simultaneously downregulating VEGF expression and upregulating H19, IGF1, and IGF2 expression. These molecular effects reverse the pathological expression patterns observed in endometriosis and leverage curcumin's established anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anti-angiogenic properties. The findings suggest curcumin may provide a novel therapeutic approach for endometriosis by targeting the growth factor signaling pathways that drive disease pathogenesis.

title: Menstrual cycle-dependent expression of tRNA-derived fragments in healthy human endometrium and uterine fluid-derived extracellular vesicles
summary:
# Summary

This study characterizes tRNA-derived fragments (tRFs) in human endometrium and uterine fluid-derived extracellular vesicles across the menstrual cycle, revealing dynamic regulation linked to reproductive function. Using small RNA sequencing, researchers detected tRFs from all 21 cytosolic tRNA isotypes, with specific populations showing marked phase-dependent fluctuations: tRNA-Val-derived 5′-tRFs peak during the early secretory phase while tRNA-Lys-derived tRFs are elevated in mid and late secretory phases. These secretory-phase-enriched tRFs, particularly those derived from tRNA-Val, are proposed as key regulators of endometrial receptivity and embryo-maternal communication during the window of implantation. Beyond their roles in normal reproductive physiology, dysregulated tRF expression has been implicated in endometriosis pathogenesis, positioning these small RNAs as potential non-invasive biomarkers for this estrogen-dependent disorder. The selective packaging and export of specific tRF populations into extracellular vesicles suggests coordinated intercellular signaling mechanisms that integrate gene regulation, translation control, and intergenerational information transfer in reproductive tissues.
